output: Thank you very much, Mr President. I very much welcome the approach described in the White Paper of getting to the root of the causes of malnutrition and obesity and the diseases that result from it.

Unfortunately, in food policy in Europe, it doesn't matter whether we're talking about nutrient profiles, food labelling or in some places in the White Paper, we're falling into a very one-sided approach.
We are trying to solve the problems we have in Europe through product policy.
And I believe that it is a fundamentally flawed policy approach to assume that there are good and bad foods.
Many amendments show that. That doesn't exist.
There are only good and bad, or also balanced and unbalanced, ways of eating.
That should be our approach.
The causes of poor nutrition are multifaceted.
to fix this by trying to influence consumers through product labeling, or even by using advertising bans or regulations, or by applying different VAT rates, will not lead us to our goal.

I think everything we do here - I don't want to anticipate the labelling regulation debate - but everything we do here should be under the heading: What can we do to motivate consumers to think about their eating habits?

A traffic light labeling that suggests to him, "I am now standing in front of a good or bad product," is, in my opinion, more of an instrument of disenfranchisement than an instrument of education and enlightenment.
That is why I would like to see a more balanced approach here and the recognition that the way via product policy alone will not get us any further. Thank you very much.
Mr President, Madam Vice-President, ladies and gentlemen, budgetary control has no new issues.
We are satisfied with the old ones and would also be satisfied with progress on the old issues.
Firstly, we urgently request support for Romania and Bulgaria in building the rule of law, the judiciary and financial controls.
We do not want to be in the same place in two years' time as we are today, and that is why this point is so important to us.
The second point is the statement of assurance for the overall budget under the graduated audit procedure, including the national audit chambers.
It would be nice if we could achieve decisive progress in the last six months that remain to the Commission, or at least agree on a common path.
As budgetary controller, one has to be modest.
The third point would be simplification.
We need to help the lower levels of administration in the Member States more in implementing EU law.
It is clear that they are completely overwhelmed by large parts of EU law, and this is why the error rates are so high.
The fourth point is external aid. Here I would like to address Kosovo in particular and the question of cooperation with the United Nations.
The Committee on Budgetary Control has been to Kosovo and I would like to announce now that Kosovo will play a certain role in the budget discharge.
I believe that we have so far remained far below the possibilities of the European Union and that we urgently need to catch up here.
The fifth point would be to improve cooperation between Member States in combating fraud.
We have homework here, especially for the members of the Council.

Parliament will vote on the legal basis, the regulation for combating fraud, in November, and then we will have to consider together how we can move forward if we want to achieve better results in this area.
That was at least our common wish so far.
Mr President, India and Europe need more and better contacts with each other at all levels, especially at the level of so-called ordinary people.
business travellers, tourists, students and so on. A brief comment on this: it is also important for the Commission's legislative programme, which we discussed earlier.
We need good safety regulations in global air traffic, regulations that achieve something and are not just measures to cover one ’ s back.
It would therefore be a sensible and really necessary measure to finally lift these pointless liquidity requirements and come up with something better.
I voted in favour of the resolution because it is a clear call on the Belarusian Government to finally respect human rights.

It is disappointing that the hopes for a democratic development for the people of this last European dictatorship were not fulfilled by the questionable, indeed falsified, parliamentary elections in September.
The same applies to the strategic suppression of the opposition and civil society.
At the same time, we have today called on the Council and the Commission to ease and intensify contacts between peoples and to democratise the country by seriously considering the possibility of a
of visa fees for Belarusian citizens upon their entry into the Schengen area and thereby prevent an increasing isolation of Belarus and its citizens.
I would like to begin by refuting the spirit of a statement I heard a few minutes ago, according to which foreigners would flood our labour market.
Secondly, I would have liked a clearer, more unambiguous report from the Committee on Employment and Social Affairs.
Trust in the European Union ’ s social cohesion can only be achieved if fundamental social rights are defined as European primary law.

We should send a stronger signal to the Council, the Commission, the Member States and the Court of Justice of the European Union, and not be content with merely demanding a balance between fundamental rights and internal market freedoms and free movement.
That will not change anything. Social fundamental rights are human rights, just like freedom rights, and they must not be restricted by the free movement of the internal market.
We need to defend and improve the European social model, and it is high time that we introduced a social progress clause as a binding protocol to the existing EU treaties.
It is time to change the Posting of Workers Directive so that pay and minimum standards are not limited to minimum requirements.
Mr President, I would like to add my thanks to the rapporteur, Doris Pack, which have already been expressed by many colleagues and quite rightly so.
We have someone with us who, we hope, does not shed blood but who invests a great deal of heart and soul in this region.
I am pleased, along with her and the other Members who have said so, that there have been some advances, including in the area of police cooperation.
This is important so that everyday life on the ground can function in a reasonable way.

Nevertheless, I believe that we must continue to remind our counterparts in Bosnia and Herzegovina that they themselves still have to do a great deal, and probably more than they are currently doing in some areas.
We must constantly call for internal cooperation, the willingness to cooperate internally within the ethnic groups.
It is not a case of everyone trying to go their own way.
Involvement in regional cooperation in the Western Balkans is also important.
Madam President, the distance between the European Union and its citizens must not become as great as it has become.
We must ensure that it is reduced.
In this context, the Ombudsman has an important role to play, especially when and where he does it well.
Above all, we must count on the Ombudsman ’ s commitment and good sense.
We must not raise expectations too high, because no one will then be able to meet them.

But on the other hand, it is also about the fact that wherever malpractices have occurred in the administration of the European bodies, they should be taken up and, if possible, reasonable solutions should be sought which can then satisfy and reassure citizens.
The Ombudsman has shown in the past that he takes this task seriously and does it well.
We can hope that this will continue to be the case, and we thank you and the rapporteur for this report.

Ladies and gentlemen, ten years after the creation of the monetary union, Europe is in a serious crisis.
Banks are collapsing or being propped up with billions from the state budget, sales are collapsing and millions of people are afraid for their jobs and their future.
But it is not only the market that has failed; the prevailing politics also seem unable to learn from their mistakes.
We believe that grave mistakes were made in the construction of the economic and monetary union.

One such error is the structural divergence between monetary and fiscal policy. You cannot create a common currency without simultaneously harmonising tax and expenditure policy at least to some extent.
I think the economic imbalances in the euro area have become enormously worse.
What we need now is better coordination of economic policy and, above all, of tax policy.
We need effective measures against tax dumping, tax havens must be closed and capital movements must finally be controlled again.
The second serious mistake is the construction of the Stability and Growth Pact.

Anyone who believes that budget consolidation is essential in times such as these clearly lives in a different world. Nothing would be more disastrous than to respond to this economic crisis with public austerity programmes.
The Stability Pact has clearly failed.
It should be replaced by an integrated European strategy for solidarity and sustainable development.
In our opinion, we need an investment offensive to renew public infrastructure and to improve the situation of the socially disadvantaged in Europe.
A third mistake is the construction of the European Central Bank itself, which is not subject to any democratic control and is committed solely to the objective of price stability.

We advocate democratic control of the European Central Bank, and we also call for a revision of the ECB's monetary policy mandate, so that in future growth and employment must be promoted at least on an equal footing with price stability.
The current crisis also offers an opportunity for far-reaching reforms of the European monetary and financial architecture.
This opportunity should not be squandered.
Madam President, Commissioner, President-in-Office of the Council, ladies and gentlemen, the automotive industry is indeed in crisis, and there are many reasons for this.
The financial crisis in particular has exacerbated the crisis in this sector, and the Member States are currently working on solutions.
The European automotive industry is a key industry.
That is why we need European solutions for this key European industry in order to overcome the crisis.
Almost all car manufacturers have European structures.

And in order to make the necessary investments in environmentally friendly cars - this has been mentioned here several times today - in order to be able to make investments in environmentally friendly and low-consumption technologies, the car manufacturers need public guarantees or low-interest loans from the European Investment Bank.
But also the supplier industry, which is very important to me, must not be forgotten here.
It is precisely in the supplier industry that small and medium-sized enterprises create jobs and carry out innovation.
But what can the European Union do to protect this key industry?
At the last plenary session, the President-in-Office of the Council made a proposal to protect key industries.
A good proposal, in my opinion. If you follow this idea, this proposal through to its logical conclusion, it means a 'Volkswagen Act' for the entire European automotive industry.
This would certainly be a good instrument.
But this protection instrument is currently being opposed by the European Commission.
And so my question to the Commission is: will the European Commission stick to its claim despite the crisis in the automotive industry, or will it change tack in the light of the crisis?

Mr President, President of the European Court of Auditors, Vice-President of the European Commission, ladies and gentlemen, it has now become customary to point out that the European Court of Auditors has found that the European Commission has not always managed its budget properly.
we cannot accept the fact that, in the relationship between expenditure at European level and administration at national level, a statement of assurance cannot be issued for the umpteenth time.

I can remember conversations with the former President of the Court of Auditors, Professor Fridmann, who once told me that, given the structures, it is simply not possible to issue a statement of assurance.
So we should already start thinking about how this instrument can be given life in such a way that the objective of the declaration of reliability, if justified, can also be achieved.
And there we have to distinguish between a few things.
Firstly, a budget that includes around 95% subsidies is much more susceptible to fraud than a national, regional or municipal budget.

A subsidy budget that is essentially managed by the Member States, who have asked us for a great deal more independence in the new financial period, because the complaint about the last funding period was that there was too much central control.
This also means that responsibility for the money must be taken at regional and national level.
I will only briefly touch on a third subject area.
We also need to learn to distinguish between fraud and waste.
These are things that are always thrown together.
I am also annoyed when EU money is used to promote things that are not absolutely necessary.
But that was not fraud, that was waste.

Therefore, those who are responsible for the projects, particularly in the area of structural policy, the Member States, also have the responsibility to ensure that money is not wasted and that only those projects are supported that really deliver added value for the region.
That is why I think we should certainly consider switching parts of structural policy to interest-free loans.
If Member States have to repay, then they will only fund what they really need.
It is not just me who finds it difficult to share in the self-congratulation of European decision-makers when it comes to dealing with the financial market crisis.
Because if Europe, if this EU were a functioning democracy, one would first have to ask about responsibility and how all of this could happen.
It's simply not true that it would be a tsunami, something natural, with all the problems we have in front of us, but it is man-made.
And in view of all the regulatory zeal that this Union has shown, one wonders why no action was taken when banks began to cross national borders to a significant extent.
Why did one not act despite appropriate warnings when the derivatives crept in?
I remember very well that Nobel Prize winner Joseph Stiglitz raised all these points here.
There was silence, there was a taboo.
That's where we need to start, because recognizing our own mistakes is the only key to finding solutions in the future.
Mr President, Commissioner, my question is: what do you think about Iceland and Hungary introducing the euro as a second currency?
Do you think that this could solve the problem of these two countries?
The second question is: do you think it would be possible to introduce a risk premium for derivatives, which would then be used to strengthen the banks' equity capital?
My third question is: do you not think that a reduction in VAT would support imports, particularly from China and India?
Wouldn't it be more sensible to consider tax reductions for our businesses and for our employees, to pay investment premiums, to adjust the progressive depreciation accordingly,
and that we increase low-quality goods by a factor of 10, so that we do not tax fictitious profits, that the money stays in the companies and that wages can thus be paid better.
Madam President, the comprehensive report shows how many human rights violations take place in the world every day.
This is particularly shocking 60 years after the declaration of the Charter of Human Rights and calls on us to redouble our efforts.
For this reason, I would like to advocate the establishment of the current Subsidiary Committee on Human Rights as a main committee.
Today we have awarded the Sakharov Prize to Hu Jia, who campaigns for human rights and the environment in China.
But as he is in detention, he could not take over the role himself.
Shame on China, and tomorrow there will be a debate here on human rights violations in Russia.
In our relations with these two countries, we must give not only economic interests but above all human rights a higher priority.
We demand freedom for Hu Jia in China and freedom for Mikhail Khodorkovsky in Russia.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen of the Council, Commissioner, colleagues, as rapporteur for the European Anti-Fraud Office (OLAF), I have made some interesting observations, such as the fact that everyone talks about fighting fraud, but then nobody actually does anything.
Or the experience that the Member States calculate their payments to the European Union with a fine-tooth comb, but when it comes to spending this money they pull on their best trousers.
and even make it easier for fraudsters. For example, it still takes an average of 36 months before irregularities are even reported to the European Union.
The European Parliament does not agree.
We want effective fraud prevention, including prevention.
We want the Member States to make combating fraud a priority and for us to discuss together the needs and problems involved.
We want national judicial authorities to have a greater interest in the further pursuit of OLAF's findings.
There should no longer be any legal grey areas when it comes to crooked tours, as there have been in the past.
We need to bring European money into line with national money.
We want an independent body with the necessary resources and legal basis to carry out its tasks.
We want an office that can work quietly and efficiently with the Member States.
Our opinion on legal basis 1073 / 99 provides for all these points.
We are calling for this regulation to be further developed, together with the Council, as the cornerstone of the Anti-Fraud Office.
We call on the Council to incorporate our opinion and to begin talks on the further development of this regulation.
We want to bring Olaf's problems to a solution.
I thank all the groups in this House for supporting this in a resolution on which we will vote tomorrow.
I call on the French and Czech Presidencies of the Council to pick up the ball from Parliament and pass it back.
We have also taken up solutions in our proposals that were developed and submitted by the Council's working group.
On our part, I can tell you that we are interested in a swift conclusion to this regulation, and I can assure you of our willingness to engage in constructive talks on the basis of our proposals.
Consolidating all of OLAF ’ s legal bases, as the Council has envisaged, would mean further considerable time would be lost, with an uncertain outcome.
Ola will soon be celebrating his tenth birthday.
Consolidation means a further ten years without an improved legal basis.
We take the fight against fraud too seriously for that.
We want to achieve what is possible now, in cooperation with the Council.
Thank you very much.
Mr President, I would like to pick up on a comment made by my Austrian colleague, Paul Rübig, a moment ago.
We spend less than 1% of European gross domestic product on Europe, and at the same time we are demanding that the European Union take on more and more tasks for more and more Member States.
This calculation cannot work. It is therefore really not credible that we have been following a savings course for many years, but we are not only saving in a frugal way
use of our money, but basically to ensure that the finance ministers can collect money again at the end of the year and let it flow into their budgets at home.
We have enough new tasks for which we should spend our money.
We also have tasks that we have not really budgeted for properly for years.
I believe that the European Union ’ s information budget should be increased significantly if we really want to bind European citizens to Europe.
There are many tasks in the area of student and apprentice exchanges that Europe could take on.
privatisation in the justice sector, a traditionally state reserve area, is progressing.
This corresponds to the changed living conditions of people in our Union.
But – and this comment has already been made several times – integration must not intrude too far into traditionally established structures, and especially not into those that are working well.
We cannot lump everything together.
Public documents and public document officials are very different in many Member States.
In Austria, and not only in Austria, the notariat as a public certification body has a long tradition and a high level of security and trust.
This should not be jeopardised unnecessarily simply because such professional groups do not exist elsewhere.
Therefore, integration and mutual openness, yes, but with a sense of proportion.
Thank you very much, Madam President, esteemed colleagues. Plant protection products have been and continue to be essential tools in agriculture.
Farmers need a minimum choice of plant protection products, because it is a question of protecting the domestic cultural landscape, high-quality nutrition and the protection of resources.
We discuss chemicals too often on a very irrational level.
But in order to get reasonable results, we have to think and act rationally.
The effects of Parliament's original position would have been fatal: a broad ban on plant protection products, lower yields in agriculture and, ultimately, rising food prices.
Therefore, I am pleased that a barely acceptable compromise was found in the trialogue negotiations.
It is to be welcomed that we have retained the three-zone system, albeit with considerable exemptions for the Member States.

A courageous step towards a consistent internal market solution would look different. Alongside acceptable compromises, we should not pre-empt a definitive definition of endocrine-disrupting substances, but should actually give the European Commission the four years it needs to present a scientifically sound definition.
Otherwise, we would be stretching the precautionary principle at this point.
And lastly, I find it regrettable that we have not been persuaded to initiate an impact assessment of the agreement now reached. Thank you very much.
When there were demonstrations of opinion the last time a government head or head of state visited, the President of this House intervened heavily.
At that time, it was about an issue that was his own, namely the Lisbon Treaty.
When today the president, the sovereignly elected president of a country, speaks and is massively interrupted and disturbed from the socialist side, the president does not intervene.
Do you know what that is?
This is not democracy, this is arbitrariness, and I call for appropriate measures to be taken.
Madam President, children and politics – the interests of citizens and politicians rarely diverge so widely.
Citizens throughout Europe are very concerned about children.
As we can see once again, children's interests are low down the list of politicians' priorities.
I do not know of any country that has its own children's ministry, nor is there a dedicated representative for the interests of our youngest citizens in the Commission.
In Parliament, the subject of children is scattered across all the committees.
Therefore, I would like to highlight in particular the fact that the Commission is today dealing with childcare facilities and the Barcelona targets.
Thank you. In our parliamentary working group on the quality of childhood, we have established the following: our children live in a very complex world.
The future of a child who is here, now and today is completely uncertain.
A child today can climb to the top of the social ladder or fall very far down it.
It can remain within its own cultural sphere or seek a new one.
He can follow a similar life path to his parents or do the exact opposite.
It can be someone of the other or of the same sex.
All of this was completely different more than 50 years ago, when we set up a united Europe.
The diversity that characterises us Europeans has become a defining factor in children ’ s lives.
The concept of diversity must therefore be incorporated into the pedagogy of childcare workers and teachers.
This is currently taken into account far too little.
The childcare facilities and schools of Europe are breaking down in the face of the challenge of integration.
We need to press the reset button on integration issues and go back to the beginning.
Let us look at this issue again, completely without prejudice, in line with the motto: unity in diversity.

Furthermore, I would like to ask all the Members of the European Parliament who come after me: do not just advocate gender mainstreaming, but also fight for child mainstreaming, especially now, in the time of the global financial crisis, because otherwise we will be saddling our children with incalculable debts. Thank you.
Thank you, Mr. President. I want to make this very brief.
Thank you very much for these constructive contributions to the debate that I have heard here.

I would just like to send out two messages at the end. Firstly, I would like to thank the Commissioner very much for not speaking out against the concept of minimum requirements of the safety net in principle.
This proposal is not devilish nonsense, and it is not a bureaucratic monster either.
It is an instrument for solving problems, and we should give it a chance.
Therefore, I would like to simply advocate for this once again.
And the second thing I want to say, and this is now directed at my British friends, and that is: cross all the parties.
My dear British friends, I do understand Caroline. I do understand your problem.
I can understand perfectly well that a country might, for a certain period of time, put energy security before compliance with a limit value for air pollutants.
I have great understanding for that.
I would be the last person to deny Her Majesty additional time when it comes to building new coal-fired power stations.
We can talk about it.
The problem is that we have not done exactly that.
This conversation has never taken place during the last four months.
I would like to ask you, and this is now the outlook for the second reading, to be open-minded at this point and to say, let us talk about a compromise at this point.
to give up the, in my opinion completely irrational, competition policy and environmentally unjustifiable resistance, the fundamental opposition to minimum standards.
I think this is the point at which we can reach a compromise, and I hope that we will get it at second reading.
By the way, I do not think that we should not look for a compromise at first reading.
This legislation is too complex for that.

I do not want to end this parliamentary term as rapporteur and hand over legislation to the next Parliament in which a legislative text is being discussed that is inconsistent, illogical and contradictory.
Therefore, I ask you to support the compromises when we vote here in an hour. Thank you.
Ladies and gentlemen, I think the crucial question with all the economic stimulus packages being drawn up across Europe is, of course, who will ultimately get the money?
Are we still writing blank cheques to the banks, even though rapid nationalisation would ultimately be much cheaper for the taxpayer?
Are we to further relieve the corporations and recipients of high incomes, even though they have been feasting on tax gifts throughout Europe for years?

The more money is wasted on such purposes, the more predictable it is that such programmes will fail and the more likely it is that the European economy will sink further and further into a very dangerous downward spiral.

The years-long policy of privatisation, deregulation and liberalisation – precisely this policy – has concentrated incomes ever more strongly in the hands of the top 10 000, and it was this policy that is responsible for the current crisis in which we find ourselves today.
Anyone who thinks that he can overcome this crisis by continuing with precisely this policy, with minor modifications, has simply not understood anything.
The exact opposite is needed.
Instead of buying up the banks' bad paper, we should use tax funds to renovate schools and hospitals and to restructure the European economy in an environmentally sustainable way.

If private companies receive public funds, then the following must apply: no public money without employment guarantees and, above all, no public money without the acquisition of public property rights, so that the state and, above all, the general public can later also participate in future earnings.
The best economic recovery programme that we could have right now would be a radical redistribution of income and wealth from top to bottom.
The low-paid sector in Europe must be reduced, rather than being encouraged further.
We need higher minimum wages and better social benefits in Europe.

We need taxes that ensure that the millionaires and profiteers from the past financial market party now also bear the enormous losses that have occurred, and not the vast majority of citizens who have never benefited from the whole financial boom.
I think that social justice is currently also the only economically sensible thing.
It is the only way to break the devastating crisis dynamic.

Mr President, at the beginning of March, the political prisoners Mikhail Khodorkovsky and Platon Lebedev were transferred from Chita in Siberia to Moscow to be charged again on the basis of unfounded accusations.
The first public hearing was scheduled for 31 March.
On the same day, human rights consultations between the EU and Russia were due to take place.
These important talks have now been postponed by the Russian side until further notice.
Russia is thus making it very clear how little importance it attaches to human rights.
The judiciary is still not only not used for justice, but also to eliminate regime opponents, and the EU is being duped as a result.
Thank you very much, Commissioner, colleagues. I am particularly grateful to Commissioner Verheugen for referring again to the ban on animal testing.

has pointed out, because it reminds me that this is now the third amendment to the legislation on cosmetics that I have had the honour to accompany: the sixth amendment, the seventh amendment and now the amendment to a regulation.
And we have indeed managed to ban animal testing.

We have managed to ensure, for example, that consumers know how long a product is still fit to consume, because they see a small tin with a number inside that tells them how long it is still valid for.

I am saying this to some of my colleagues, especially Mrs. Roithová, who was unfortunately unable to be here at the beginning. Labelling is never a warning. At no time. If a product is not safe, then it should not be on the market. It should not be marketed.
Any product on the European market must be safe and harmless.
However, labelling gives consumers the opportunity to choose.
That is democracy and that is their freedom of choice.
I think we have a very good piece of legislation.
I have tried to make the procedure very transparent.
As Mrs Kostet said, I have tried to bring together different opinions.

It is important to me that we produce legislation that is good legislation for everyone: for the consumers of the European Union, for the industry that has to work with this legislation and ultimately for all those who benefit from it.
to Mr Stell, I will say again that DEO works differently for everyone, but the claim that it keeps you sweat-free is still being made.
It is therefore important that statements about the depth of the wrinkles around my eyes after a week in Strasbourg are reasonably realistic and reliable.
I would like to thank the Commissioner once again, also in relation to the explanations he has given to my fellow Members, and also my staff, who have done the lion's share of the work.
Thank you very much.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, Mr Van Nistelrooij's report on the Green Paper and the future shape of cohesion policy is certainly one of the most important reports of the Committee on Regional Development in recent years.
We agree with the basic concept of the Green Paper, which states that territorial cohesion is about the polycentric development of the EU.
overall, and to ensure the sustainable development of the sovereign areas with their various characteristics and peculiarities, while at the same time preserving their diversity.
It is right that in the next planning period, a more comprehensive system of phased assistance for transition regions that exceed the 75%
of GDP, must be created in order for these regions to obtain a clear status and thus have more security in their development.
The report by Mrs Krehl on obstacles to the absorption of structural funds rightly lists the most serious shortcomings that applicants face when
the use of the Structural Funds, such as excessive administrative costs, too many complex regulations and slow, cumbersome centralised administration in the Member States.
The report makes a variety of recommendations to the Commission on what effective measures should be taken to remove these obstacles.
I would like to single out and confirm two such measures.
Firstly, project managers currently have to keep their project documentation for a period of ten years in order to have it available for inspections, which may be carried out by the Commission.
This is excessive bureaucratic effort, particularly for smaller projects.
It is right that this should now be reduced to a period of three years. Secondly, the EU Commission ' s assessment criteria for innovative projects are causing considerable problems.
Innovative projects should not be judged by the same criteria as other projects.
Something different needs to happen here.

Madam President, if Europe is taking money in hand, and if, above all, the Member States are taking a lot of money in hand to help the car industry in its crisis-ridden situation, then it cannot just be a question of safeguarding the status quo and preventing bankruptcies here. It is a question of a number of other concerns.
The most important ones have been mentioned.
It is about securing long-term employment for people who are looking for jobs and need them.
To achieve this, we need to promote new technologies, innovation and, above all, sustainability in the transport system more than we have done hitherto.

We should therefore link all our measures to these objectives, so that in a few years' time we do not have to make the accusation that we should have thought about all this many years ago, and then we would not be in the next and new crisis.
Madam President, it would be nice if you could also give me the extra half a minute that my Green colleague, Ms Breyer, has just been given.
The Commission has tabled a good proposal to update the regulation on novel foods.
Unfortunately, some amendments attempt to supplement this proposal with requirements that are incompatible with the regulation ’ s objectives or that even seek to interfere with existing case law.
The obligation of food businesses to monitor novel foods with a view to the health and welfare of animals would simply be too great a burden for retailers.
Once again, there is an attempt to undermine the existing GMO legislation, namely the demand for special labelling of such novel foodstuffs derived from animals fed on genetically modified feed.
We have just heard it.
In principle, everything that concerns GMO legislation should not be covered by the novel foods regulation.
I say this even though I myself, for example, submitted the motion to specifically exclude new plant species from the definition of novel foods.
However, it cannot be the case that interested parties in this House abuse the novel foods regulation for electioneering purposes.
It is clearly such an attempt that we are seeing in Amendments 62 and 90, for which the Greens have requested a roll-call vote.
Who exactly are you trying to hang out to dry?
My group has always stood up for the consumer ’ s right to know what is in food.
So why should we not indicate the nano origin of ingredients on the label?
Although Amendment 62 somewhat clumsily attempts to anticipate my report on food labelling, I must say that I consider this labelling of novel foods to be entirely compatible with my report.

I therefore recommend that my group support the amendments mentioned, and I will include in my report on the food labelling regulation references to the part of the novel foods regulation that deals with labelling.
This is possible because we postponed the first reading on food labelling to the next legislature, against the wishes of the Greens.
But now it is clear to everyone how beneficial this postponement was.
A brief word on cloning: this is animal cruelty.
We are against it.
Nevertheless, foodstuffs must be included in this regulation, because otherwise in a
Madam President, it is very simple.
If you do not want this man to chair the solemn opening session of the European Parliament, you should vote in favour of my motion to amend the Rules of Procedure. Thank you.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, one thing is clear from the outset: the handing out of food to the needy, particularly in these economically difficult times, is not up for debate in the upcoming vote on our colleague Siekierski's report.
I would therefore like to make it clear from the outset that it was not easy to find a common line within our group.
I therefore respect the personal decision of each of my fellow Members to deviate from the rejection of the report that we have agreed upon and not to vote in favour of it.
Why can I not support the outcome of the committee vote in this form?
There are a number of reasons for this, which have absolutely nothing to do with helping the needy in the European Union in these economically difficult times.

On the contrary, the amendments submitted by my colleague, Mr Busk, on behalf of the Group of the Alliance of Liberals and Democrats for Europe, but unfortunately ruled inadmissible, were intended to place the existing system on a sustainable footing.
The current system is an anachronism from the days of agricultural overproduction, which, fortunately, belong to the past.
Thanks to the successful decoupling of direct payments, intervention stocks have steadily declined in recent years, so that today up to 85% of foodstuffs have to be purchased on the open market.
However, this has fundamentally changed the nature of the programme and has led to the loss of its agricultural policy dimension.
However, as this is a social programme, we must create the appropriate legal basis for it.

We are hereby informing you of the legal service of the Council's assessment that Article 37 of the EC Treaty on the common agricultural policy only applies to the common organisation of the market in sugar.
Article 308 as a legal basis. Otherwise, it would clearly be an infringement of the Member States' national competences.
The only solution, given that our amendments are inadmissible, can be a new draft from the European Commission.
This should also continue to abide by the principle of cofinancing, because only on the spot can the sense and nonsense of such programmes be assessed in a way that satisfies everyone.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, first of all, we welcome the European Commission proposal and this Green Paper in principle.
There is, and this is not a new issue, a mass phenomenon, which has been described by the various previous speakers, where relatively minor damage occurs to a large number of people.
The damage to the individuals concerned is small, but when added together it is large.
We need an instrument to deal with this problem.
I think it is right that we are thinking about such a thing.

I also very much welcome – to remain on the positive side – the fact that the Directorate-General for Consumer Protection has placed a very important emphasis in its Green Paper on the issue of alternative dispute resolution mechanisms.

This is a big difference compared to the White Paper on competition policy, which was also discussed here in the House yesterday, and which has so far completely ignored this possibility of out-of-court dispute settlement mechanisms.
I think that the Directorate-General for Consumer Protection is going further in its Green Paper than the competition colleagues.
However, I would like to make two things very clear, which I think should be understood as critical remarks.
Parliament will, in a few minutes, at 12 o'clock, adopt my report on the Commission's Green Paper on competition policy.
We will, by a very large majority in this House, demand that the European Commission adopt a horizontal approach when dealing with this issue.
We cannot end up with sectoral instruments: one for consumer protection, one for competition policy, another for the capital markets, maybe one for the environment.

Perhaps one more thing on the social side: they all contradict each other, they all interfere with the legal systems of the Member States and, in the end, they lead to a legal mess that no practitioner can deal with.
We have had such examples in the past on many occasions.
I am just thinking of the debate on the directive on professional qualifications, which we later brought together in one instrument, because this fragmentation was no longer possible.
The Commission should not make the same mistake here.
It should have promoted the horizontal approach from the outset.
This is the clear position of Parliament, and it will become clear here in a few months, in a few minutes.

My final point: I very much welcome the fact that we have reached a consensus that we do not want a litigation industry on the American model, with annual sales of USD 240 billion, where only lawyers profit in the end and consumers get nothing.
We want genuine rule of law in Europe and we want to remain true to our traditional system and our understanding of the rule of law.
Ladies and gentlemen, there are many ways of dealing with the lasting consequences of the unexpected financial and economic crisis.

This revision package, this legislative measure within the framework of the European economic recovery programme, is a good answer, albeit not quite sufficient, but it is at least a good answer to this temporary, albeit extremely critical, situation.
This includes promoting a greater simplification of procedures and greater flexibility in applying the existing
provisions in the Structural Funds regulations, a demand that, ladies and gentlemen, the European Parliament has repeatedly made in recent years.
It is also to be welcomed, and I would like to stress this in particular, that, through an amendment to Article 7 on the eligibility of expenditure, the EU Member States and regions are to be enabled
to invest structural policy funds in energy efficiency and renewable energy in the housing sector, and that these measures should not only be available to low-income households.

The amendment in question therefore rightly deletes the line 'households with low incomes' and instead introduces an upper limit of 4% of the total EFR allocation for each Member State for such expenditure.
That is just one of the many improvements proposed.
In short, if this overall package is implemented, increased expenditure and thus further liquidity for carrying out necessary

targets for the Structural Funds, the European Agricultural Fund for Rural Development (EAFRD), the European Social Fund (ESF) and the Cohesion Fund, and a simplification of the rules to allow for rapid implementation of the programmes.
a significant contribution, albeit not a sufficient one, to dealing with the current crisis situation.
Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, I generally welcome the European Ecolabel.
It is right that we should make a new attempt to further spread this Community environmental label.
I also think it is right that we should stick to voluntariness in future.
This sign will only prevail in the future if there are clear criteria and standards that are prerequisites for acquiring this sign.
I call on the Commission to draw up reasonable, clear and comprehensible criteria.
The incentive principle must be at the forefront here.
It would be regrettable if the introduction of the environmental label failed due to bureaucratic obstacles.
In the end, it is the consumer who decides whether it is a success or a failure, because it must be accepted by them.

Even though I personally have a great fondness for the Blue Angel, which we have in Germany, this should ultimately not be a matter of taste, but it is important that consumers are given at a glance information about the special characteristics of a product.
A benefit for consumers will only arise if the Member States give up their own national labels and there is a single label at European level in the end.
A jumble of symbols confuses the consumer and does not inform them.
A sign, a statement. We should work together on this. Thank you.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I think that in this parliamentary term, in terms of the discharge procedure for the European institutions, we have put a lot of things on the road together.
I regret, however, that it has taken so long, because in principle we are only now completing what was already on the cards with the Commission's resignation in 1999, i.e. ten years ago.

This shows that we still need to improve our way of working and our procedures, and we need to ensure that the necessary transparency is established more quickly when it comes to informing citizens about how their tax money is being used.
I should like to thank Mr Kazak in particular, with whom I had the honour of working for the EPP Group on Parliament ’ s discharge.

I am very sorry, and that is why I have just had a quick look around, that a colleague who expresses himself very intensively in the press on various things of the European Parliament does not take part in the committee discussions, nor in the committee votes, nor here in the plenary debate.
I want to make this very clear at this point so that it is preserved for posterity and remains preserved, because we are very well aware that there were a number of problems here.
and it was the Members who are here now who, over the last five years, have ensured that the necessary reforms were achieved in a number of areas in the interests of the Members.
And to all of you, I would like to say a very warm thank you. We have done what was necessary, and we have not ensured that everything is widely discussed in the media, but not actually implemented.
Thank you for your serious cooperation.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the ODI report - thank you to the rapporteur - really takes us forward because it contains innovative elements, such as naming and shaming, a milestone in budgetary control.
Targeted criticism instead of blanket accusations.
That the Socialist Group is now backing down and would rather sweep the truth under the carpet is a scandal and a disservice to our control tasks.
The credibility of Parliament is at stake, and I appeal to my fellow Members not to allow this.
A word about our biggest worry cases, Romania and Bulgaria.
The Commission ’ s failure in preparing for accession is most apparent here.
We lost a lot of money.
The Commission stood by for a long time and only froze funds in 2008, over EUR 1 billion for Bulgaria and around EUR 142 million for Romania.
Freezing assets is not a solution to the problems.
The cooperation and verification mechanism, for which the President of the Commission himself is responsible, is a paper tiger, and the progress reports do not deserve their name.
The Odi report makes proposals as to how this could be improved.
The European Commission has deceived Parliament on the accession readiness of these countries and the enlargement commissioner should draw consequences from this.
However, we are concerned with the management of future enlargements and that is why this issue remains on the agenda.
Our group will grant the Commission discharge, but I personally will refuse discharge to the Commission.
Unfortunately, the Commission's enthusiasm for reform, which we could see was beginning to rekindle last year, has already died down again.
stagnation in the fight against fraud, nothing on prevention, more ethics not desired, and so on.
The Commission and Mr Kallas in particular should be clear about one thing: if it wants to be part of the new Commission, it needs the support of Parliament.
Mr President, yes, we have a problem with copyright and we have a problem with the remuneration of artists.
That is correct, and we must take action and do something about it.
But if we are to become active, it is a question of really responding to the challenges of the digital age, not of putting the saddle on dead horses.
I will explain to you why I am saying this.
The proposal that is before us actually fulfils only one promise, and that is the promise to promote the music industry.
If we look at what happens to the artists themselves and their income, and we do the sums, we come to
Such low average amounts are certainly not enough to provide a living for an artist.
Moreover, this money does not reach the artists directly.
The fund is too small to achieve anything.
One artist with whom I discussed this said that here there is a redistribution from the living to the dead.
I had to disagree with him. This is a redistribution from the artists to the producers, to the music industry, and only to the big ones.
All in all, Commissioner, this proposal is not suitable.
We need to find better ones.
We need to create something like a flat rate, for example.
The proposals you are making in relation to the 'use it or lose it' clause remain theoretical rights.
Please let us do something clever and well founded, as the artists in our hearing in the Committee on Legal Affairs recommended and requested.
Mr President, I regret, along with the rapporteur, Mr Zabbarini, that the Commission has not continued with its original plan for an integrated action plan.
There are many good reasons why all those involved, from local authorities to the European Union, are striving to improve urban transport.
We know that most people in Europe live in towns and cities and that the current traffic conditions are far from optimal.
Therefore, I am generally in favour of the plan and of the report by Mr Savary on preparing integrated proposals on the basis of Parliament ' s initiative.
I thank him for his commitment and also for the specific proposals.
At the same time, however, I would like to make it quite clear that what many fear or believe they have to fear will not happen.
Nobody wants to take away from the municipalities, nobody wants to take away from the local and regional authorities their right to shape their transport conditions.
We in Europe simply want to contribute to the fact that common rules are followed here, if a municipality, if a city, if a regional unit considers that to be reasonable.
The principle of subsidiarity is not jeopardised.
What we want to do here serves to protect this principle.
It remains that we should, in the interest of the citizens, make sure that if a citizen travels 10 or 20 km within the internal market,
Europe, he will come across a traffic-calmed zone, which he thinks he knows from his home town, only to find completely different rules in place.
Nobody wants to impose city tolls or any other regulations on any municipalities, but if they are actually introduced, they should be carried out within a clearly identifiable framework for citizens.
For more than 100 years, we have agreed on the meaningfulness of common approaches to traffic signs.
This should also be the case in future with this issue.
Mr President, we have been dealing with urban transport in the EU for a long time.
Why is that?
We are not even responsible. Responsibility was originally defined because about 80% of the population lives in urban areas, and because of the climate debate, we now want to take on the issue.
Fortunately, thanks to our protest, we were able to reduce the European Commission ’ s ambitions to a single action plan for urban transport.
I thank Commissioner Tajani for his insight.
The word 'subsidy' actually means 'support' rather than 'prescribe', but as an Italian, he would of course know that better than I, as a German with only a little Latin.
First and foremost, I am concerned that our report, Parliament ’ s report, should highlight strict compliance with the subsidiarity and proportionality principles.
Legislative measures at European level are not permissible for urban traffic.
Support measures - that is our job.
It makes sense to collect ideas and promote the exchange of best practices.
Not everyone has to reinvent the wheel.
Our cities need tailor-made solutions, but only the local stakeholders can work these out, because only they know what is necessary.
Municipalities have to deal with very different situations and therefore need sufficient scope for action, in order to keep city centre trade viable.
This is important for the attractiveness of a city.
It is therefore important not to exclude motorised individual transport and to pay more attention to urban logistics.
I would therefore like to see research into urban last-mile distribution being promoted.
That relieves a city.
It is also important that demographic change is taken into account.
After all, our society is getting older and older.
Mobility needs and housing requirements are changing.
We need to provide people with everyday goods close to their homes if we want to reduce traffic, and that is a challenge for the retail sector.
Anything else would just be asking for the green field.
Finally, we do not need an observation post for urban transport.
Such a body would cost a lot of money and produce a lot of paper that would simply be filed away in Brussels.
Madam President, I fully support the Commission objective of creating a legal framework for the coordinated introduction and use of intelligent transport systems in road transport.

My years-long personal fight to introduce eCall, the electronic emergency call system, on a mass scale has shown me the urgency almost on a daily basis. However, intelligent transport systems can do much more.
They serve environmental compatibility, increased efficiency, improved safety and non-discriminatory competitiveness in road freight and passenger transport.

Furthermore, you support intermodality, among other things by developing interfaces with other modes of transport such as rail, inland waterways and air transport, where the introduction of intelligent transport systems has long been under way.
All this cannot be achieved by the Member States alone, but only as a Community task.

Intelligent transport systems based on EU-wide binding basic standards and minimum quality regulations will improve the environment for innovation and provide planning security, particularly for small and medium-sized enterprises.
I particularly welcome the planned open, vehicle-integrated platform for intelligent transport system services.

The action plan finally includes a timetable for the introduction of individual systems: (a) for road safety, such as ESP and eCall; (b) for continuity in traffic management, for example, information on
tolls or parking management to avoid search traffic. C) The use of road, traffic and travel data in real time will enrich not only professional drivers but all road users.
The directive aims to set out detailed requirements for the coordinated, EU-wide, mandatory introduction of intelligent transport systems and their application in the Member States.
It also ensures the handling of personal data.

But be careful: the introduction of intelligent transport systems causes high costs, as infrastructure and vehicles first have to be equipped with the necessary information and communication technologies.
We still know too little about the willingness and ability of potential users to pay.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, we clearly support help for people with rare diseases.
However, amendment 15 is ethically totally unacceptable.
There must never again be a debate in Europe about valuable and worthless lives.
There must be no political or social pressure on parents in Europe to make a conscious decision against having a disabled child.
Selecting embryos would be a major ethical disaster.
This amendment must therefore be rejected, and it will not be enough to simply delete the word 'eradicate', which unfortunately corresponds to the fascist-like language we are using.
but it would still be a case of selection of embryos.
It would be intolerable if this amendment, which talks about the selection of end-embryos, were to become a stepping stone for a new ethic in Europe.
We must take a very firm stand against genetic discrimination.
Therefore, Amendment 15 must be rejected in full, otherwise, our group will unfortunately also be forced to vote against this report, which is otherwise quite positive.
Mr President, in the vote on discharge, for the first time since I have been a Member of this House, I voted against discharge for the Commission, and I would like
My reasons for speaking are essentially about the way in which the European Commission has handled and managed the accession of the two new countries, Romania and Bulgaria.
We are dealing with a whole host of problems in Romania and Bulgaria, with a high level of corruption, with a lot of money, a lot of European money, that has disappeared.
The European Commission only started freezing these funds in 2008.
We lost a lot of money in 2007 and now we are dealing with hardly any control systems or control systems that only function in a patchy way.
In Romania, we are dealing with a high level of corruption and problems in the judiciary.
All of this is due to the pre-accession process.

I want to send a signal to the European Commission, telling it that it must adopt a different approach to future enlargements. I also want to tell it that, if one looks at what it has done in other countries, it could achieve greater success if it wanted to.
I would like to call on the Commission to help the two countries to set up financial control systems worthy of the name and to help them overcome the systemic weaknesses in these two countries.
Otherwise, we will have a chronic problem and a chronic worry for the whole of Europe.

Madam President, I would like to draw your attention to the fact that behind me, in the area of the right-wing radicals, there are people who are not Members, and as far as I can see, they are very well using the voting card.
Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I expressly welcome Mr Schnellhardt's report and thank him for his good work.
Following the numerous crises in recent years involving products of animal origin that pose a threat to the health of humans and animals, comprehensive legal regulation is essential.
The current rules need to be revised.
We still need to ensure a high level of security.
Although I agree with the report in principle, there are some points that make me think.
The regulation has been made less strict in many areas and thus the trade in animal by-products has been loosened somewhat.
Some examples: there is a permit for the use of certain category 1 material in pet food.
Materials in category 2 or 3 can be disposed of more easily under official supervision with low waste quantities per week, regardless of their risk.
The risk of animal by-products of any category is determined only in part by the quantity.
The European Commission will issue the implementing regulation, and the draft contains, like the previous one, many powers for the Commission.

This means that the Commission can adopt comprehensive and fundamental rules on the handling of animal by-products under the comitology procedure. This means that Parliament, as is unfortunately often the case, is left out.
Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, Afghanistan has a difficult history.
I believe that families there are very close-knit and that women play an important role in the family.
That is why economic development is so important, especially the support of small and medium-sized enterprises, which should be promoted in particular.
Modern infrastructure is also needed to help the country develop.

I think that infrastructure projects in particular could help to create greater mutual understanding, and of course, through the use of information and communication technology, a different world view could gradually take root there, while fully respecting identity.
Marine pollution is only of interest when there has been a spectacular incident, and then there is a lot of discussion about the threat to our seas from this ongoing pollution.
Unfortunately, in the meantime, this keeps slipping further and further into the background.

The constant and repeated infringements, which contribute just as much and even more dramatically to the fact that not only our seas but also our coasts are under threat, were serious enough for us to take seriously.
I would like to thank my colleagues with whom we were able to discuss this, for the fact that we have all consistently supported the line that our sea needs to be protected.
I am speaking as a citizen of an inland country, but as a European, I feel responsible for protecting our seas from persistent pollution caused by cheap economic activity.
interests, and to integrate the principles of the second and third maritime packages, which is urgently necessary, as the Committee on Legal Affairs has also proposed.
Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, I too would like to congratulate the rapporteur, Mr Girma, on his very good report.
Worldwide, the situation regarding respect for human rights is very bad in many countries.
But also here in Europe, if we think, for example, of Belarus.
Therefore, the European Union must clearly step up its efforts by implementing human rights policy in all areas and setting clear benchmarks.
In our understanding, human rights are indivisible.
This could, for example, happen during negotiations for a new agreement with Russia through a binding human rights clause that would have an impact on all areas of negotiation.
I would also like to reiterate my support for the subcommittee on human rights becoming a main committee.
The EPP-ED Group calls on the Government and the Chinese Communist Party to conduct the forthcoming human rights dialogue in a constructive manner.
This would greatly ease relations between the EU and China.
We have a genuine interest in improving bilateral relations.
But this dialogue must also be conducted honestly.
Therefore, we will have to maintain our legitimate demands on China.
From our point of view, the human rights activist must be released immediately.
We should resume talks with the Dalai Lama, the religious leader of Tibet.
The Chinese region of Tibet should be made immediately accessible to journalists and United Nations human rights experts.
In my opinion, the People ' s Republic of China has shown, before the Olympic Games, that it can grant more press freedom, for example, without endangering the stability of the country.
Now it should have the courage to embark on new reforms: reforms on re-education camps, the rights of the accused, the death penalty, religious freedom and freedom of assembly.
She should really address these human rights issues and enter into dialogue with us.
Madam Vice-President, we welcome the Czech Senate's decision today with great pleasure, and I would like to offer my warmest congratulations.
President Klaus responded, among other things, by claiming that the Lisbon Treaty was nevertheless dead, because it had been rejected in the referendum in Ireland.
He is thus riding roughshod over the majority decisions of his own Parliament and Senate, and confirming his disgraceful and sectarian attitude in other areas of politics as well.
Fortunately, after the positive votes, he is only making a fool of himself.
We Greens have a positive and constructive attitude towards the European integration process, and of course we also have critical comments where necessary.
Once again, many thanks to the Czech Senate, Parliament and Government.
Mr President, last week, in a Foreign Affairs Committee meeting, which I was pleased to attend as a new Member, I heard Mr Machmellow.
an Iranian film director who told us very clearly that before this election there was 20% democracy in Iran, but after this election, which was a coup d ’ état for him, there is no democracy at all.
I also agree with this description: it was indeed an electoral coup, in which massive electoral fraud deprived the majority of the Iranian population, who were calling for change, of their rights.
Mr President, what interests me above all is this: there will be an inauguration in mid-August for the re-elected Mr Ahmadinejad.
What is currently being discussed in the Council and with the Member States?
Will there be an official, formal representation of the European Union and of the Member States?
I hope that this will not happen, because I think that following this electoral coup, there should be no official representation of the European Union or of the United States.
Member States, so I am in favour of further dialogue. But at this inauguration, I think no one should officially represent the European Union.
What is your position on this?
How is this being dealt with at the moment?
Thank you very much, Mr. Giegold. The crisis in Europe did not only come about through unethical behaviour on the financial markets, Mr Barroso.
There is the growing social inequality, there are the dangerous and unsolidary macroeconomic imbalances between the Member States, and there is Europe's dependence on rising resource prices.

The citizens of Europe deserve a Commission that keeps these deeper causes on the agenda, instead of tabooising them, instead of doing the opposite, instead of shifting the responsibility onto the Member States.
We need genuine European coordination of economic and fiscal policy.
We need tax cooperation in Europe that puts an end to tax dumping.
Only then will it be possible to halt social division and finance the necessary ecological investments.
We need a policy that so consistently promotes green technologies and lifestyles that the EU ’ s dependence on finite resources drops sharply.
This is an ecological, green revolution. I do not find any of this in your programme, Mr Barroso.
That is the reason why I cannot vote for you.
Thank you very much, Mr. Martin, one minute. We need a revolution of democracy. Mr. Barroso, imagine that you
If you were to run for election in Europe, just you, all by yourself, no other candidate, how many percent of the votes would you get?
What would the citizens do?
They would probably have listened to you today and seen that you have promised everything to everyone.
They would also, hopefully, get information on what they did five years ago.
You promised the most to the greatest number of people.
You have to compare what they promised before, what they are promising now and what you can expect.
I think that is very, very little.
You stand for the old, for the failed Europe, for contributing to the financial crisis because the Commission failed, for the lack of commitment on climate issues, and so on.
I would side with the majority of European citizens, because I believe you would not get more than 10%, or 12%, or 15% of the votes.
Madam President, I would like to speak in favour of keeping the new proposal, because it is clear that the negotiations in Honduras have failed.
There is no joint proposal for this election.
This election is illegitimate because the current president came to power through a coup.
That means I ask the plenary to give its assent to the proposal of the Bureau for this amendment, that the elections be removed. Thank you.
Democracy needs a revolution, and it is never wrong, Mr. Dartmouth, to advocate for transparency, Mr. Verhofstadt.
And you in particular, now in the Swedish Presidency, could be a shining example and really tell us how it went.
is about the very unworthy game of musical chairs for top positions in the European project, and also, and here Mr Barroso could lead the way, in the appointment of the Commissioners.
Germany and Austria are setting a negative example here, but unfortunately others are too.
Qualified people are sitting here in the room, but they have no chance of access.

Now that the Lisbon Treaty has been adopted, please be courageous and honest and say that we need much clearer and more transparent decision-making structures for the Commission and the top positions.
And it should be possible to find the good people with so much expertise here from the European Parliament and not to recruit them in any federal states.
Mr President, the subject we are discussing here this evening has already been on the agenda of the European Parliament on several occasions.
As we know, the Commission is guided by the conditions of the International Monetary Fund when granting aid.
This has already been mentioned a few times.
In this context, the EP has repeatedly made inquiries to the Commission.

In November 2008, the European Parliament called on the Commission to submit an analysis of the impact of the conduct of banks that transferred their assets from the Member States that had joined just before.
On 24 April this year, Parliament repeated this demand.

Furthermore, Parliament has requested to be informed about the statements of intent concluded in detail between the Commission and the Member States receiving aid, and which set out in detail the conditions for the aid.
As far as I know, these questions have not been answered to date.
My first question is therefore: is it true that these questions have not yet been answered?
If that is the case, then my second question is: why have these questions not been answered so far?
My third question is: when can we expect an answer to these questions?
Ladies and gentlemen, the GUE / NGL will not support this resolution.
The Stockholm Programme sails completely past the real challenges of today.
The fundamental flaw is that a Europe of rights is being established which is to be granted to EU citizens but not to other people living in Europe.
These people should not be denied the same civil and human rights.
On the one hand, the EU wants to join the Charter of Fundamental Rights, which we welcome, but on the other hand, migrants are being sorted according to the principle of legal or illegal.
Frontex is being upgraded to combat so-called illegal immigration, and I say to you that this completely distorts the view of an unbiased immigration policy.
Stockholm has failed here.
Another problem is the completely unbalanced relationship between security and freedom.
Yes, there is no freedom without security, but there is also no security without freedom. Freedom is not a bargaining chip, but a universal legal asset of every human being.

Stockholm, on the other hand, symbolises the EU's obsession with security, in which huge supranational databases are created without adequate control mechanisms, bringing together intelligence and police data from across Europe.
This means that people's right to self-determination in information terms is being destroyed and the vision of a transparent human being is becoming a reality.
Finally, allow me to say, as a Member from eastern Germany, that, 20 years after the fall of the Berlin Wall, I find it unbearable to see Europe becoming more and more of a fortress.
Mr President, Commissioner, you are right: the railway system is a very safe system compared to other modes of transport.

However, alongside many positive aspects, liberalisation in the form of fragmentation of individual companies, separation of infrastructure and operations, outsourcing of maintenance work and leasing of equipment and staff has brought about a great deal of uncertainty.
In my opinion, it must be guaranteed that the national safety authorities issue safety certificates and authorisations in accordance with the requirements of the ERA, the European Railway Agency.
Are there controls, enough controls?
For example, is it guaranteed that staff training, certifications and working conditions are properly monitored?
How does this relate to the introduction of a uniform Community certificate?
What about the European train driver's licence?
Efforts regarding ERTMS must be increased further.
The implementation plan recently drawn up and alluded to by you must be binding and must not be delayed by financial or national considerations.
Efforts to introduce the technical specifications for interoperability (TSI) must be continued and extended.
This would be a huge step forward, particularly in terms of the approval of the equipment, and would provide a higher level of safety in terms of maintenance.
Many of the recent railway accidents and near-misses are due to inadequate maintenance.
For cost reasons, the intensity is particularly neglected and the intervals of maintenance work are lengthened.
What is the situation here with a Europe-wide certification system for maintenance workshops?
In my opinion, everything must be done to avoid the situation where each individual Member State returns to its old rules and acts differently and unilaterally.
The border crossings between the individual Member States are particularly risky from a security point of view.
How does the Commission intend to deal with this problem in the short term?
In the balance sheet that you draw up, I would like you to take account of the social aspects and not to forget them.
We need a revolution in democracy. Mr President, Mr Commissioner, good morning in this new world of the Treaty of Lisbon. This is an opportunity for you.
I have been following your reports in this House for eleven years now, previously as a journalist, and ultimately they are very similar.
Now, however, you have the opportunity to look forward.

Use the expertise of this House. It is no coincidence that colleagues Sandergaard, Chrysogonos, Makris, Mr Staes, and my colleague Mr Ehrenhauser from our list are gathered here in the Chamber right now. Restart the work of the Court of Auditors.
Look at what works elsewhere, in Germany, where one can check the cost-effectiveness and sense of the expenditure, in Austria, and develop
a concept, perhaps as part of an own-initiative report that will come before Parliament, on how you can do what you do much better so that you can actually carry out your tasks.
We need a revolution of democracy.
I remember very well that the debates we are hearing today began ten years ago in this House.
I would urge you, Commissioner, to take to heart what comes from ALDE, but also from the Greens and some of the left-wing Members of Parliament, and to write it down in your notebook.

Don't you realise that with the approach that the European Union has taken into and is taking in the negotiations, we cannot, as honest brokers, resolve the imbalance? I think, in connection with what we are currently experiencing in Copenhagen at this time, that we really need a new approach.
The developing countries are no longer developing countries in many of the things they do today.
Emerging countries, developed countries, and very self-confident.
If we fail to make a fresh start with fair rules from the European side, then we will end up where we do not want to be, namely in protectionism and in the consequences of 1933 in Europe as well.
Thank you. I would like to use my blue card to make a clarification.
The support in Austria is not granted to just one person.

Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, a microfinancing instrument for the unemployed, those threatened by unemployment and, above all, for people who do not have access to normal credit markets is a good intention and is supported in principle by our group.
However, the performance of the Commission and the Council in this procedure so far is simply inadequate, partly wrong and cannot be supported by our group.
The first point of criticism concerns the question of financial resources.
The financial resources on the table are not financial resources for a programme to combat unemployment. This cannot be called a micro-financing programme. At best, it is nano-financing.
Secondly, we are fundamentally opposed to such sleight-of-hand tricks that have no effect, that come at the expense of other programmes and that simply disappear without trace.
Thirdly, I am also of the opinion that the sustainability and success of such a programme must include organic mentoring and coaching.
Many new start-ups, particularly in the microfinancing sector, fail, and therefore the programme must also ensure that this is prevented.
Fourthly, social security benefits in the Member States must not be withdrawn as a result of participation in this programme, otherwise it will be ineffective.
Unemployment cannot be fought in a sustainable way in this manner.
We will reject it in this form.
Madam President, today, after eight years, we all basically know it: the previous Afghanistan strategy, which was based on military action, has failed.
The imbalance between military operations on the one hand and the insufficient resources for civilian purposes on the other is actually preventing an improvement in living conditions in Afghanistan.
Our main message as the GUE / NGL group is therefore that a radical change of strategy is needed.
We need a radical change of strategy, away from the military and, above all, towards the people.
This means that 40% of Afghans are unemployed. More than half of them live in extreme poverty and have neither a health service nor adequate education.
The social issue must be given top priority, and that is what we expect from the Council, from the Commission and from all those involved.
This includes strengthening responsible government action, strengthening agriculture and integrating former Taliban fighters.
But I will tell you quite frankly: anyone who only goes halfway down this road and again puts their faith in military adventures is throwing away every chance.
It is five minutes to twelve.
Madam President, my question is: is it not sensible to prevent unemployment altogether?
Do you see any public tasks that could be entrusted to the unemployed?
And above all, how could we strengthen small and medium-sized enterprises so that new ones are established?
are there any possibilities to apply cross-border methods and best practice to create more and new enterprises?
We need a revolution of democracy.
As an admirer of so many Swedish traditions, I was all the more disappointed by the assessment of your work.
It was, unfortunately, not a people ’ s presidency, but a presidency of council princes and also a presidency of major investors of all streams. It was not a stream.
I know you, Madam Member of the European Parliament, from that time, when you were one.
Now you are saying something else.
I very much hope that when you return, you will pick up where you left off, namely, be inspired by parliamentarism.
Why did you adopt the directive so late?
Why are we now dealing with something akin to a board at EU level, and why is this being done under the auspices of Sweden and its transparency?
Please use your future to return to where you once were.
