Mr President, this debate is a central one on the character of the European Union.
The question is: market or social policy?
The Commission ’ s previous deliberations on the future of cohesion policy are unacceptable.
The Structural Funds must be strengthened, not reduced.
Europe must not do less, but more to overcome economic and social disparities.

I will say quite frankly that renationalisation, even of parts of the current cohesion policy, would have serious consequences for disadvantaged regions, including in Germany, for example in eastern Germany.
The most recent round of enlargement of the European Union has actually increased the differences between the regions in the Union.
Incidentally, I also think it is important that the particularly unfavourable socio-economic conditions in regions whose per capita GDP is just above the 75% threshold should also be recognised.
An abrupt end to funding after 2013 would be devastating for these regions.
Ladies and gentlemen, strengthening the European Union ’ s specific, city-related funding is also extremely important.
important, as there is great potential for the economy and for finance, and as the crisis has particularly affected this area.
One last word on the border regions.
As a Member from eastern Germany, I can tell you that we need to invest a great deal and do a great deal there.
I am greatly concerned that the Commission will fail in this respect.

Madam President, regional structural funding is an essential instrument in European integration, and in the past, the Objective 2 funding has been perceived by people in many regions as a driving force for structural and economic development.
It is an important element in dealing with structural change in the regions and also in the municipalities.
Europe is really tangible here for the citizens, because it is also having an effect on the ground.
However, we need simpler, clearer and more transparent structures for the Cohesion Funds and better controls to ensure that the funds are used sustainably and efficiently.
Cohesion, convergence in social and economic development, is a major objective in the EU, and therefore we must not weaken this instrument in the future, but rather strengthen it.
Cohesion policy in Europe needs more clarity, more transparency, efficiency and also the self-responsibility of the individual Member States.
It is therefore important to stick with the system of additive support.
We do not want the structural funds to be renationalised.
Therefore, I would like to ask the Commission to ensure that the funding criteria for 2013 meet these requirements.
Mr President, I have two points of order to make. Mr President, I would like to ask you to allow me to make them in the order in which they arose.
First of all, I have a request for the whole House.
It relates to an urgency that we are debating this afternoon.
This is the case of Ms Amitu Hai Dar.
We have an urgency on the agenda this afternoon.
I ask my fellow Members to trust me in the following course of action. Some Members who have been closely involved in this case know, and I can confirm,
I cannot go into more detail, but I am one of those who know that we are about to have a solution to this case today.
Prominent representatives of the EU government are working on a positive solution.
It would therefore be sensible to delete this urgent matter from the agenda this afternoon, because any further debate would only hinder the work that some diplomatic channels are carrying out there.
Therefore, I would ask the House to agree that we should, exceptionally, remove this urgency from the agenda, because we will do more to resolve the case in this way than if we were to discuss it.
That would be my first request, Mr President.
I will put the second request immediately afterwards.

Madam President, it seems symptomatic to me, for us, that is to say for the EU in general, but also for Parliament in particular, that we reflexively hold discussions on situations in countries whose situation is not new.
Unfortunately, very often overnight events lead to us suddenly pouring out waves of political demands, which I sometimes find somewhat questionable.
I do not think it would be good for us to use a failed plane bombing as an excuse to launch a general debate on Yemen.
I think we should be clear that it would be in our interest to consider a strategy for dealing with this situation.

I also believe that the situation in Yemen requires careful analysis, namely that it is a failed state in which a government operates that no longer has control over large parts of the country, and we should analyze for ourselves what risks for Europe arise from this.
The risks seem to be that terrorists are being trained there, and the risks seem to be emerging on the coast of Yemen, where piracy is clearly taking place.
That is why I think it is up to us, and perhaps also to Baroness Ashton, to consider what we can do to counter these risks.
In my opinion, the answer can primarily be: How can we support the government in Yemen in regaining control of the country and containing these risks?

All other discussions about the long-term development of the country must of course also be held, but I do not believe that it is really sensible and that we will be taken seriously if we now launch a list of demands across all policy areas, from media freedom to women's rights, here in parliament.
I don't think that will achieve anything in Yemen. Instead, we should focus on what help we can provide in the current situation to solve an acute problem. Thank you.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the massacre on 23 November last year in the province of Maguindanao in the Philippines was a black day for human rights and a barbaric terrorist act.
57 dead, raped women, injured people – you are speaking a bloody language.
As a journalist, I am particularly struck by the fact that among the dead were 30 journalists.
According to the renowned International Crisis Group, in no documented case have so many journalists ever been murdered at one stroke.
For this reason, it is appropriate for Parliament to find clear words on this, as we are doing today in the draft resolution.

However, the EPP Group would like to amend the text at three points by means of separate votes. We should support the Philippine Government in its fight against terrorism and violence, and therefore refrain from making accusations against the Government.
Therefore, on behalf of the EPP, I am requesting a separate vote on recital F. This one criticises the Philippines for showing all signs of a non-functioning legal system.
"Quote: In point two, we would like to delete the assumption of so-called initial delays in the investigation."
Finally, point 6 claims that there are missing persons in Philippine prisons.
This is also an unproven assumption at present and, in our opinion, should be deleted.
Ladies and gentlemen, I think it is good that the Social Democratic Group has dealt intensively with this report.

I would like to point out, however, that in December 2008, Parliament adopted a resolution that was almost unanimous, in which we called on the European Commission to table precisely such a proposal as the one we are discussing here today.
Parliament must at some point also stick to what it has decided, and what has been the declared will of this House for years.
We have resolved the outstanding issues through a compromise in the Legal Affairs Committee, because, of course, there are always concerns here and there with every legislative proposal.
This compromise is, I may say quite clearly, ready for a decision.
I do, however, understand that it may be a good idea to extend the circle of those who support this report.
If Mr Schulz's efforts are aimed at expanding the circle of supporters, I have nothing against postponing it to the next plenary in March.
That is my personal position on this.
That is not the position of the group. We decided differently this morning.
But I think we should give the Socialists a chance to come to their senses here, so that they can do something for the SME sector, for relieving the SME sector, and for cutting red tape.
I understand that.
Mr Almunia was responsible.
I did ask you about your interventions, but you can now say what you have done yourself.
Mr Barroso, did I understand you correctly when you said that the Member States are to blame for the Greek crisis because they refused to follow your proposals?
Could you please tell us which Heads of State or Government were involved?
Welcome to this house.
The loss of confidence in Copenhagen was, in my opinion, one of the major problems.
But that could theoretically go on, worldwide, but also within the European Union.
My question to you is: what is going to happen with the instruments that we adopted last year?

ETS is something that really bothers me, because I am not quite sure how the Commission is now going to deal with benchmarks and carbon leakage, for example, on the basis of the decision taken in Copenhagen.
The second question is: there is a proposal from the French Government that importers from the
Third World, where we want to go, into the ITS system, in order to make the market bigger and to allow these countries to participate in this system.
Do you have an opinion on that?
Madam President, this is about the European Union having a clear strategy and preventing a gold or oil rush.
Europe must actively intervene here.
Europe can prove its strength as a partner here by speaking with one voice.
It is about shipping above and below the water, it is about fishing, it is about resources in the form of primary energy sources and raw materials.
Here, together with the other partners in the Arctic, we need to have workable, reliable agreements.
We need cooperation instead of confrontation.
We need dialogue in the area of environmental protection in order to preserve natural resources and nature.
We need dialogue on animal welfare in order to preserve biodiversity. We need protection and respect for the inhabitants and their human rights.

If we carry out positive resource management together with our partners in the Arctic, we will have the chance to go down new political paths here and to ensure that the Arctic remains a region of peace.
This will be a first litmus test for our common foreign policy, where we can demonstrate that we Europeans are pursuing common goals.
Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, the G20 summit in Pittsburgh has provided a good basis.
He has called for economic recovery plans to promote decent work, to contribute to safeguarding and creating jobs, and to give priority to employment growth.

The ministers of labour of Europe are therefore called upon to urge the G20 members: first, not to scale back their economic recovery packages, but to use them more extensively and more strongly to create jobs; secondly, to ensure that short-time working is financially secured

to prevent job losses; thirdly, to use public investment to create jobs and sustainable, ecological growth; and fourthly, to strengthen and expand social security measures and to ensure access to them.
This also ensures purchasing power in the internal market and employment and combats poverty.

The cooperation with the social partners is particularly close to the hearts of the European employment ministers, in order to strengthen the social dialogue even more forcefully, as the Commissioner would like to do.
Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, first of all, I would like to thank Mr Leichtfried for an excellent report, which conveys sound and good content.
What is it all about in the end?
It is a question of strengthening transparency, consumer protection, safety and coordination, as well as quality management.
I would, however, warn against our setting up a new authority to deal with these issues.
I see the subsidiarity in the report as positive, and it should definitely be maintained.
It is also very important to me that there is a clear distinction between taxes and fees, and that all tasks that are sovereign in nature, whether they are police or criminal investigation tasks,
whether it is police work, counter-terrorism or anything else, all these measures should clearly be financed from taxpayers ’ money and not from user charges.
If individual Member States have a vital interest in taking security measures beyond this, then I see them as being liable for the costs in this area as well.
Whoever entrusts the security of the military sector must also pay for it.
I certainly see it differently in the technical or personal sphere, which can certainly be to the detriment of the passenger, provided that it is clearly declared what it is about.
It is also worth noting, however, that the impact assessment presented to us by the Commission calculates a cost of around 0.1% of the total transport tariff for these measures.
We should not start too high.
I believe that we can reach a workable compromise with the Federal Republic of Germany, which has exceptional reservations in the area of subsidiarity.
I hope that the final result will be very close to our proposal.
Madam President, Commissioner, President-in-Office, the use of passenger name records is being used in a similar way to SWIFT to combat international terrorism and
serious crime with the fundamental rights of every individual to the protection of his private life and of his informational self-determination.
We must also bear in mind, however, that in Europe and throughout the world, in the age of mobility, there will be no sufficient security without effective and rapid exchange of data.
In the digital age, we must also guarantee special protection with regard to information self-determination and privacy.
I therefore also believe that it is necessary to make a clearer distinction between the data needed to combat crime and the data that is sensitive to privacy.
For me, it is not a question at all that we must incorporate controls, legal remedies, rights of access, claims for damages and the length of the storage period into the agreement.
With the 'push' method, it must be determined whether exceptions can or must be made in urgent cases.
With regard to the use of flight passenger data, we should also include serious crime.
For me, these include offences such as child pornography, trafficking in human beings, murder, rape and also drug trafficking.
In my opinion, this would also be a contribution to protecting the personal rights of those concerned.
I think it is a good thing that we are postponing the decision on the Passenger Name Record (PNR) agreement in question in order to develop a basic model for all future agreements of this kind.
develop and to provide the Commission with a framework for negotiations that takes account of our own ideas on data protection.
Perhaps in the future there will be room to consider carrying out the fight against terrorism and crime together with our transatlantic partners in a joint body.
That would certainly be a step towards combating globalised crime on a global scale.
Thank you. The previous speakers have rightly pointed out the importance of the EIB.
Ms Kadenbach, for example, spoke of the necessary contribution to social convergence.
I would like to put this into a political context.
We are deeply concerned to see that in an increasing number of countries, parties of the extreme right are gaining support.
And they do so because social convergence in the European Union is not working as we would all have liked.
These extreme right-wingers very often appear as wolves in sheep's clothing, concealing behind them massive cynical contempt for humanity.
Last example, Mr President, and I know your political stance: what Mr Mölzer has just done. He gives a very nice speech, but in between he calls one of his political opponents, me, a psychopath.
That is the language of Hitler fascism. That is the way they work. Political opponents have already been branded as subhumans.

I ask you to take appropriate action against this, and I ask the colleagues from the EIB to continue their work, also in the area of transparency, to become even more transparent, because your way of proceeding is also a help in combating the new fascism that is emerging.
Policy coherence for development means that we should not take back with one hand what we give with the other.
For example, it makes little sense to invest aid money in developing agriculture in developing countries if we simultaneously destroy local markets through direct or indirect export subsidies.

Likewise, no matter how much we strive for better healthcare, we will not succeed if we hinder trade in generic medicines or drive up the prices of medicines to unaffordable levels through patent extensions.

And agrofuels may reduce CO emissions in the EU a little, but they lead to deforestation and land-use change in developing countries, and thus to more climate change, which we just wanted to avoid. Not to mention the displacement of indigenous people, loss of biodiversity and land for food crops.
At the moment, we, that is the EU as such, are not particularly coherent.
The EU has, in theory, recognised for some time that policy measures should not contradict each other.
The Treaty of Lisbon enshrines policy coherence for development.
Article 208 states that when implementing political measures that may affect developing countries, the Union shall take into account the objectives of development cooperation.
We must be measured against this demand.
This year, we are reviewing the progress made towards achieving the Millennium Development Goals.
It is clear that without policy coherence, we will not succeed in fulfilling our promises.

We must all be aware of this responsibility, all of us who adopt and implement legislation. Before we adopt a measure, there must be an impact assessment so that we can see what negative effects European legislation might have on developing countries.
We need expertise in the Council, the Commission and Parliament to identify inconsistencies.
The Commission ' s work programme on policy coherence is a good step in this direction.
Now it is a question of how this plan is implemented.

Policy coherence in the interests of development means that we must take greater account of the interests of local people in the fisheries reform and must not subordinate them to the interests of European fisheries companies.
It means that we must dry up tax havens into which money from developing countries flows.
It means that we cannot support ACTA if there is still a suspicion that this agreement could hinder access to medicines or technologies in developing countries.
The Commission has so far been unable to dispel this suspicion.

Policy coherence also means that, when creating the External Action Service, development policy must remain a strong, independent policy area and the Development Commissioner's powers must be extended, not reduced.
It also means, for example, that we should allow fair trade to be used in public procurement in municipalities, as my colleague, Heidi Hautala, has proposed.
In many cases, we Members need to keep a close watch on the Commission and the Council to ensure that they develop policies in a coherent way.
However, in many cases, it is also up to us Members to be consistent in the interests of development.
We need to network better between the committees and we need one or more permanent rapporteurs.
The report, which was adopted unanimously by the Committee on Development, makes many good proposals and takes us a long way forward.
I would like to thank all shadow rapporteurs, the Ombudsman and all the NGOs for their help and cooperation, and I hope that our joint report can be adopted by the plenary tomorrow.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, I have seen from the voting list that you, Mr President, intend to declare amendment 2 inadmissible.
I ask you to reconsider this once again.
It is indeed correct that Article 229 of the Treaty provides for a meeting of the European Parliament on the second Tuesday in March.
But that is not in contradiction to the motion put forward by my group.
My group ’ s motion aims to move the Strasbourg week from the tenth week to the eleventh week.
That does not stop you from holding a plenary session on the second Tuesday in March.
We want the Strasbourg week to be the eleventh week, so I say that there is no inadmissibility here.
I would ask you to reconsider this.

Madam President, in response to the questions raised by my colleague, Manfred Weber, has Commissioner Malmström informed the College that there are serious concerns in this House about the current state of negotiations?
Is the Commission aware that what it has decided today is certainly not what Parliament considers to be sufficient progress?

Are you aware, Mr President, that what you have adopted today, if implemented, will mean that employees of a private company, of the Swift company for example, will have more control rights than European Union officials?
Has Mrs Malmström informed you about these things?
If so, how could you then adopt it?
If not, why didn't Mrs Malmström inform you?
Thank you, President, for your kind words about our debate.
That was not the answer to my question.
So I will try again, more precisely, and I ask you to answer very precisely.

Did Mrs Malmström tell you during the debate in the Commission before the College adopted its decision that there was a risk that Parliament would vote against the text that you are now presenting as an agreement?
Or did Mrs Malmström tell you that with this text we will get a parliamentary majority?
I repeat very precisely: did Mrs Malmström say, 'With this text, we will get a parliamentary majority', or did she say, 'With this text, the parliamentary majority is not certain'?
I would be grateful if you could answer precisely.
Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, for months now, we have been discussing the issue of driving and rest times in road transport.
I am wondering what arguments are now being used to prove that what we have been discussing for months is nonsense.
The most recent argument is that we urgently need to change something here so that nothing changes.
Do you not realise, Mrs Bauer, how absurd this argument has become, how full of lies and half-truths it is?
This dispute is nothing other than a dispute over the extension of self-employed workers ’ working hours.
But in principle, working time legislation has been about protecting people from excessively long working hours for 150 years.
This applies to both the self-employed and the employees.
It makes no difference whether it is a self-employed worker who is overworked or an employer who overworks an employee.
It is about protecting people from themselves and from excessively long working hours, and sometimes also from employers and clients.
Secondly, in terms of road safety, it is claimed that nothing can happen.
Ladies and gentlemen, we are not in a DIY store and we are not amateur craftsmen. We cannot make laws on the basis of the principle of seeing what comes out.
Everyone knows that excessively long working hours are bound to lead to accidents, particularly in road transport, with serious consequences.
Thirdly, who is going to control the kind of detailed rules that you envisage on our roads in future?
Today, a regional employment judge cannot verify whether someone is genuinely self-employed or not without carrying out an extensive review of the case files.
Stop this nonsense. This is about fair competition.
It is not a question of setting a precedent today or tomorrow for future discussions on extending working time.
Do not play such games with this House.
Madam President, today, as for many years, I am fighting for road safety, for better working conditions for our professional drivers and for a better image of their profession.

Clearly, today is not about the traffic safety-related rules for driving and rest times that all self-employed and employed professional drivers must comply with. Today is about working time regulation.
It is primarily a workers ’ protection regulation, and therefore serves only to protect the social interests of drivers and in no way contributes to road safety.
We do not need to protect the self-employed from themselves.
Self-employed lorry and bus drivers cannot be monitored for compliance with working time limits without excessive bureaucracy, nor is there any justifiable reason why they should be monitored at all.
Limiting the working hours of self-employed professional drivers, and perhaps in future self-employed craftsmen, architects or Members of this House, is the wrong signal.
Rather, the Member States should ensure that sham self-employment is not allowed under the
drivers and drivers.
Madam President, I would like to congratulate the rapporteur, but also our shadow rapporteurs. I think that a good report has been produced here.
The main point for me is that both sides, who are currently making life difficult in Bosnia and Herzegovina, should finally come to their senses and move back from their extreme positions.

One extreme position is to repeatedly question Bosnia and the integrity of the country and to threaten referenda that cannot achieve anything, but which do create tension and hatred, and the other extreme position is to question the basic structure of Dayton.
must be adapted.
Yes.
It has already been adopted and more needs to be done.
If we are told time and again that the office of the High Representative should be abolished, I agree.
However, the side that is calling for this must create the conditions for it to happen, so that politicians in the country themselves take responsibility.
That must be our goal, but politicians must also take responsibility.
And let us hope that, in the next elections, the voters will also elect those politicians who are prepared to take responsibility for their own country.
Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, only a few days ago, we saw in the television pictures, with shock, how large areas of Poland and other Central European countries were flooded.
People have died in the floods and our heartfelt sympathies go out to their families. Villages, towns, agricultural land and infrastructure have been destroyed.
The images on television have already been replaced by other images, but the enormous damage still has to be painstakingly repaired.
In dealing with the damage of this magnitude, these countries should also experience the solidarity of the countries of the European Union.
Mr President, after the flood disaster of 1997, Poland and this time also other Central European countries have been hit by a flood disaster once again.
We cannot regulate the weather, and floods are part of the nature of rivers at certain times.
But the questions we have to ask are whether, in the knowledge that these natural events can always happen, the right preventive measures have been taken against
damages caused by such natural events and whether, for example, in landscape planning, due consideration was given to the issue of sustainable disaster protection.
If aid is provided, it should first and foremost benefit those directly affected by the natural disaster in order to secure their livelihoods.
But in international cooperation, too, natural disasters do not stop at national borders. More coordinated action at European level is needed to prevent damage caused by natural disasters.
The joint flood protection measures on the Oder, which have so far been jointly built by Polish and German authorities, show that jointly operated precautionary measures can provide protection.
But we are not yet at the end of the matter.
We do not need a European disaster protection system.
The protection of a country against disasters should be the primary responsibility of a country ’ s government, which should also include the preparation of disaster plans.

However, the delivery of aid in the event of damage and incidents should also be efficiently coordinated at European level, and those organisations which provide aid quickly and without bureaucracy on a voluntary basis should not be left out of pocket.
I therefore support Commissioner Georgieva ’ s plans to ensure that we can quickly implement these.
Unfortunately, my debilitating illness has so far prevented me from speaking in the international public with the clarity I have already achieved on other European issues.
But, Mr President, in one minute.
You are in a historical period.
You will be judged on the financial markets on your most urgent priority, whether you succeed in bringing in regulations that actually close loopholes.
At the moment, everything that has come from the Commission and that we have had in Parliament so far does not rule this out.

There are no necessary improvements here; you will then be remembered in history as someone who, when the house was already ablaze, failed to provide the necessary fire-fighting equipment and certainly not new construction materials.
Point two: equity capital. If we fail to stop this fateful, devastating shadow banking system, not just to curb it, then take away the banks' right to create money.
real economy and also billions of citizens throughout the world, not only their own economic basis for action, but also their trust in everything that is called democracy.
Otherwise, China and the totalitarian regimes that are already keeping an eye on their banks will be better off than the European Union.

And point three: of course we need a system that minimises systemic risks, so that we can implement the 'too big to fail' principle with progressive tax regulations, so that if a bank becomes too big, we have to block it.

In 2007 and 2008, Libya voted against the United Nations General Assembly resolutions calling for a moratorium on executions, and repeated requests by human rights organisations have not yielded any official information.
It is therefore very important to us that the identities of those executed should finally be officially revealed.
Every execution is one too many.
We reject executions and the death penalty.
It is inhuman, it is unkind, and every one of them is one too many.
That is why we very clearly ask in our resolution that we change this, that we make it public and that we now work together to bring about a change here.
No more black figures, but open information about what is happening here, and humane punishment for real criminals, but not excessive punishment for those who are critical of the regime in Tripoli.
Mr President, I wanted to support the motion by Mr Cohn-Bendit on behalf of our group.
We see it in exactly the same way.
This is a highly sensitive, very delicate issue, and it is certainly sensible to find a broad parliamentary majority.
It would therefore be good if the report could be postponed and an attempt made to reach any possible consensus.
Therefore, we support this.
Ladies and gentlemen, in the time available, I will confine myself to the Cancian report.
There is no dispute that passenger rights, and therefore consumer rights, in coach transport must be implemented.
I would like to stress once again what problems exist at the moment for small and medium-sized enterprises, particularly from the point of view of the Federal Republic of Germany.
Local and regional public transport cannot be separated, particularly in rural areas, as they often serve the same radius of about 50 km.
Secondly, it is not possible to make advance payments to small and medium-sized enterprises independently of their debt.
Here we have the principle of liability, which has so far proved itself very well and has also kept fares stable.

Thirdly, I cannot imagine that delays not caused by the entrepreneur should be paid for by the entrepreneur, just as liability should only apply to circumstances for which the entrepreneur is responsible.
Mr President, first of all, I would like to thank Mathieu Grosch for this profound report and for the enormous amount of work that he has done.

Europe without dividing borders cannot be experienced without a functioning transport system, and the European economy would not be successful without an efficient transport system, without efficient, future-proof transport systems on land, on water and in the air, indeed not even conceivable.
We need more community and less national egoism, particularly in the case of environmentally-friendly rail transport.
In order to ensure a sustainable future for transport, all modes of transport must meet the challenges in society, in environmental protection and in the economy.
The report provides valuable and guiding advice on this.
However, there is one point on which I cannot support the report: I cannot support the creation of a transport fund from cohesion policy resources.
Why?
Transport is certainly a special factor in promoting cohesion in Europe, but a separate fund from cohesion policy funds would not meet the expectations it raises,
the Cohesion Fund, and to relieve unduly the Member States that are particularly called upon to ensure a sustainable future for transport.
We have just set up a special committee in Parliament to debate what the EU cohesion and structural funds should look like in the future, and its work should not be prejudged.
Therefore, I ask you to vote in favour of the amendment to the report.
And furthermore, I think that if we talk about sustainability and the future of transport systems, we should also give the maglev technology a future and a chance. Thank you.
Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, we have a single market, but a fragmented system of supervision.
It is obvious that this cannot work.

We need strong central European structures that can draw on the expertise and detailed knowledge of the bodies of the Member States, but which also have enforcement powers in the event of an emergency.

I think that, against the background of the enormous tax burden to which citizens have been exposed as a result of this crisis, they expect that decisions will be taken quickly, speedily and sensibly.
Citizens are not very interested in national power games where it is all about who decides what.
What citizens want is for decisions to be taken effectively, for problems to be solved.

It is objectively the case that, when emergency measures are required, they must be centrally regulated and decided at European level if coordination between the Member States is insufficient and does not work.
This is not in line with the decisions taken by the G20, the G8 and the Heads of State and Government, and unfortunately not at the level of the Council either.
The Council is doing things on a small scale.
This is not an acceptable policy for citizens.
We expect the Council to get moving.

Ladies and gentlemen, Commissioners, you do sometimes wonder what we have actually been doing for over a year, with all the discussions and debates and all the meetings in the trialogue, where I could have imagined spending my time in more pleasant ways.

And here we are, more than a year after the European elections, when we were proclaiming in the streets and squares how we were going to rein in the financial markets, and we are actually faced with a very meagre, if not non-existent, result.
The problem has already been described.
We are dealing with markets that operate globally today.
The Member States still believe that they are able to enforce the rules of the game with their national supervisory structures.
The fact that this did not work has also contributed, not triggered, the financial crisis, but contributed to it, to Europe being hit as well.

And so I do ask myself when this insight, which is so often expressed in Sunday speeches, will be translated into political action, when it will suddenly be a question of protecting stock exchanges, protecting banking structures, and no longer protecting citizens.

We in Parliament have taken on this task, and I hope that the Member States will also take on this task, because they are jointly responsible for the citizens for implementing proper supervisory rules that ensure that individuals' savings are also permanently safe.
I would like to see, Commissioners, that the Commission does everything possible to ensure that we reach a good result as quickly as possible, in the interests of the people of Europe.
Ladies and gentlemen, we in this European Parliament have a responsibility to ensure strong regulation of the financial markets.
Everything we proposed has been seriously watered down in the trialogues.
The Council and the governments do not want strong intervention rights.
Mr Giegold is right when he describes what is currently happening as 'tower of Babel thinking'.
The intention seems to be not to tighten regulation, but simply to make the system a little less prone to breakdowns.
We will have a fragmented supervisory structure that will not be able to really supervise the financial markets.
Insufficient conclusions are being drawn from this financial crisis and this economic crisis, and the next crisis is already being prepared.
Many national economies, including those in Europe, will not survive the next blows, with all the social and economic consequences for the people of Europe.
They expect that this predatory financial market capitalism will be caught again and that the primacy of politics will be restored.
When the UniCredit Bank board chairman, Alessandro Profumo, said at the weekend, "We need a strong and unified European financial supervisory authority and a common rule for all the
Member States and he is calling for stricter capital rules, then that is an appeal to this Parliament to actually establish these rules.
It is the task of this European Parliament to put pressure on the Council to urgently introduce effective regulation. This would be only the first step towards a status quo ante.
Let full-throated promises finally be followed by deeds. My group stands for strong European regulation.
Anything else, no regulation, would be a danger to the euro, to a common EU and to democracy.
Madam President, this debate is poorly timed.
I would have liked to see the Dutch team win, even if it was the last time at this World Cup.
probably a bad omen, because we are not allowed to watch anymore, and then they will probably not win.

But on this subject, Commissioner, you are sending out the right signals. Mr President, today or tomorrow, to be precise, we are finalising a legislative package that we have been discussing for more than two years.
The controversy is not the only reason, but also an upcoming European election and the entry into force of a new EU treaty, which led us to adjust some things.

But I think if we draw a conclusion from today's debate, I come to the conclusion that we are now voting on a compromise that is well worth saying yes to, even if one does so at one point or another with a clenched fist in one's pocket.
I would like to highlight two aspects.
The first are the environmental requirements for industrial plants.
The point is that we need to better enforce best available technology in Europe.

I believe that we have found an exception clause in the compromise that is now before us, which gives the European Commission, Mr Potočnik, and I am addressing this very clearly to you, the tool to intervene in the
in the coming years to pay more attention to the fact that the approximately 52,000 industrial plants affected in Europe ultimately use best available technology better than they do today.
I have heard many arguments in this debate.
I would like to pick up on one point and reflect it here, because I cannot understand it at all.
I have often been told that the use of best available technology is too expensive and that we would thereby endanger the competitiveness of European industry, which is already in crisis.
I do not like to recall my past, but I come from the former GDR, from East Germany.
The economic and ecological collapse of the GDR was also due to the fact that, over decades, investment in the latest technology had been neglected.

I believe that every industry, every industrial operation, is called upon to invest in the latest technology for the sake of its own existence, because otherwise it will lose its competitiveness and, incidentally, also reduce pollutant emissions.
I think today we have the chance to bring into force a law that will improve this initial situation.
The second part of the directive, and this is the 'fist' I mentioned, is the large combustion plants.

Once again, we are talking – we did this in the last directive – about further exemptions until 2020, and in some cases 2023, for large combustion plants that do not have to meet environmental standards, in particular the limits for air pollutants.
I am addressing this to the Council, but perhaps also a little bit to all of us, that we really need to think very carefully about how sensible it actually is that we regularly...
ambitious environmental targets, which we welcome and discuss here, but whenever it comes to implementing them, to putting them into concrete legislation, then a tragedy begins in Europe.
Then the Member States will suddenly realise what it costs and what bills they have to pay, and then those who have made advance payments will usually be punished.
There are a number of Member States that already meet the requirements today and who will end up being left out in the cold.
I will say it again: when it comes to industrial plants and large combustion plants, which are essentially coal-fired power stations, we are not talking about science fiction.
We are not talking about things that are not available, but rather about the state of the art.
Best available techniques, that is, what is available on the market under competitive and cost aspects, can be installed in these plants. And against this background, we are closing.
Today we are voting on a package of laws that I believe is better than what is currently in force, and therefore I encourage all of us to vote in favour.

I would also like to thank the colleagues who have helped to achieve this result over the past two years, sometimes under very difficult circumstances, for their support and their hard work, and above all for the fact that almost all the important political groups have signed up to this compromise.
Mr President, three interesting things have happened while we have been debating. Firstly, it is clear that Europe will be the world champion in football.
A good omen, I believe.
Who exactly it will be, we'll see.
I don't want to give any preferences.
Secondly, Germany is getting compliments from the UK.
Thank you, Chris Davis. That doesn't happen every day.
It is worth noting that.
And thirdly, the world is still in order, even with the Greens, football is more important than environmental protection.
Unfortunately, our colleague Bas Eickhout is already gone again.
But congratulations to our Dutch friends, of course.
There are two aspects I would like to mention in relation to the debate I have just been following.
Firstly, I understand only too well all the worries and concerns of my Eastern European friends.
As an East German, I am half an Eastern European.
I understand that not every environmental step that has been taken in the Western European countries over the last few decades can now be immediately replicated in Eastern Europe.
We must be able to afford high levels of environmental protection.
Yes, that's true.
But I believe that even if you take that into account, it is worth agreeing to this compromise, because it takes account of the very different starting positions of many Member States.
Secondly, the argument that the use of best available technologies endangers competitiveness, particularly vis-à-vis China. This argument is not only false, it is even dangerous.
We have to deal with the fact that industrial processes in Asia are definitely cheaper than they are here.
We will only succeed in this competition if we use the best available technology in as many industrial sectors as possible.
Who does not do this will lose competitiveness.
Nobody is asking, not even this law, for an industrial plant to be switched off tomorrow.
Nobody is asking for a leap from zero to a hundred to achieve any kind of pollutant limit value within a week.
We have enough transition periods, but to say that we should do without it for reasons of competition is wrong.

And I believe that with this directive we have not only made an environmental policy decision, but I also believe that we have made an industrial policy decision that will be worth working on in the coming years. Thank you very much.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to begin by thanking the colleagues from the other political groups for their cooperation in drawing up my report on Kosovo, and also Ms Eva Maat, who has done some very valuable work in the secretariat here.

I would also like to thank you, Commissioner, for your cooperation. I also agree with what you said, that it was important for you to go to northern Kosovo and even spend a night there, so as to make it clear to the population there that you are there for them.
You gave a speech at the university when the EU House was opened.

I think that was an important signal to make it clear what this European Parliament has already said in the past and is saying again now, namely that it supports an independent Kosovo on its way into the European Union.
We are currently in a situation where there is once again an important crossroads for Kosovo, and especially for the citizens of all ethnic groups in Kosovo.
In a few weeks, the International Court of Justice will decide and give its opinion on the status of the declaration of independence made a little over two years ago.

I hope that this decision, this opinion, will then be an opportunity, something like an 'open window', as it is called in English, to put regional cooperation on a new footing, but from the point of view of self-determination.

Kosovo and independence, and to make it clear that the common future for all the states in the region, for Serbia, for Kosovo and all the others, is membership of the European Union, and that on the way there, the problems that still exist must be resolved.
The text of the resolution also makes it clear that the future of Kosovo lies in the European Union.

I am pleased that we have a majority in favour of calling on the five Member States that have not yet done so to do so shortly, and that the Commission is being called on once again to begin visa liberalisation as soon as possible.

After all, the citizens of the country, especially the young people, must not get the impression that they live in an isolated state and cannot get out, while everyone else in the region will soon have this future.
I think that is the task.
I hope that I will find a large majority for this tomorrow in plenary.

I have also tabled a new amendment, which places strong regret at the violence of the last few days at the forefront, and very much hopes for a swift and independent investigation, and that this is not used as a basis for inflaming emotions in the coming days and weeks.
Madam President, High Representative, Commissioner, colleagues, the latest events in Kyrgyzstan, the outbreaks of violence, the slaughter of people, the

The burning of houses and the streams of tens of thousands of refugees who were trying to flee from the violence and leave their homes, all of this has shocked me and has evoked my sympathy for the victims.
At the same time, these events in Kyrgyzstan are a reminder of the situation in Central Asia, which must not leave us here in the European Parliament untouched.
On the contrary, we should give our full attention to the countries of this region.

We should not just focus on the countries that are rich in natural resources and of particular economic interest to us in Europe, but should take all countries into account on an equal footing within the framework of the Central Asia strategy adopted in 2007.
That is, I think, our obligation.
The Central Asian States, which freed themselves from Tsarist and later Soviet rule less than 20 years ago, carry a heavy legacy.
Stalin arbitrarily drawn borders, without regard for the homelands of the peoples living there, clearly make coexistence within and outside the borders of these states more difficult.
poverty, corruption, disrespect for human rights and minority rights, authoritarian regimes, economic conditions, such as in Tajikistan and Kyrgyzstan, which have forced hundreds of thousands into
forced labour immigration and, as we are now seeing in Kyrgyzstan, conflict resolution by use of force, are all casting doubt on the future of these states.
The European Union cannot just stand by and watch.

If we do not want these countries to follow a path towards fundamentalist structures and if we do not want to leave the people there to dictatorial regimes in the long term, then we need to make a full commitment.
In addition to the urgently needed material aid, we need to maintain contact with these countries and actively support the democratic forces developing there.

Let us give Prime Minister Roza Otunbayeva a chance to pacify Kyrgyzstan and to bring it towards stable political conditions. Let us give the resolution we are debating today and will vote on tomorrow

resolution should be followed by deeds, so that the people of Kyrgyzstan and the other Central Asian states feel that we will not abandon them on their way into the future or leave them at the mercy of undemocratic forces.
The expectations of us Europeans in these countries are high. We should not disappoint them. Thank you.
I voted in favour of this report for four reasons.
The service 's architecture has many weaknesses, the subtleties of which will continue to cause us great concern.
The second point is that the European Ambassador will manage Commission funds in future.
This exposes the funds to a wide range of risks, and the insurance against these risks is still completely unclear.
The third point is the giving up of this Parliament ’ s rights in a Council regulation.
We are in this procedure in de facto codecision, but we have allowed the Council regulation to take away rights from Parliament.
Therefore, on my own file, the budgetary procedure, I would like to say that I do not feel bound by today's decisions.
The fourth point is the question of how we actually treat each other in Parliament.
We adopted a reform of Parliament in the last term and procedures which we have now all kicked into touch, with the agreement of the Bureau and the President.
Why are we carrying out a parliamentary reform that lays down procedures if, when push comes to shove, we go against them and do not abide by them?
For these reasons, I cannot vote in favour of this report.
Mr President, Commissioner, I am very much in favour of green genetic engineering, but cloning is something different.
You cannot compare the two at all.
Cloning is animal cruelty, and many colleagues have already said so here.
I also raised this point before the summer break in the discussion we had: only a fraction of animal clones are actually viable.
And those that survive, many of them die very early on, and in great pain.
They suffer from malformations of limbs and organs.
They have immune deficiencies, they are susceptible to disease.
This means pain and suffering and this painful death.
Can we be responsible for that?
We are campaigning for animal protection and then we want to support such a technology.
No, we do not want that, and that is why we decided in July that we do not want cloned meat or animal products from cloned animals.
That is only logical.

Yes, we know that products from cloned animals or their offspring are probably not harmful to health, and I personally assume that they would actually be suitable for human consumption, but there is actually no shortage of meat from conventional production.
Why should we allow ourselves to be drawn into this cloning?
It is completely unnecessary to carry on with this animal cruelty, and the population does not want it either.
The population does not want these products, and we do not need them either. There is no argument for cloned meat in the European Union.
That is why we want a very clear regulation specifically for cloned animals and, yes, also for their offspring.
I want a ban on cloning and cloned products in the European Union, and I believe that we can enforce this.
Of course, this must also apply to imports.
I want to be sure that cloned meat is not being slipped into my food without being labelled, as has already happened here.
Scotland. I do not believe that all meat can be traced back.
It may be possible with beef.
However, with pork, due to the volume produced, it will not be possible to trace back whether there was actually a clone in the ancestry.
Fundamentally, I think that yes, this is a fundamental issue, an ethical issue.
Other Members have said that we must deal with ethical issues, and we are responsible for ethical issues.
There is, of course, the question of animal protection, but there is also another question.
Experience shows – I am an animal producer and I have studied this – that all reproductive techniques, as soon as they really worked in animal production, were ultimately also applied to humans.
So today we have to ask ourselves: in the not too distant future, do we want to and can we accept cloning technology for humans?
I do not want this and I cannot do this.
And even if a spare part clone could one day donate a vital organ to me, a clone of myself, I wouldn't want that.
I do not think that this is an exaggerated horror scenario.
I think that this would be possible in the not too distant future if we were to allow cloning.
We urgently need a ban, not only on cloning, but also on cloned animals and their offspring throughout the EU.
I am not afraid of the WTO.
We can make this happen.
We do not have to copy everything that the United States is imposing on us, no matter how strong our transatlantic friendship.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, it is no coincidence that we cannot easily agree on this issue, because two incompatible matters are being mixed together here.
We could probably have agreed on trade marks if we had concentrated on that.
However, merging it with the subject of the internet is also factually incorrect, because new, different strategies are needed here than in material law.
This cannot simply be transferred.
Furthermore, we must be clear that we pay for any prosecution on the Internet with infringements of civil rights, because we cannot see or find out about it ourselves.
And the third thing is really clear to me: if I already threaten cultural enjoyment on the web with punishment, so that I practically always have one foot in the
prison, if I look into the Internet, then I will not be able to take advantage of the opportunities that the Internet also offers for art, culture and creativity.
That is why I am calling on you: let us continue to talk about this.
Thank you.
Mr President, property, whether it is intellectual or physical property, is a cornerstone of the market economy.
I therefore believe that it is important that we think about how we can regulate this issue together in a good consensus.
We need a warning in all cases.
It must become easier for consumers, for the self-employed and for small and medium-sized enterprises to obtain the relevant licences.
The availability of licences for intellectual property rights valid throughout the EU is particularly important.
We need a single market for intellectual property.
Above all, we also need the original language to be available throughout Europe.
Every language is an important cultural asset and it should be possible for licences granted for a language to apply throughout Europe and thus be used by all citizens.
We need interoperability and technical neutrality in order to be able to use this intellectual property accordingly.
We also need sanctions for those who infringe competition and trade law.
Madam President, I have taken note that the Council does not want to discuss the multiannual financial framework.

I would like to tell the Council that the European Parliament would like to discuss this with you and that the Council cannot behave like a kindergartener and say, 'You do not answer our questions or our proposals'.
Secondly, in this context, I must ask the Council whether it wants Lisbon or not.
Lisbon is not a purely technical change, but means more tasks, more task fulfilment and, therefore, also the need to answer financial questions in this context.
Furthermore, the Council has taken decisions that will lead to new financial expenditure.
This has been explained by many colleagues.

If the Council wants this and if the Council wants no more money to be spent, then the Council must also say where it wants to make savings, how it wants to make savings, where it wants to shift things exactly, and not just make general

proposals for percentage shifts and then leave it to Parliament and the Commission to decide where the cuts should be made, especially as our possibilities for reallocating and changing the budget are extremely limited.

The tasks that you have given us are so challenging that they do not involve small, incidental sums. I will just mention the areas of financial solidity of our states and their corresponding financial difficulties.
If the programme is to be used, then we are not talking about small amounts that could be shifted around.

The same applies to ITER, the same applies to internal security issues, the same applies to major research issues, and the same applies to issues relating to the areas of priority that Parliament has, namely youth, education and research.
We are partly dealing with inadequate implementation, and we are partly dealing with a lack of transparency in the spending of funds.
All these questions must be dealt with concretely and not answered by the Council remaining silent.
Mr President, Commissioner Hahn, ladies and gentlemen, I have a problem with parts of the debate.

When we talk about mountains, islands and sparsely populated regions, I get the impression from some of the contributions that these are assigned extraterritorially and directly to the European Union.
But that is not the case.
Each of these areas belongs to a sovereign Member State.
The implementation of European decisions and objectives is still the responsibility of the Member States, in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity.

If I look at the European level, we have always paid particular attention to precisely these areas: mountain regions, sparsely populated areas, islands, in Commission communications right up to the Lisbon Treaty.
Many decisions have also been taken here in Parliament to support this region.

Now, when I hear a lot of complaints that there are still irregularities in these areas, I ask myself: how have the Member States dealt with the impulses that we have sent out from the European level?
And how have the regions themselves proceeded and used the European funding opportunities?

A new strategy is being called for here. I already feel sorry for the officials in DG REGIO, who are supposed to bring the multitude of problems of islands, mountains and sparsely populated areas together in one strategy.
At the same time, we are experiencing an inflation of strategies: Danube, Baltic Sea, Black Sea, climate change and the like. We need a strategy for the strategies so that we can keep an overview.

Ladies and gentlemen, I think what this is really about, and this has been said today, and I am very grateful to Commissioner Hahn for mentioning this, is that we have to fight for cohesion policy and regional policy at European level to continue after 2013.
That, I think, is the focus of our work and we should convince our colleagues in Parliament that this will be the case.
In continuation of cohesion and regional policy beyond 2013, mountains, islands and the other areas will also have their place.
However, we should not get bogged down in individual motions for resolutions on topics for which there is currently no need.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, first of all, thank you to the colleagues who have worked so intensively on this project, particularly Mr Quadras.
This is proof that European policy is capable of reacting to crises and offering solutions that help people further.
We have had this situation of security of supply on several occasions.
The problem of security of supply has thus become clearer and more obvious.
We were forced to act, and we have acted.

We have found solutions that were not easy, which also mean new instruments, and which, and I think this is the interesting thing about them, do not just create infrastructure standards in Europe, do not just create common rules for prevention and emergency plans, do not just provide certainty, as the Commissioner rightly said,
has shown that we can offer people 30 days of security in the future, but also shown that we are able to explore compromises, compromises that then lead to workable solutions.

It was a long haul, but now it is becoming clear that both the role and the responsibility of the companies that build the networks and are responsible for the networks are at the heart of the matter, but also the flexibility of the policy that says we will not force things that will not make sense in the end and will not be clever.

are no longer appropriate. For example, flexibility in the case of repayments, the necessary repayments, but not saying that this applies to everything, all the time and at any price, but also the necessity or the possibility of being able to assess individual cases, as well as strengthening cooperation at regional level.

I think it is equally important to point out that the Member States must also carry out their responsibilities, but if no solution can be found there, the Commission gets an additional task. The Commission, which is strengthened, but which does not decide alone, but which decides when no other solutions are possible or which has the final say.
So flexibility, solutions that keep the burden within manageable limits, the but ensure that there are solutions, and that is the good news from Brussels for the people of Europe.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, Commissioners, I do not need to stress how important cohesion and regional policy are for living together and growing together in Europe.
I also believe, however, that future regional and cohesion policy must focus more on cities.
After all, cities are an important driving force in the development of Europe.
At the same time, however, they are currently experiencing ecological, economic and social problems in a particularly acute way.
So, my very specific question to Commissioner Hahn is: what possibilities do you see to highlight the urban dimension?

Commissioner, more specifically, you recently suggested at an event here in Parliament that you could envisage a certain percentage of regional funds being reserved, precisely to highlight the urban dimension.
Are these considerations still relevant?
If yes, how high could this percentage possibly be?

Mr President, first of all, I would like to thank Commissioner Hahn for starting a new debate here as former Minister for the Economy and Innovation, which I believe can make a major contribution to growth in Europe.
If every small and medium-sized enterprise in Europe created an additional job, we would have no unemployed people in Europe.
We should therefore consider how we can encourage small and medium-sized enterprises to hire new employees and become active on international export markets.
But the research infrastructure is also an important issue, as is the infrastructure in the transport sector.
The Commission has a lot of work to do here, especially in restructuring the east-west links, for example Paris-Budapest or the North-South links.
Additional jobs can be created here, which we urgently need in Europe at the moment.

Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, there is no doubt that we have made progress in the last few years in terms of equality between men and women. Nevertheless, there are still many women who are being pushed into low-skilled jobs.
This applies not only to activities that are traditionally carried out within the family.
In the deregulation of recent years, social security - liable employment has often been converted into atypical, precarious and insecure working conditions.
especially women.
Employment development in Europe from 2000 to 2010 included 60% new precarious and atypical employment relationships, two-thirds of which were women.
Women are often denied democratic participation in companies because of their part-time or precarious employment.
However, women today are far more involved and better educated than women of all previous generations.
Nevertheless, they earn on average 25% less than men.

We have discussed this issue in great detail in the Committee on Employment and Social Affairs and have developed good proposals on the issue of women 's employment and their inclusion, regardless of the status of their work.

social security systems, the creation of childcare facilities, the orientation of pensions and social systems towards independent living, and the shaping of work according to the principles of good work.
All of this has essentially been taken up in the report on precarious employment of women.
The Committee on Employment and Social Affairs is satisfied with the outcome of this work and requests your approval of the report.
Madam President, Mr President-in-Office of the Council, landmines will probably still be a major problem in the distant future.
Do you believe it is possible to initiate our own research programmes to detect landmines?
This is not just a European problem, it is a global problem.
Can you imagine finding suitable institutions here that are involved in research programmes in this area?

Ladies and gentlemen, in the transatlantic dialogue, there is much that unites us: these are the fundamental values, the fundamental rights of freedom and democracy. But there is also a lot that divides us, namely the application and concretisation of these respective rights when it comes to transatlantic relations.
This contradiction becomes particularly clear when it comes to security issues.
Data protection has long been a source of constant misunderstandings.
Therefore, if we succeed in obtaining a very good framework agreement, it will be a very big step forward.
We must find a solution here so that citizens on both sides of the Atlantic can assert their rights with the other partner.
A second important point where we have similar foundations but very different approaches is climate protection.
Therefore, I very much hope that it will become a subject and that it will be discussed clearly.
The European Union must not use the transatlantic dialogue to weaken its own promises and commitments.

For the first time, the Alternative Investment Fund Managers Directive brings a certain amount of transparency and rules into this impenetrable thicket of hedge funds and alternative investment funds.
Unfortunately, these rules have remained incomplete.
It is still possible to bring unregulated products onto the common market through passive sales.
In principle, market access for these products is not effectively restricted.
Europe has thus missed an opportunity to extend its rules internationally and to achieve uniform regulation.
Secondly, unfortunately, contrary to Parliament's wishes, there is no effective debt brake at European level for the funds.
The rules on how high the funds' indebtedness can be remain solely in the hands of national authorities, and this means that the consequences of the crisis are not being drawn at this point.
There will be a national race to the bottom in terms of regulation.
What is decisive for us once again is that the looting of companies by private equity funds will not be effectively prevented.
Of course, there are private equity investments that make sense for companies.
Unfortunately, however, the rules adopted now are far from sufficient.
The information rights of employees, which they are entitled to expect, as well as the protection of small and medium-sized enterprises from this form of looting, are not effective.
It is therefore very difficult to justify this to the electorate.

Unfortunately, the responsibility for this does not lie with Parliament, but rather with the intensive lobbying activities of the relevant industry, which has been particularly successful, especially with the British
governments, the French government, but I am afraid I must also say, in part, the German government. In Parliament, we had representatives of these lobbies, mainly in the form of the Liberal Group.
We cannot support this proposal, because it would set a precedent for the failure of Parliament to enforce effective rules.
It is not possible for us to go before our voters with our heads held high and say that we have succeeded in effectively regulating this shadow economy.
We hope that we will make further progress when the directive is revised.
Thank you once again, especially for the cooperation here with the colleagues in Parliament.
Thank you. First of all, I would like to thank Mr. Wałasek for his cooperation.
I can only underline that. Even with different positions, it has been good cooperation.
Many people thought – I want to stress this once again – many people thought that the crisis of the euro area was over.
Ireland shows that this has not been the case.
Europe continues to voluntarily hand itself over to the financial markets.
EU Member States must continue to sell their sovereign debt on the financial markets and earn the confidence of speculators by committing fiscal suicide.
Over the weekend, we had to see once again where this leads.

This time it was not the Greeks, who are supposedly prone to fraud, but the model pupil Ireland, which was driven into the European rescue fund to rescue its banking system, which for a long time was also considered exemplary, as an example of deregulation.
Meanwhile, the financial sector is already eyeing the capitalisation of Portugal, whose interest rates are rising ever further.
The ECB bears some responsibility for this tragedy.
It has failed to do so and continues to fail by imposing Eurobonds, a civilised alternative to the state casino.
While one half of Europe is recovering from the crisis, the ECB is calmly watching the other half fall further into poverty and insecurity.
The destruction of entire economies may suit the taste of the European fund and banking lobby, but it has helped neither Ireland nor the currency union nor the ECB.
Mr Trichet, do not listen to Mrs Merkel's simple financial ABC.
If you want to save the economic and monetary union, do not let European government bonds hang in the air.
Introduce Eurobonds before the next victim of speculation falls.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, if you consider how we discussed this Commission proposal in the last parliamentary term, from total rejection to cheering, saying that wonderful things were coming our way, then I have to say that I am delighted with what is now before us.
I would like to express my sincere thanks to the rapporteur and his co-rapporteurs for having produced a decent opinion from this wide spectrum of opinions.
I think the magic word for this is patient orientation.
So, the many side effects were not taken into account and they said: what does the patient need?
I believe that it is right that we have decided that patients should be able to obtain information in various ways and not just one.
However, I would like to stress that the doctor will continue to have to be the patient ’ s confidant and will certainly continue to have an influence on key issues.
We guarantee that because we have banned advertising for it and the information that patients receive has been checked and approved by an authority.
The information is thus scientifically translated and the patient can certainly deal with it.
Of course, it is right that not everyone can investigate this.
But we have different ways of doing that, and I think it is so widespread that we do not need to worry.
I only hope that the Council will finally have the courage to take up this issue.
It is very important that we act quickly.
Patients expect this.
I think we will have done a good job.
Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, coal is part of the energy supply, that is not disputed.
It is also indisputable that competitiveness must be ensured and that subsidising cannot be the right way.
The question now is whether the date of 2014 or 2018 is the right one.
Mr Rapkay has put forward a lot of sensible arguments, which I do not need to repeat.

My request to the Commission is really only this: there is clearly a large majority in Parliament, in all the committees involved, in favour of saying yes to the question of whether competitiveness must be a criterion.

But that on the question of the date, we simply provide the necessary flexibility, which is certain to create the possibility for the Member States to implement it, and that the burdens for people, for jobs, for economic structures, remain manageable.

The only argument that was mentioned, which incidentally was also contrary to the impact assessment of the Commission, which initially seemed to support 218, was the argument that climate policy reasons could play a role here.
I would like to stress this point once again: it makes no difference to the climate whether we burn foreign coal or our own coal.

My request to the Commissioner is to consider whether this four-year deadline, Parliament and, if such a new argument were to arise in the Council, whether it might not lead the Commission to become a little more flexible.
I take it that the Commission is giving this some thought, and I thank you for that.

Mr President, Minister, Commissioner, if we have to draw a lesson from Copenhagen, where you, Commissioner, held the Presidency, it is that in future the European Union must speak with one voice at such conferences.

We may have different opinions in one or two places, but if we Europeans are already speaking with different voices there, then our weight will not be credible, and if we are not credible, then other people will not follow us.
Why should they?
That is why I believe that the figures that we decided on at the end of 2002 and under Chancellor Merkel in 2007 must basically be maintained and represent the foundations of these negotiations.

I agree with you that the ambitious targets of two degrees should not be seriously questioned today in favour of a significantly lower degree figure, because that would make the achievement of these targets extremely difficult and would cause people to lose heart.
I am greatly concerned that we are calling our intellectual property into question.
Our industry invests money and manpower to develop things.
If we now go and loosen the fence around this intellectual property and leave the holes in it as they are at the moment, then the valuable parts will run away from us, namely our property.
I am very much in favour of technology transfer, but our companies must also see a point in investing in this, and therefore intellectual property must not be weakened.
As regards the commitments, I also tend to demand serious commitments from our partners worldwide.
However, they must be credible and they must be feasible for these countries.
Otherwise, we will do exactly the opposite of what we want, namely that they will move away from us.
So, being credible in Cancún is an important point.
The really big decisions will be taken later in Johannesburg, but in Cancún, I think we should prepare the ground.
I am a farmer by profession.
If the field is not in order, you will not get a good harvest.
Let us focus on good arable land.
Mr President, there was an incident this morning during the debate on the European Council, which I do not want to go into myself, because I was the target of an insult.
But I would say that there are levels on which I cannot be insulted and where I do not perceive it as an insult, because to insult me, you need a certain degree of seriousness.
I would, however, like to express my heartfelt thanks for the countless signs of solidarity that I have received here in this House since then.
I would like to thank you all, dear colleagues from all the political groups.
I think this is a sign of our shared democratic and pro-European convictions, which the overwhelming majority of this House shares.
Thank you very much.

Mr President, Commissioner, Minister, Mr Gauzès, colleagues, shadow rapporteurs, I am pleased that we have now taken this step, that for the first time a sector of the European financial system is being placed under European direct supervision.
This is also a step towards a strong Europe in a single market, with strong controls over a sector that contributed to the crisis.
I would like to make one further comment on this, Mr Barnier. The selection processes for the new bodies are currently under way, and the new bodies will only be as good as their staff.

I can only hope that when it comes to selecting staff, we are really talking about expertise, independence from the financial industry that you are supposed to be controlling, and a clear commitment to serving the public interest, and that in the selection procedure we will not see national quotas again, overriding these important criteria.
Furthermore, there is still a lot to do in the area of credit rating agencies.

We still do not have proper transparency of the information on which the rating agencies work. We have far too much use of regulatory ratings in financial markets and in regulations on the financial industry.
We have serious conflicts of interest, we clearly have a market dominated by three major suppliers, and we have no liability for ratings that are compiled carelessly.
Therefore, Commissioner, I would like to say that it is good that the consultation is now under way and we look forward to the results.
I also think that civil society should participate in this consultation, but then we must make a courageous proposal.
I am already looking forward to your proposal.
Be as courageous as you have been so far and let us jointly produce legislation next year that really solves the problems that still exist in the ratings market at European level.
We owe this to our citizens.
Mr President, Minister, Mr Barnier, ladies and gentlemen, the last few months since the beginning of this year have shown what damage credit rating agencies can do.
There is an urgent need for action.
I therefore welcome the directive, and in particular I welcome the fact that ESMA will be entrusted with the supervision and registration of rating agencies.
I think this is an important and good step forward.
However, many good and forward-looking proposals have been lost in the negotiations on the report.
I would like to point that out once again.
The appointment of the ESM is therefore the only real and positive innovation.
The proposals for a public database for the production of ratings have disappeared, as has any mention of the creation of a public rating agency.
The consistent and continuous review of ratings by supervisory authorities has also fallen through the cracks.
