The Commission had also proposed funding for independent ratings in order to overcome the fateful issuer-pays model for the assessment of financial market products, but nothing has come of that either.
I hope that these aspects will be taken into account in the reports still to come on this subject that Mr Gauzès and you, Mr Barnier, have mentioned.
Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, first of all, I would like to thank those who have worked on this report, in particular Mr Bendtsen.
I would like to draw attention to two aspects and perhaps make you think a little.
We agree that energy efficiency, energy-saving use, is the solution: the simplest and most sensible solution.
The question is how we are going to achieve this goal.
And I would like to ask: what has it actually achieved that we in Parliament have approached people again and again with new and higher percentages?
Have we really achieved more and moved more?
Or why and where has energy, is energy being used more sparingly?
I have learned that the industry, which consumes a lot of energy, uses energy more sparingly because it saves money and because it is advantageous for it.
I have learned that people, when they know that this device uses more energy or less energy, when they see that and can decide for themselves that it is in their interest, will then make that choice.
Perhaps the method is not to work with percentages at all, but rather to use incentives.
Second question: the Commissioner has just very clearly and emphatically explained how complicated it is just to describe the concept, what is it actually?
How different is this in the Member States?
The honourable Member has just pointed out that the Member States have very different situations, economically and geographically.
Is the right answer to work with one figure, with one binding figure, or should we not think of something else?

I think that in energy policy, we need to move away from the debate of the past, which thought that we could achieve something with simple figures, with binding targets and with such miracle solutions, because clearly we have not achieved enough.
Perhaps we should try the other way, the way of differentiated, varied responses, which take account of the different situations and sensitivities, but which also have an effect.
It is the effects that matter, not our feelings.
This regulation that we are discussing today has not received a lot of attention in all the committees for a long time.
Now, at the end, when the result was in, some people in this House woke up.
Some have unfortunately not turned up for the debate, although it would have been very interesting to hear their opinions.
As a former member of the Convention, I still remember the spirit of the Convention very clearly.
has clearly called in many areas for the European Parliament to be granted certain rights in order to maintain its credibility within the institutions and with the citizens.
The Convention has not given us a free gift.
These were extremely difficult negotiations, particularly those conducted by the Belgian Presidency with the Council, which have enabled some important progress to be made.
I would like to recall what Mr Lehne said: it was the question of the constant possibility of checking whether the Commission has overstepped its powers.
Of course, there are some not entirely ideal arrangements, such as the international trade agreements, but nevertheless I can declare myself in agreement with the result.
I would like to thank the Belgian Presidency.
She has really stuck her neck out to make sure that something was done, and so has our rapporteur, who negotiated with heart and mind.
I would like to make the following statement on behalf of our group. This is a motion that my colleague Göran Färm submitted on behalf of our group in the Budget Committee.
We did not resubmit this amendment, but the Group of the Greens / European Free Alliance has resubmitted the motion with 40 signatures from Green Members.
We will abstain on this motion.
However, I have reached agreement with the Green Members on this. We are absolutely in agreement on the substance.
However, since I have said in principle that we as a group will not table any new amendments, we will abstain.

I would like to say clearly, however, that we support this point, the financial transaction tax, and hope that our colleagues on the other side of the House will support us in the votes in the next few days.
I think it is important that we do not use the national tax systems against the internal market.
This is particularly important when it comes to cross-border services, where equal treatment is required.
We now have a negative example in Hungary, where retroactive price controls have been used as a form of crisis taxation.
This is unacceptable. The financial, insurance and trading sectors are particularly affected, but energy is also very strongly affected.
I hope that this will not also be used in the area of health services in the future.
I would also like to ask President Orbán to review these regulations, because they are in themselves completely contrary to the concept of the internal market.
It is important that the EU addresses the problems of migration and asylum law.
The Commission has accordingly tabled proposals.
There is currently no precise analysis of the implementation of the existing legal provisions.
There are hardly any mature calculations and analyses of the new proposals either.
I note that the EPP unreservedly supports the right to asylum and the right to protection for those in need of protection.
However, looking at reality, we are also faced with the problem that the right to asylum is being claimed for very different reasons or is even being deliberately abused.
The right to asylum and subsidiary protection is not an instrument for general immigration into the 27 Member States.
We must also ensure that organised human traffickers are not given the chance to make billions out of the fate of people by exploiting our asylum laws.
Asylum procedures must be carried out in a qualified and careful manner.
Persecuted people must have the guarantee that they will find protection in the EU.
Accordingly, the new drafts provide for corresponding obligations on the Commission.
I believe many of them are correct, such as interpreters, proper medical treatment and taking into account special protection needs.
However, I would like to mention some points that I consider to be problematic.
The authorities of the Member States have far too few possibilities of preventing abuse.
The possibility of carrying out accelerated and border procedures should be restricted excessively.

If an applicant seriously violates his cooperation obligations, there are hardly any sanctions available. On the contrary, if he goes underground illegally, the Member State should not be able to close his procedure negatively, but when he reappears, he should be subject to an extended range of procedural options.
Even in cases of manifest unfoundedness, a fast-track procedure should be possible only after the second request for renewal.
This means considerable increases in costs.
The free legal advice in the form of a lawyer, as is currently the case according to the wording of the Commission draft, is also a considerable increase in costs for the Member States.
I simply ask the Commission to reconsider the consequences of its proposals on practical reality, the financial implications and the problems for the administrations in the Member States.
We want a high standard, but it must also be feasible and must not set impossible tasks for particularly affected Member States.
Mr President, thank you very much for allowing me to speak again briefly.
I have a big request for you, Commissioner Füle.
I would like to begin by saying that false assumptions lead to false cost estimates.
We currently do not have falling numbers in the area of asylum seekers, but clearly rising numbers.
It has to do with visa facilitation, for example, for Serbia and Macedonia.
We are currently also seeing a massive increase in abuse of asylum rights in an attempt to enter a country in the European Union.

If one starts from the false assumption that, by disputing the first instance in a higher qualified form, costs would be saved in further instances, one automatically assumes that all Member States have a poor first instance.
That is not the case, however.
If we increase the standards overall, it will inevitably lead to increased costs.
Increasing quality standards for everyone and also making procedures more complicated, and I am happy to discuss this, leads to more costs in practical reality.
I would therefore like to ask you once again to take the concerns of the Member States seriously, because we have practical reports from the Member States, and we will continue to receive them.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the Commission is credited in this report with having a successful competition policy.
However, we must not forget that unregulated competition can also contribute to rising unemployment and to the growing gap between rich and poor.
Unfettered competition still takes precedence over people ' s social needs in the European Union.
The credo for free markets cannot disguise the fact that they not only fail again and again, but are incapable of establishing social justice in the long term.
A few proceedings on anti-competitive practices and price-fixing do not solve this problem.
A fair and at the same time social internal market can only function with strong and fair regulation.
More is urgently needed.
For example, it is not acceptable to subject public services to private competition.
The report sets out pointers for the Commission's future competition policy.
Much stronger consumer protection, the exclusion of public services from competition rules and the monitoring of cartels: all of this is urgently needed.
Furthermore, a treaty clause for guaranteed social progress, which is at least on an equal footing with competition rules in Europe, would be necessary.
Ladies and gentlemen, it is a really important moment that today we are commemorating the Holocaust of Roma and Sinti for the first time in the European Parliament.
Let me begin with the farewell letter written in 1943 by a 14-year-old boy, Robert Reinhardt, before his deportation from a German children's home to Auschwitz.
He wrote: "I have found my parents again."
We are on the transport to the concentration camp.
I have internally struggled so far that I can endure death.
Thank you once again for everything you have done for me.
Greetings to all, see you in heaven.
Your Robert.

Ladies and gentlemen, this boy, like 500 000 other Roma and Sinti, did not return. And although Roma and Sinti, together with Jews, were among the first victims of industrial mass killings, today, 66 years later, the full truth about this remains unspoken.
And the complicity of the majority society is often concealed.
We need the whole truth about this chapter of history.
The Holocaust of the Roma and Sinti must be punished and recognised as a crime against humanity so that this history does not repeat itself.
We must fight the prejudices against these people with passion.
Racist incitements and also acts of violence, as took place in Hungary, for example, last year or the year before, are something we must put a stop to, and we must do so with civil courage.
Freedom, equality and solidarity are not the privilege of individuals, but the right of all, because human and civil rights are indivisible. Thank you.
Yes, dear ladies and gentlemen, colleagues, from my point of view, it is now about using the possibilities of the law in the best possible way.
of a strong financial supervision in Europe. We have our doubts whether these possibilities will really be fully exploited institutionally.
We see shortcomings in the procedure. As Parliament, we have not received the list of candidates in advance, but only the final result.
It would be easy for us to send the executive directors back now.

We clearly have a lack of gender equality in the appointment of posts, and we have, due to certain events in the process, doubts that the Member States really appoint these institutions in the European interest.
And the other points in our letter justify asking a fundamental question.
But beyond that, of course, it is a question of the suitability of the individual, and each person must be assessed individually and according to their competences.
There we see two major problems.

We have seen in the hearings that one of the candidates questioned whether this is a European institution, and instead spoke of a network of supervisory authorities, and was not prepared to correct that, even when asked.
On the question of whether they are prepared to make all their contacts with the financial sector transparent, we have not received any clear statements.
We have sent a letter to all three candidates, and our vote will depend on whether we receive clear and comprehensive answers to it.
Mr President, we, the Social Democratic Group, have tabled the same motion.
However, I would like to recommend to Mr Mauro and to this House that we express it more precisely.
We are dealing with a very large influx.

Italy, like every other country in the Mediterranean region, is certainly overwhelmed by the current problem and certainly Italy will not be the only country to be confronted with the mass influx of refugees.
We can assume that Malta, Greece and Spain will also be confronted with this problem in the foreseeable future.

So we would ask that the title of the debate be very precise, namely, a statement by the Commission on immediate measures by the European Union to support Italy and other EU countries affected in coping with the influx of refugees.
Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I warmly welcome the outcome of the conciliation procedure.

With the implementation of the regulation, we will succeed in ensuring that, in the area of coach transport, firstly, all passengers, including those with reduced mobility, will have a high level of protection comparable to the standard of other modes of transport.
people, secondly, a high general level of consumer protection is ensured and thirdly, account is taken of the fact that this is a transport sector dominated by small and medium-sized enterprises.

This means that the extreme demands that would have made bus transport almost completely impossible due to excessive demands are off the table, as are demands that would have taken the majority of Member States completely out of the scope of application of the regulation.
Bus services, which benefit the general public, must also offer a high quality of service to disabled people and people with reduced mobility.
They should have a right to assistance, and in the interests of social integration, this should be free of charge.
Madam President, I would like to congratulate everyone, particularly Mr Cancian, for his efforts in the difficult and lengthy process of drawing up a compromise on this difficult dossier.
There have been successes, which have already been mentioned, but there have also been disappointments, such as the scope of application and other niggling details, which have already been mentioned.
With this compromise on bus transport, we have covered passenger rights for all modes of transport.
The next necessary step is to create a joint, coherent and efficient framework programme for all passenger rights.
Fair and clearly defined rights are particularly important for the EU ’ s credibility with European citizens.
In order to be credible, the coherence of rights for the various modes of transport must therefore be respected.
It is not acceptable that fundamentally different criteria apply to the rights of air passengers than, for example, to those of rail passengers.

Therefore, when drawing up a framework programme, it is particularly important that we do not aim for the lowest and weakest standards, but that we do not lose sight of ambitious goals in the interests of the citizens.

Madam President, Mr President-in-Office of the Council, Mr Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, we are in the middle of a major economic crisis. We have been talking about that for months, and that is all true. However, we usually talk in headlines and then we carry on here as if nothing had happened.
I think it is because we in Parliament do not behave concretely enough or honestly enough, but rather we are engaged in headline-grabbing politics.
What are we actually doing to promote growth and stability?
Are we not ourselves helping to bring about a more proper and intelligent use of funds?
Are we really carrying out a critical examination of whether we are making the right expenditures or does each and every one of us not have a new idea every day as to where we can spend more money?

If we do not have the strength to concentrate the financial resources on the future projects, and that means conflict and disagreement and decisions, then it makes no sense to talk about innovation and research without making the funds available.
It is relatively dishonest.
If we want to stimulate the economy, then we must also pursue a policy that stimulates the economy, and not spend all day thinking about how we can disrupt industry or the economy.
We continue to adopt decisions with gusto, and today we are constantly calling for new decisions which hamper economic development.
Does anyone really believe that the question of binding energy efficiency targets, to take just one example, will contribute to economic growth?
Do you really believe that the decisions we have taken in the context of energy have stimulated the economy?
Or did we disturb them?
I would like us to talk about this more honestly and then perhaps make fewer decisions, but make the decisions that will really move things forward.
It will be more demanding, more laborious, and will also mean conflict, but that is the essence of democracy.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the call for a financial transaction tax is nothing new. For many years, opponents of globalisation have been calling for such a tax.
Yet, only five years ago, hardly anyone would have believed that it could be implemented in the foreseeable future.
But this week it is on the agenda of the European Parliament, together with a CO tax and Eurobonds, and I think that is a good thing.
I can only welcome and support the financial instruments proposed by Anni Podimata in her report, and I think we will vote accordingly.
They are urgently needed instruments.
Consolidation of public budgets has so far focused solely on cutting expenditure.
However, cutting public spending primarily affects employees, pensioners and the poor, in other words, those who depend on the welfare state.
The revenue side of public finances has so far been completely overlooked.
It is the second adjustment knob that can be turned in order to consolidate public budgets.
Above all, however, the main causes of the public debt crisis and the repayment of public debt can be made to contribute appropriately on the revenue side.
Increasing tax revenues is simply essential for consolidating public finances.

In fact, the high level of public debt is mainly a result of the nationalisation of private debt, namely the taking over of private banks' debts and the public sector financing the consequences of the financial crisis.
The Member States are by no means the only ones responsible for the debt crisis.
It is therefore not only logical but also politically necessary to ask the financial sector to pay up.
The financial transaction tax would finally involve this sector, which is the main cause of public debt, in paying off this debt.
This is an important political signal that will be sent out by this report if it is adopted in the way that it has been proposed.
But we also believe that Eurobonds are a sensible and therefore necessary instrument.
They contribute more to reducing debt than all the sanctions or more or less well-intentioned advice.

The complaints from some of the surplus countries that their interest payments will rise as a result of Eurobonds are not acceptable, because at the same time these surplus countries also earn money from their exports to the deficit countries.
You can criticise that as a transfer union.
Anyone who wants a social Europe, who wants the EU to hold together in the future, must at least agree in principle to a transfer union.
Finally, I would like to say to the Commission that I hope that it will now finally submit the proposals for a financial transaction tax that have been demanded for over a year.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the bail-out packages are supposed to protect EU Member States from insolvency.
But they are burdening people with brutal austerity programmes and leaving the culprits unpunished. Furthermore, the Commission is now also interfering in national wage policies.
Mr Hahn, it is absurd and brings tears to one ’ s eyes to hear this formal argument that none of this was meant to be social policy.
This position is totally unacceptable. Pay policy is not the EU ’ s business. This policy of the Commission is in flagrant contradiction with the idea of the European Union.
The European Union was never a project for wage competition and social dumping.
It talks about the rigidity of wages that are to be reduced.
The wages should reflect market conditions.
Ladies and gentlemen, the blind speak about colour. People who put it like this have no idea about collective bargaining systems.
You are interfering in collective bargaining autonomy and you are turning the much-vaunted autonomy of the social partners and social dialogue into an absurdity.

I want to say clearly that this Parliament is called upon to ensure the maintenance of autonomous collective bargaining policy, to protect social dialogue and to prevent further erosion of social security.
We need measures against counterproductive austerity policies and punitive actions.
Mr Barroso, you are a clever man, but you have not answered my question.

So I am asking again: when Mr Oettinger says, 'We currently suspect that certain installations connected to the network will not survive our stress tests', I interpret that as meaning that you are aware that these installations are so poorly secured that they will not meet the criteria.
Why are you not intervening today?
In my eyes, this is a real danger.
Madam President, I would like to ask the two representatives of the Council and the Commission why they think they have to come here today with their talking points.
I do not think that it will help anyone if it is made very clear that, in preparation for the Istanbul Conference, the Member States of the European Union and the European Union
must finally fully meet their commitments to development aid and that it cannot be tolerated in any way that Member States are increasingly shirking this commitment.
It is not acceptable that one billion people go to bed hungry every day, most of them in the LDCs, the poorest countries in the world.

It is not acceptable that a large proportion of our development aid does not go towards promoting and building up agricultural structures, but rather that this proportion has rapidly declined in recent years, despite the fact that one billion people are starving.
Then to talk about hoping that the LDCs will become more resilient to shocks, I am beginning to think that is cynical.
I call on you to address the problems clearly and specifically and to take on specific commitments to actually help the LDCs.
In connection with the internal market, public procurement law, the awarding of public contracts, is an essential component.

Mr Barnier, when you say that you want to promote social integration in this context, I have to ask you: I have heard little about protection of social rights, I have heard little about protection of collective agreements.
That is one of the basic rules that you say you do not want to call into question.

If we want to protect fundamental rules, we must ensure that basic social standards are protected when public contracts are awarded, that collective agreements are not undermined and that wage dumping is prevented.
This is part of a modernised public procurement law.
I would like to state clearly that social fundamental rights must no longer be subordinated to economic freedoms and free competition.
Either we want a social Europe or we want a permanent conflict with the people who are turning away from this Europe in which they do not see their interests safeguarded. Thank you.
Madam President, Commissioner Kroes, radio frequencies are a precious and limited resource which must be managed with care, foresight and taking into account all societal aspects.
That is why the Radio Spectrum Policy Programme is of such particular importance.
Radio frequencies must be used efficiently, taking into account economic, social and cultural aspects.
All technical possibilities must be fully exploited in all frequency bands in order to achieve maximum efficiency.

The Telecommunications Package adopted last year must be the basis of radio spectrum policy, because it also establishes that radio spectrum management in the EU and its Member States must meet cultural, social and economic interests equally.
This must be taken into account in particular when harmonising frequency use in the EU.
In this context, I would like to point out that the implementation of the Digital Dividend 1 has so far been implemented in the Member States at very different speeds.
Therefore, further opening of the UHF band, particularly below 800 MHz, should only be considered after taking into account the experience gained from freeing up this band.
Many technical and financial issues have not yet been finally resolved.

The issue of interference and the question of compensation for cultural institutions and local authorities in the event of technical conversion, for example in the area of wireless microphones, have still not been satisfactorily resolved.
The different interests must be responsibly balanced against each other when allocating frequency bands.

On the one hand, providing universal mobile broadband coverage is a basic demand in the interests of European citizens. At the same time, however, we must continue to take into account developments in the area of public and private broadcasting as an important instrument for information and cultural diversity.
We must set the course correctly for Europe's technical development, its positioning in the field of digital information technology and cultural diversity.
Mr President, Mr Rehn, you have described the situation and said that it has got worse.
The Eurozone, the gap is widening between countries that are recovering and countries that are in a weaker position.
The costs of supporting the weaker countries are rising ever higher.

In this situation, I have a few questions for you. As the Commissioner for Economic and Monetary Affairs, I would like to ask you: on the two-tier accounts of the ECB system, liabilities and claims between central banks have piled up to over EUR 400 billion.
This is because banks are still not being financed sufficiently from the capital markets.
How do you assess these liabilities that are not provided for in the euro system?
Then, the big problem why we are not making progress with the restructuring of the debt is that the banking system, as Mrs Ford also said, is not properly set up.
The proposal for a European restructuring system has been delayed.
When is the Commission going to put this forward?
As Economic and Monetary Commissioner, how do you assess this delay?
What are you doing to make it go faster?
Then we saw, as we Greens had been saying for some time, that the measures against Greece and Portugal would not work as long as the macroeconomic conditions in the euro area were not better.
We still have absolutely rising interest burdens, macroeconomically, for these countries.
We are seeing increasing capital exports and capital flight from some of these countries.
We still have insufficient demand in a number of European partner countries, particularly in the stronger eurozone countries.
We have rising resources and food prices, at least if you take the recent ups and downs out of account.

I wonder, apart from the bail-outs for these countries, apart from the help on the financing side, what measures are being taken to ensure that the weaker countries receive relief on the macroeconomic fronts, on the tax front, on the front of external demand, on the interest rate front?
And how
I would therefore encourage you to come forward with a strong package that improves the macroeconomic conditions of these countries in the euro area.

And lastly, in my view, the measures have gone against the spirit of the EU Treaty by hitting the weakest in Greece, Portugal and Spain, while the stronger, the wealthy, have not so far been called upon to shoulder any of the burdens.
What measures will you take to ensure that the wealthy in particular are called upon to contribute to the cost of this crisis?
Mr President, Mrs Győri, Mrs González Pérez, ladies and gentlemen,
You can be against Ferenc Gyurcsány or you can be for Ferenc Gyurcsány.
You can either reject or support Ms Kinga Gál as a politician.
It is surely up to each and every one to decide freely and independently whether or not they agree with a person 's political beliefs.
The debate we are having here is about the last dictatorship in Europe.
And we are handing it over to one of the most brutal and bloodthirsty dictators on the European continent.
I find it absolutely unacceptable that a democratically elected member of my group should be compared to a bloodthirsty dictator.

I think Parliament must reject this, particularly coming from a representative of a party which is itself characterised by the fact that it has subjected the Roma minority in Hungary to hatred and persecution which is without parallel in Europe.
If there is one person who does not have the right to criticise Ms Kinga Gűnths, it is Ms Maurel.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, dear visitors, the cultural diversity of Europe is an important asset that we must preserve and continue to expand in the future.
In order for this to be possible, we must face up to the challenges of the digital age not only in the information and knowledge society, but also, above all, in the cultural and creative sectors.
Cultural diversity is not only an ideal value, but also a significant economic factor.
Around five million people in the EU work in the cultural sector.
This is approximately 2.5% of total employment.
The cultural sector is a growth sector, developing faster than many other sectors of the economy.

The development of information and communication technologies is driven forward by the content offered by the cultural sector just as the nature and quality of the content has changed and developed further through technology.
We therefore need to create a framework for the cultural and creative industries in which they can develop sustainably in the light of the challenges of the digital age.
We need a modern, accessible and secure system in which everyone can flourish equally.
Culture and the creative industries are a driving force for Europe.
That is why we must also focus on this area in the field of school and vocational education.
We must provide young people, and also in the lifelong learning offerings, with cultural and creative skills.
We also need to strengthen the entrepreneurial skills of cultural professionals, not only entrepreneurial skills but also good media skills in the area of new technologies.
We need a framework in which cultural creators can earn a living.

That is why we will have to deal intensively with the issues of copyright, social security and intellectual property, but also with open access to information and content, in order to ensure further development.
of the creative and cultural industries in a digital environment in the future, and to preserve Europe's cultural diversity and cultural heritage as an essential feature of the European Union.
Mr President, Mr Kelam, colleagues from the Baltic countries, ladies and gentlemen, today we are remembering the expulsions and mass deportations from the Baltic countries in a special way.
These expulsions are among the darkest events in European history.
Indeed, the last century was called, not least and not unjustifiably, the century of expulsions.
Today we are all bowing before the victims of these inhuman acts.
In this context, we demand a thorough investigation of Soviet history and, in particular, of the crimes of Stalinism.

Russia today should not see this as a political attack, but rather as support for coming to terms with its own history, which, just like the history of other European countries, is characterised by both bright and dark sides.
But just as we think of the victims from the Baltic States, we think of the Armenians who were driven out of Turkey at the beginning of the last century.
We are thinking of the so-called population exchange between Turkey and Greece.
The deportations in Europe reached a particular peak in the middle of the century under the Nazi regime and under the communist regime of the Soviet Union.
The expulsions after the Second World War also inflicted deep wounds, as did the expulsions during the Yugoslav wars.
However, we must not forget that many colonial regimes were characterised by expulsions.
We must demand a serious examination of the various forms of expulsion and the false justifications.
This also includes establishing guilt and punishing the guilty parties, wherever possible, as in the case of Mladić, for example.

However, the decisive answer is European unity, particularly through and with the independent Baltic States, and a clear commitment to continuing the European unification process, which is not yet complete.
This historic task is what we owe not only to ourselves, but also to the victims of the many expulsions in Europe.
Many politicians from all countries in Europe, from east and west, have worked on it.
Let us take the Gdańsk Declaration, for example, signed by the Polish President, Kwasniewski, and the German Federal President, Rau.
If we want to learn from the past, we must be vigilant, even against the first signs of expulsions, such as in the case of the Roma.

We must reject all statements such as the one that we should send the Arab immigrants back across the Mediterranean in boats, and we must defend ourselves if some want to undo the achievements of Europe without borders and return to a new narrow nationalism.

Let us bow down in reverence to all the victims of expulsion and deportation, but let us unanimously affirm our support for the continuation of European unification as the only real guarantee against a return to the horrors of war and genocide.
policy, which brought so much misfortune to the people of Europe and beyond in the last century. ' Never again' and 'Never forget' were demands that arose immediately after the Second World War.
They have not lost anything in terms of their validity and are best accommodated in a united Europe.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I had the opportunity to experience the independence referendum in Southern Sudan as an observer for Parliament, and I was impressed by the great support among the people of Southern Sudan for wanting to determine the future of their country for themselves in future.
I also saw the great expectations of the people who saw the independence of their country as liberation from slavery.

Thousands, perhaps tens of thousands, of so-called internally displaced persons, who had been driven from the south by decades of fighting into refugee camps in the north, set off for their former homes in southern Sudan.

Of course, in addition to the independence of their country, the people of South Sudan want above all to live in peace and to move towards a happier future, after all the decades of armed conflict in which millions of South Sudanese lost their lives.
On 9 July, the new state of South Sudan is due to become officially independent and expand the family of states in our world.
The starting conditions are unimaginably poor, and much has already been said about that.
Seventy per cent of the people live as nomadic herders, eighty per cent are illiterate, the conflict over Abyei is spreading into a military conflict.
There were already dead and wounded.

As if that were not enough, rivalries between the tribes and armed groups in South Sudan are also increasing, and according to estimates by observers, more than a thousand people have already lost their lives since January.
The number of wounded and raped is almost incalculable.
The Sudan was already one of the failed states.
There is a danger that the state of South Sudan will begin to look like a failed state.
I am firmly convinced that Europe has a great responsibility towards these regions.
In view of the colonial past of those countries, the common European foreign policy is called for here, together with the United Nations and the African Union, and it can prove itself here too.
It is not just about money, but also about supporting the fragile civil society that is emerging.

We must negotiate hard with those who hold the fate of the country in their hands, namely the tribal leaders and the leaders of the military groups, so that they do not disappoint the hopes of their people in the country.
Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I am very grateful to Mr Bartolozzi for taking the initiative to make Mongolia the subject of a debate in the European Parliament.
Whereas the former Soviet republics of Central Asia are now being discussed in Parliament from various perspectives, Mongolia and its situation have found only very limited echo here.

Mongolia, as a state of the former communist bloc, was very unjustly overlooked, as it was one of the first to take advantage of its independence and embark on a path towards democratisation of its society.

Mongolia adopted a democratic constitution in 1992 and since then several elections have taken place, which, according to observers, were generally democratic and fair, which is far from a given for the countries in this region.

The Mongolian Government has been trying for several years to combat corruption by law and in 2010 introduced a moratorium on the death penalty, something that even some large democratic states have not managed to do to date.
Nevertheless, the three million inhabitants of this vast country, dominated by extreme climatic conditions, are suffering from the consequences of a transformation process that is still ongoing.
a nomadic population is now moving into the few cities, which are not equipped to cope with this influx.
The country's mineral resources are not being extracted in an environmentally friendly way.
Energy problems are making civil and economic life more difficult.
Trapped between its two large neighbours, Russia and China, Mongolia is in danger of becoming even more dependent on these countries than it is already.

The efforts and achievements of Mongolia in democratising society should be acknowledged, and here I see the glass as half full and not half empty, as they are exemplary for this region and show what is possible despite a difficult past.
Mongolia should continue to receive our support, not only through aid programmes, but also through the European Union having a direct presence in the Mongolian capital, with its own

representation as a sign that the fate of Mongolia is not indifferent to us Europeans and that European interests will not continue to be perceived from a representation in Beijing. Just one more sentence, please.
I am very sorry that the Conference of Presidents was not prepared to give this issue additional emphasis by allowing a parliamentary resolution to be tabled.
But, Mr Posselt, of course I recognise that right.
I assume – and I hope – that you also recognise the right that the position of the Dutch Government is not announced ex cathedra, but is made available for discussion in a multinational democracy.
I have the utmost respect for Mr Rüttgers, who is in favour of a 2.7% increase in the EU budget.
I have taken note of that, and I am pleased that you have listened to me carefully, because in this Parliament the positions of all governments are discussed openly.
I did not quite understand the point of your question, but I think I heard you say that you also support democracy.
That is a step forward.

Firstly, I reacted so strongly because I am still annoyed that we have not managed to get a joint resolution on security policy through, because the absolutisation of one position was more important than unity.

Secondly, I said so at the time and I will say it again now: I have also learned from this and I now see that we need to do more, to apply more pressure and to show more initiative in the area of more safety requirements for European nuclear power plants.
That is precisely why I supported the joint motion for a resolution and why I am in favour of supporting the Commissioner and not permanently attacking him.

Ladies and gentlemen, on the intermediate category. I could certainly have imagined other solutions, Mr Pieper, for example, that the regions which currently have the highest funding and will no longer have it after 2013, should receive a transitional arrangement.
The discussion, however, showed that we need to take into account other regions, particularly those affected by the crisis.
We are currently talking about 51 regions.
And given the number of regions affected, it would be almost negligent not to set up an intermediate category.
Only in this way can a reliable framework be created for the responsible promotion of these regions, which must overcome their underdevelopment by their own efforts.
Otherwise, the Pieper report has many bright and many dark sides.

We criticise, among other things, the lack of a clear commitment to a much more determined fight against poverty, and we consider the wording in this document on linking cohesion policy with conditionalities that force the Member States to carry out reforms to be unclear and very open to interpretation.
In short, cohesion policy must remain an instrument of social balance and solidarity in all regions.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the interregional cooperation is becoming increasingly important in the future.
Cross-border cooperation and macro-regional cooperation are extremely important in an increasingly integrated Europe for achieving the 2020 goals.
Cross-border regions still face special challenges today.

Cross-border concepts and projects, such as in the areas of infrastructure, cooperation between enterprises, research collaboration, labour market development and further training, are a driving force for large parts of the European Union.
We need clear and simplified structures for the implementation of interregional projects.
Differing national requirements for the project partners often make cooperation and the implementation of tangible, sustainable projects difficult.
It is an important step that the Pieper report includes the demand to increase the share for interregional cooperation, Objective 3, to 7%, and I would like to strongly support this.
Madam President, Commissioner, colleagues, after almost three years of intensive work, after far more than 3 000 amendments, after countless talks and discussions,

Debates. After the most difficult technical negotiations and political trialogues, it is now up for a vote. The political package, the great compromise between the European Parliament and the Council of Ministers on the future labelling of foodstuffs.
This regulation is intended to give consumers more and better information on foodstuffs so that they can make a targeted purchasing decision.
But that is not all: the European food industry should also benefit.
Legal certainty, less red tape and better legislation are the goals.
This is particularly important for small and medium-sized enterprises, and it should be noted that the food sector in the European Union consists of SMEs to over 80%.
At the same time, this labelling must be applicable to all foodstuffs and all non-alcoholic drinks, from staple foods to highly processed ready meals.
That is why mandatory labelling in traffic light colours is not possible, because this labelling does not fit on staple foods.
Despite all the difficulties, despite all the political and ideological differences in Parliament, despite all the national selfishness of the Member States, the compromise now reached is impressive.
Allergens will be apparent at first glance.
The plant origin of plant oils is indicated.
There is a country of origin labelling for pork, poultry, sheep and goat meat.

For other products, an impact assessment will show whether and how origin can be indicated, for example for milk and milk products, single ingredients, i.e. products consisting of one main ingredient, meat in processed products,
products, etc. Imitations must be clearly labelled on the front, and the same applies to formed meat and formed fish, which are made up of small pieces glued together.
Packaging must not give the impression that it contains something that it does not.
If there is a picture of fruit on a yoghurt, then fruit must be in it, not just colouring and flavouring.
In addition, the most important nutrients must always be indicated per 100 grams or 100 millilitres for reasons of comparability.
Water added to meat must be indicated, as must the date of the first freezing of meat and fish, and many other details.
The most important thing, however, is that all information must be legible.
We have defined readability, we have fixed a minimum font size, and we have asked the Commission to lay down binding rules for other factors, such as the contrast between the font and the background.
At the same time, we are protecting the craft from excessive costs and bureaucracy.
Non-pre-packed fresh products must provide allergen information, but not the full nutritional information, which is costly to determine through analysis.
The Commission wanted this originally, but that would have destroyed our national and regional specialities, it would have led to a European homogenous mush, and that is why we prevented it.
But now we have all agreed, and the serious consumer protection groups are quite satisfied with what has been achieved.

I would therefore like to thank all those involved: the shadow rapporteurs, the Commission, and in particular Commissioner Dalli for his statements, which he will hopefully make shortly, and which will untie the knot that has formed in our political discourse.

I would like to thank in particular the Hungarian Presidency of the Council, which has achieved something that was hardly to be expected, something almost impossible, namely agreement on this regulation.
Mr President, Commissioner, President-in-office of the Council, allow me to join in thanking the rapporteur, Mrs Ayala Sender.
As has already been mentioned, the Council has blocked progress on this file since 2008, until a compromise was finally reached under the Belgian Presidency.
As I said, cross-border criminal prosecution in various road offences is to be made possible throughout Europe in future through improved data exchange between the Member States.
Our top priority must be to increase road safety and reduce the number of road deaths and injuries.

Together with the own-initiative report by Dieter Koch on road safety, which also makes a number of very concrete proposals, this directive can, through its repressive nature, also contribute to better compliance with these rules.
I do not need to go into the rules, as they have already been presented positively here.
The change in the legal basis from European transport policy to police cooperation has finally enabled the Council to draft a common position.
This means, however, that some countries such as the United Kingdom, Ireland and Denmark are excluded.
Consequently, not all European citizens are treated equally.
Citizens must be aware of the rights they have in terms of data protection.
We have tried to strengthen this aspect so that access to the data collected, its transmission and its storage are regulated as strictly as possible.
We were also very concerned about information.
Although the current text does not fully meet Parliament ’ s expectations, I am nevertheless optimistic that this is a first step in the right direction.
I hope that in the next few years we will be able to build on this basis in order to take a step towards a greater harmonisation of European traffic regulations.
The revision clause inserted by Parliament would definitely allow such an approach.
I am very sorry that this text has now become embroiled in the institutional conflict between the Council and Parliament over the correlation tables.
We agreed during the trialogues to find a compromise through the joint declaration on this subject.
I would just like to remind you very briefly that tomorrow the European Road Safety Charter will be launched.
Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, first of all, I would like to thank Mrs McGuinness for this report.

Thank you very much to the colleagues who have prepared the reports before, Mrs Merkies and Mrs Kovaliew. This is a follow-up, and the day is approaching when we will not only take political decisions.
but we also have to prove that we mean it when we say that research and innovation is the decisive issue for the future viability of the European Union.
I am therefore very grateful that the representative of the Committee on Budgets has also pointed out that good financial resources in this area are an important issue.

We will soon have to fight this battle to see whether we have the strength to provide the necessary financial resources so that this area of research and innovation can really become a central priority.
It is of central importance for the question of whether and how Europe will develop in the future.
Then, incidentally, small and large Sunday speeches will no longer be enough, but we will have to produce facts.
Secondly, we cannot avoid the fact, and I think this is very clearly brought out in the report, that we need to look very closely at whether we cannot use the resources we have more effectively.
How can we simplify the process?
How do we achieve speed?
How can we make research funding more effective?
How can we bring greater structure and greater simplification to the somewhat confusing maze of different research funding programmes?
Sometimes less is more: few projects but with a lot of money invested have a higher impact in the end.
I am therefore grateful that in this report, too, we have taken another step forward.

Of course, the European Research Council and the European Institute of Innovation and Technology will play a special role and of course the area of small and medium-sized enterprises is an important area, but last but not least,

We also need a solution for those countries that are not yet at the stage of excellence funding, which is the only principle that must remain binding and in future. We need an answer for those countries that are not yet there.
This report also raises the idea of possible competition, which could perhaps be used to promote new top research centres in neighbouring regions.
I think that this is an important task that still needs to be solved.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, in view of the current exceptional situation as a result of the financial, economic and budgetary crisis, decisions at European level should focus on pressing and essential issues.
Only in this way can we restore our citizens ’ confidence in the seriousness of our actions.
My report is about the strategy to avoid endless human suffering resulting from around 35 000 deaths and over 1,
1.5 million often serious injuries resulting from road traffic accidents, as well as to avoid the associated annual macroeconomic damage of EUR 130 billion.
The number of deaths alone is equivalent to the number of people who would have died in the crash of 250 medium-sized passenger aircraft, all of them fully occupied.
What wouldn't we do to prevent that?
The costs of accidents exceed the annual total budget of the European Union considerably.
I am therefore certain that it is in our interests to act sustainably in this area.
Thanks to the constructive cooperation of all shadow rapporteurs and many colleagues, we have managed for the first time to draw up a uniform, holistic and coherent approach to improving road safety.
It takes into account the contents of the UN Road Safety Decade 2010-2020 as well as the contents and objectives of the road safety work of the EU Member States and a broad range of civil society.
It contains, firstly, ambitious but realistic goals for the period up to 2020 and, with the vision of CEREM, for the period beyond that.
The aim is to reduce the number of people killed in accidents by 50% by 2020, the number of children killed by 60% and the number of seriously injured by 40%.
This is in the most fundamental interest of each and every one of our people, but also in the interest of our national economy.
We recommend that the Member States break down the objectives and methods to their territory and expect them to have their own road safety action programmes.
Secondly, it should state who is responsible for the individual activities and measures.
This is by no means just the European Union.

In the spirit of subsidiarity, the holistic approach means that the Member States, regional or local authorities, companies and even every individual road user must take responsibility in accordance with their duty to contribute.
Thirdly, it includes the 'how', that is, the instruments, activities and measures to be used to achieve the objectives.
Legislative proposals at European level are only one strategy.
We cannot and do not want to do without guidelines, recommendations and mandates.
Particular attention has been paid to subsidiarity, which means that responsibilities and accountability have been clearly defined, even where they are shared.
We appreciate the Commission's diverse activities.

However, as they come from the most varied Directorates-General, such as transport, the economy, consumer protection, the environment, communications, research and development, the internal market and others, they are not always consistent, often uncoordinated and sometimes contradictory.
We therefore propose the appointment of a coordinator for road safety within the European Commission.
This permanently avoids the creation of a road safety agency and greatly increases the efficiency of the European Commission.
The choice of measures and their evaluation both require a scientifically sound approach based on comparable, high-quality data, definitions and statistics.

We therefore call, among other things, for a harmonised analysis of the causes of infringements and accidents, as well as for the exchange of data throughout the EU, while maintaining a high level of personal data protection.
Six main areas for action were identified.
They concern people as road users, road traffic regulations, vehicles, infrastructures, their intelligent interconnection and the special consideration of vulnerable road users.
I ask for your support tomorrow.
Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, yes, we should focus on urgent and essential issues in road safety.
I am very convinced of this, and in my opinion, this primarily concerns the two biggest attention thieves in traffic, namely alcohol and excessive speed.
The European Union must also say something on this issue and not just propose technical measures, which, in my opinion, are good but far from sufficient.
The problem of inattentive drivers is particularly dramatic, especially in cities.
The speed must therefore be reduced.
I was very pleasantly surprised that there was such a broad majority in favour of recommending a 30 km/h speed limit.

We must pay particular attention to – and this is included, and I think it is important and good – putting vulnerable road users at the heart of our attention, and I hope that these measures will finally be implemented.
It seems pointless to me, but I would like to extend an invitation to you.
I have never seen him in the discussions on Rio or on other major world conferences, but I have always heard him speak here in plenary.
I really would like to ask you, Mr Pieper, to come to committee.
Get in touch with us, offer us your proposals, and then we will talk to you further about the oral questions.
and your contribution just now, you cannot do anything with that.
Madam President, Commissioner, thank you for your general comments.
However, these general remarks are not an answer to the questions that have been asked.
The questions are aimed at ensuring that we will reach results quickly.
The industries that we want to exempt from these additional burdens, or in which we want to establish fairer conditions, need planning security.
We therefore need decisions soon.
We need swift, reliable decisions and clear statements from the Commission.
This process has been dragging on for a very, very long time.

We all knew that this was a very complicated procedure that we were embarking on, but back then, in the compromise on the Emissions Trading Directive, we also said that we would do this with the Emissions Trading Directive under
the condition that the industries in Europe that are overburdened as a result can benefit from relief, because otherwise they will not be able to compete with other areas of the world.
I expect that what was the basis for business, the decision in Parliament, with the Council and with the Commission, will be implemented in that way.
I am a little worried that the circle of those affected is getting smaller and smaller, that we are becoming more and more petty in our approach, that we are excluding more and more areas

It is not just about steel, it is about aluminium, you know that, it is about many other areas, chemicals and so on, and that we simply do not implement what was the political basis of the decision back then.
Commissioner, you rightly pointed out that the situation for industry in Europe has not become any easier since this decision, but rather much more difficult.
We have a completely new situation. We have a much greater competitive situation.
We are all fighting for this, and in our Sunday speeches we say that industry is a very important area for the future development of Europe.
Yesterday, we heard some more clever speeches here.

I expect, and I think Parliament expects, that what was agreed back then will also be implemented now, and that it will be to the benefit of the industries, not the industries, but the people who work in these companies, the jobs, and thus also Europe's future prospects.
Do you know of any example that would actually justify the effort, that one spends many millions, that's billions, to develop these body scanners?
Are there any examples that prove that this has increased safety?
Mr President, I would like to begin by thanking the representative of the Council for his very clear description of the situation at the moment.
We have heard throughout this debate that it is a question of improving the situation for women in employment who have to decide whether or not to have a child.
In my opinion, the directive focuses too much on increasing maternity leave to 20 weeks.
But do we really believe that the decision to have a child can be reduced to 20 weeks of maternity leave?

As a mother of three children, I am well aware that comprehensive concepts and good offers for families are necessary in order to positively influence the decision to have a child and to establish equal opportunities for women.
The draft directive before us does not come close to meeting the needs of the very good childcare concepts and overall concepts in some Member States.
We need flexible solutions that take account of the situation in the Member States and in society and the resulting needs of families.
Maximum demands and wishful thinking will not lead to a solution here.
Therefore, we in Parliament must first of all develop a solid and realistic basis for negotiations.
The comprehensive protection of mothers and the well-being of children must be given the highest priority.
This can be done in different ways, using different models.
If you want to go through a brick wall with your head, you will not necessarily get to your destination any faster.
Lack of willingness to compromise does not open doors or build bridges.
A more moderate proposal would be a better solution and would provide greater protection for mothers, enabling us to move forward here.

I think we really need to look again at an approach based on the Commission's draft directive, with greater flexibility for different concepts in the individual Member States, in order to improve maternity protection for women and also protection for children in their first months of life.
That will not work with the draft that is before us today, and which certainly did not have the great majority that some of my colleagues have tried to suggest today.

I would like to see a little more flexibility and a little more realism here, and a little better recognition of what the Member States have already achieved today, without calling into question our fundamental demands.
Madam President, Commissioner, I would like to make a few comments on the tax agreement between Germany and Switzerland, as others have already done.
Instead of putting a stop to tax evasion, which would make sense, instead of plugging loopholes for tax evaders, we have produced a Swiss cheese with big holes.
The five-month transitional period allows tax evaders plenty of time to transfer funds from Switzerland to other tax havens.
Since foundations and trusts are not covered by the agreement, it is also sufficient to convert assets held in Switzerland into foundation assets.
The relevant forms are available from Swiss banks.
Some time ago, the German tax authorities were sent information about a number of tax defaulters who had hidden their assets in Switzerland.
The agreement now grants these people amnesty, and the tax inspectors are no longer allowed to use the information they have.
What kind of agreement is this that ties the hands of tax investigators?
In an advertising video, Swiss banks say that, as a result of this agreement, customers will benefit even more from the values of the Swiss financial centre in the future.

This agreement clearly lacks not only the necessary vote at EU level, but also runs counter to the EU's efforts to establish greater transparency in tax matters, to make tax collection more efficient in the Member States and, above all, to combat and prevent tax fraud.
Therefore, from our point of view, this cannot be acceptable.
this agreement, and we ask the EU to oppose it strongly if necessary.
Mr President, pursuant to the rules of procedure of our house and in agreement with the political groups, I am requesting that this work be sent back to the committees.
Madam President, I am pleased that we have succeeded in producing a sustainable and effective directive together with the report by Roberta Angelilli, and I would like to thank her for that.
This directive is a significant step in the fight against child abuse.
However, we must not lose sight of one thing: the vast majority of crimes are still committed within the children's closest social environment.
The perpetrators are found in families, schools and circles of acquaintances.
We are still at the beginning of our enlightenment work here.

That is why our children need to be made strong, strong enough to have the courage to confide in someone close to them in an emergency, but also strong enough to recognise dangers such as grooming when using the internet.
We must therefore jointly ensure that a sense of responsibility in dealing with the media plays a greater role in our children ’ s education.
Education is of the utmost importance here, as many teachers and parents have no idea what can be done with their children's computers or mobile phones.
I think there is a great deal of catching up to do.

It is therefore to be welcomed that this report also places great emphasis on prevention, as the authorities, but also schools and families, must do everything possible to prevent such abhorrent crimes from ever taking place.
For this reason, the EU needs better negotiating strategies with third countries such as the USA and Russia.

Pages stored outside the EU can only be deleted effectively and quickly if we also work closely with these countries to track down the perpetrators and send a clear signal together condemning sexual abuse of children.
Once the perpetrator has been caught, the legislature has often, unfortunately, been surprisingly lenient and has only suspended the punishment of serious crimes on probation.
The seriousness of the criminal offence should be taken into account more in future.
Overall, however, it is important that the public debate continues, as the subject has so many different dimensions.

Prevention, victim protection, technical development, as well as deletion, blocking, case law, investigation and intensive scientific documentation of the facts must continue to be advanced after tomorrow's vote.
We must not sit on our hands.
Rather, we must all take on the mandate to act, each and every one of us, and ensure prevention and awareness.
Mr President, for years, the volume of the EU budget has remained more or less the same.
The EU's tasks have constantly expanded and its financial needs have increased significantly.
The financial market supervision has created the necessary new institutions.
However, in order for these institutions to carry out their tasks effectively, they need to be well funded.
The economic weaknesses of some EU Member States have become obvious as a result of the financial and banking crisis.
An extensive economic recovery programme would be needed to strengthen the economies of these countries.
Some economic experts are even talking about the need for a Marshall Plan for the EU countries affected by the crisis.
The EU needs a growth-enhancing spending programme, not a growth-destroying austerity programme, one that guarantees work and income for workers and young people in the EU.
But what is the Council doing in this situation?
It shortens the Commission proposal, which is already far behind what is needed.

If, in the current political situation, an appropriate increase in the budget is not possible, Parliament and the Commission should at least do everything they can to prevent the cuts proposed by the Council.
...make it more difficult. Thank you very much, Mrs. Zimmer. Ladies and gentlemen, right now in the crisis, it becomes clear what kind of European Union we actually really want.
Do we want a union in which the glue that holds us together is the fact that we say that we do not care whether people have to live in poverty in any Member State of the European Union?
We have a shared responsibility to fight poverty and social exclusion.
Or do we opt for a Union such as the one just sketched out by the colleagues from the right-wing extremist group in this Parliament?

Sometimes I think that the attitude of the Member States and particularly of some of the governments of these Member States brings us dangerously close to this vision of a European Union as formulated by the far right.
We are not doing anything together, we are not setting up any joint instruments, we are not setting up any joint indicators, and we are using vague formulations wherever possible.
The report by Mr Daul makes this very clear in many areas, and I believe that we need to become much stronger in this respect.
What we need is an immediate end to the privatisation of public goods and social security systems.
We need minimum social and ecological standards.
With winter approaching, we cannot allow people to end up on the streets, to be evicted and ultimately freeze to death because of rising energy prices.
We have a shared responsibility here, the responsibility of the European Union.
We must stand up for that, not for this nationalist drivel that we have heard from the right.
What would we have advised our colleagues in 1931 or 1938, when there was a depression, when the financial markets had already undermined the democracies and when we were heading for wars?
I think that two things are necessary, two emergency measures: we need something like an interim government.
Do what you have to do quickly, do not let yourselves be held back, regulate every product, every institution and every player, as Mrs.
Merkel said. But a transitional government that alone enforces the financial emergency program cannot have a future if there is no democratic emergency program.
In this case, that means that of course we need a European community, of course we need other treaties, of course we need a system that works, but that only becomes legitimate if there are referenda.

We must already now campaign for this, because the situation has become so dramatic that a major rethink is required before the right-wingers, before the national separators, gain the upper hand again over something that was already built up.
Madam President, there is no doubt that this august House is in favour of transparency and openness towards citizens.
My EPP Group does this too, of course.
However, the report before us goes far beyond that objective.
Existing legislation and interinstitutional agreements are being ignored, and the protection of competition procedures, sensitive and personal data is being disregarded.
This seriously violates data protection, which we always champion so strongly elsewhere.
Are we now to hand over people ’ s private lives to the world public?
That is precisely the danger with this report, because administrative procedures and decisions, which include personnel issues for example, are to be published.
The same applies to the protection of trade secrets and sensitive information in the context of authorisation procedures, court proceedings and competition procedures.

With its vague, unclear and far too broad definitions, the report allows public access to such documents. After all, a document is defined as any data and content that is in any way related to the policies, measures and decisions of all EU bodies and institutions.
This includes, in large part, preparatory, confidential and secret documents, as well as all information related to them.
And so this definition includes not only documents but also information.
Information from ongoing interinstitutional procedures, such as from informal trialogue negotiations, should also be published.
Do you really know what that means, Mr Rapkay?

It means public access to the procedure. It means public influence on the decision-making procedure. Then we might as well just sit the lobbyists down at the table and let them negotiate. Is that what the citizen wants?
I can't imagine it.
In addition, documents relating to non-legislative procedures, internal administrative measures, etc. are to be published. Where is the public interest in this?
Or would it not rather hinder our work?
I do not think that this report is in the interests of our citizens.
It would lead to a flood of useless information.
It would make our work impossible and, above all, as you heard from the Commissioner, it is unacceptable to the other institutions because it violates existing rules and laws.
You know that, Mr Rapkay, and basically you are working against greater transparency and against the citizen.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, with such an important and comprehensive subject as the white paper on the future of transport, there is always a risk that you end up with a Christmas tree, where everyone is trying to fit in their two or three favourite topics.
If you look at the result that is up for a vote tomorrow, you can certainly congratulate our rapporteur, Mathieu Grosch.

He has managed to work out a good, coherent and manageable result from several hundred amendments, which focuses on safety, environmental protection, energy and technology as the main priorities for future transport.
I expressly welcome the fact that important issues such as the social, health and employment conditions of employees are appropriately mentioned here.

This is particularly important in view of the impending shortage of skilled workers in the transport sector, against which we must take active measures if we are to prevent future bottlenecks in the supply of well-trained skilled workers.
Further opening up cabotage in road transport only on condition that social dumping and unfair conditions are prevented.
I also welcome the fact that the aspect of improving road safety is given an important place here.
Road safety is an issue that must continue to concern us in the future.
Other points I see more critically.
I would have liked to have heard something about model projects where more traffic is transferred from the road to the railways and waterways.
For this reason, I do not think that the discussion on the jumbo jet is the best solution for greater sustainability.
Likewise, we should wait and reconsider the further liberalisation of the rail sector and the opening up of national passenger transport.
There are still technical harmonisations and fair competition conditions to be resolved.

But as I said, in this report, I hope that the expectations that Parliament is expressing here do not remain a pious wish list, but that a goal will be achieved by 2020 and that our proposals will have become reality.
Ladies and gentlemen, it is just 20 years since the Central Asian States gained their independence as a result of the collapse of the Soviet Union.
Coming from a dictatorship, the establishment of democratic and state of law structures in these countries will remain a challenge for a long time.
Nevertheless, these countries are important trading partners for the EU and its Member States due to their wealth of natural resources and raw materials.
It is therefore only logical to regulate the EU ’ s relations with these states within the framework of partnership and cooperation agreements.
Partnership and cooperation agreements are intended to promote cooperation and lead to partnership in action, but not to one-sided stigmatisation.
Child labour and especially child forced labour is unfortunately still a widespread evil in many countries of the world that must be combated.

However, I would point out that in many agricultural regions of Europe, it was common practice to extend children's school holidays during the harvest season and to allow children from farming families to help with the harvest, which today nobody would describe as forced labour.
However, if there are signs of child labour in the cotton harvest in Uzbekistan, consequences must be drawn.

The same consistency must then apply to all other countries where child labour still exists, including those countries where we pursue economic interests of a completely different order of magnitude, such as India or China.
We should not apply double standards here.
Mr President, President-in-Office of the Council, President of the Commission, ladies and gentlemen, I can only echo the congratulations that have been expressed to the Presidency of the Council.
In difficult times, you were not only an honest broker, but also a European broker.
I think that was very pleasant to feel in these difficult times, when we have in a number of Member States political trends suggesting to people that you could achieve more on your own than together.
You have put the common factor at the heart of the matter.
It was also very refreshing to see that you have found support among the people of Poland for this clear European agenda, even in domestic political debates.
It was not easy in this context.
I have the greatest respect for what you have done.
In this six-month period, we have managed to get quite a lot on the way in the area of financial market regulation.

If I may just single out one area that I myself was able to negotiate, namely the issue of regulation on short-selling and credit default swaps on sovereign debt, where the Commission did not dare to propose a strict regulation, where we were told by all

Member States were told, you will not get your way, and where, thanks to the Polish Presidency of the Council, it has been possible to sanction a highly speculative instrument and where it is necessary to ban it, so that we can contribute to greater stability in the financial markets.
I would like to thank your Finance Minister and your Secretary of State, who have brought this about in a painstaking process of discussion between Parliament and the Council.
This is an example of what we can do together in the interests of safety and stability in Europe.
Thank you very much for that.
Ladies and gentlemen, the new surveillance mechanism for economic imbalances has been in force since yesterday.
Now, the specific, substantive regulations are also available.
Which developments does the Commission now consider to be dangerous, and which economic problems does it want corrected?
The Commission considers wage unit cost growth of more than 3% per year in euro area countries to be a problem.
However, there is no limit on falling wages.
Cheap, dear Commission, does not necessarily mean good.
It is not enough to urge the Member States to achieve prosperity at the expense of their trading partners.
Without investment in education, health and energy efficiency, we can exploit our workers to the bone, but we will not write any economic success stories.
Another example from the scoreboard: the loss of export market shares is to be reviewed and sanctioned.
That is nonsense.
With increasing world trade, Europe's share will inevitably decline statistically.
The indicator consequently penalises those countries which are already integrated into world trade.
We therefore believe that the Commission should withdraw this scoreboard and subject it to a general overhaul.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to begin by joining in thanking our rapporteur and all those who have worked on the report, because it is not routine, even when you are dealing with competition policy for the fortieth time on the basis of a report from the Commission.

I should like to thank the Commissioner very much, because 2010 was the year in which you were fully responsible for the first time for competition policy, and for the very comprehensive presentation.
I would like to make a request, and you have just mentioned it in your speech, that we should make some progress in the area of public aid in the banking sector.
should come, because now, due to the new stress tests by the European Banking Authority, a new capitalisation in the banking sector is being called for.
I do not believe that this will contribute to the stability of the banking system if, on the one hand, we still have a large number of unanswered or unresolved legacy issues
and new cases are already arising as a result of the pressure to increase the capital ratio, particularly in the private banking sector.
