So I really urge you, Commissioner, to come up with a quick solution that also contributes to stabilising the banking sector so that it can meet the challenges in the international environment.
The second major issue, control of mergers, will also continue to be a major issue for us in the coming years.
Here, too, I can only urge that the Commission continue on the clear and transparent path it has so far taken in merger control.

I believe that it is in the interests of all those involved, particularly consumers, that we ensure in the area of merger control that competition is possible even in larger or different structures. I do not want to judge that.
In that sense, thank you to everyone who has worked on this, and I hope, Commissioner, that we will soon reach a solution in the area of banks.

Commissioner, I think you have just hit the nail on the head by saying that we can all agree that we are in a position to start a third industrial revolution in Europe if we understand that we can see sustainability as an industrial opportunity.
You also mentioned resources, and that brings me to a thought that I have totally forgotten today.
I forgot the human resources in my office.
Mrs Phillips, please allow me to say that you have worked like a trojan horse. Your achievements are wonderful. You have been a leading figure in this matter.
I would like to thank you for that.
Mr President, in order to have freedom of speech in Parliament, we also need a microphone system that works.
The button to switch on my microphone has broken.
I would ask a technician to restore my freedom of speech.
Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I am surprised that we have to be reminded again and again of what is actually meant by structural and cohesion policy.

Cohesion and structural policy is one of the European Union's central policy areas and is designed to strengthen economic and social cohesion within the Union and to promote growth and employment in underdeveloped regions in particular.
The necessity and success of cohesion and structural policy have been proven precisely in the years of crisis.
In many regions, the situation would be much more difficult in terms of economic development if these funds were not available.
The implementation of the Structural Fund support is based on operational programmes, as has already been mentioned, which describe the support strategy of the respective region.
The European Union, the Member States and the regions are working together in partnership here, thus creating real European added value.
The success of cohesion and structural policy also depends on the reliability of the programmes and the funds made available and still available.

That is why I am very surprised when, time and again, Member States, but also the Commission, as we are now seeing, try to use Structural Fund money as a piggy bank for all sorts of current policy initiatives.

This does not do justice to the purpose and intent of cohesion policy and, furthermore, it undermines the confidence of the players in the regions who are already working towards achieving the objectives on the basis of long-term strategies and agreements.
Certainly, combating youth unemployment and promoting economic growth are important goals.

This will only be achieved, however, if the partnership-based cooperation between the regions, the Member States and the Commission is maintained and the stakeholders in the regions are involved and remain involved.
Only they have the knowledge of what needs to be done in their regions.
Cohesion and structural policy is the unifying, partnership-based, solidarity-based bond between the various levels of policy in the European Union.
It makes the Europe of solidarity visible on the ground in the regions, and it should not be abandoned lightly on the basis of political quick fixes.

Mr President, I support the compromise reached in the trialogue, which is intended to strengthen the negotiating power of dairy farmers vis-à-vis the processing industry, although I know that it does not solve all the problems that dairy farmers are currently facing in some Member States.
Fifty years ago, Commissioner Monnet already provided for preferential measures under the CAP for the creation of producer groups.
In my country, Luxembourg, dairy farming is the mainstay of our agriculture, due to the high proportion of grassland.
Our farmers have long understood that the best solution is to come together in a cooperative which takes on the processing of the milk itself.
Five Member States voted against the compromise in the Council for precisely this reason, as it fails to live up to its intended purpose as a soft landing instrument.

The European Commission has the right of initiative as regards legislative proposals, and I would therefore like to call on it to submit a legislative proposal to the Council and to the European Parliament before the end of 2012 to correct this obvious and costly error in the system.

A linear reduction in the super levy, which is charged on every kilo of milk delivered, until 2015, or the abolition of the fat correction could at least mitigate the negative effects of the expiring rules without harming the Member States that are not affected.
The milk package before us is also intended as the final report of the High Level Group, which is supposed to bring about a soft landing for the end of milk quotas.
It is astonishing that this report makes no mention of milk quotas at any point.
The young and ambitious farmers in Luxembourg and in four other Member States who are trying to prepare themselves for the liberalisation of the milk market and who want to expand their production
want to be able to compete on the world market after 2015, they can only do so by buying up more quotas now, just before the end of the milk quota system.
This is an enormous additional and unnecessary capital requirement, which puts them at a disadvantage compared to their colleagues in the 22 Member States.
So, please, Ms. Blunk. Question: Are you aware that we already have community bonds?
The resolution does not talk in general terms about Eurobonds, but rather about very different options.
Do you know that not all of these options require a treaty change?
I find it questionable that you have again made general statements about Eurobonds.
We should not give up the differentiation that we have achieved here with Mrs Goulard ’ s good proposal.
I would like to hear a little more differentiation in your statements and I am curious to hear what you have to say about that.
Yes, I'll just say very briefly, dear colleague Gullar, this is not an evaluation that I have made here.
I would just like to point out that the last Italian government bond before Italy entered the euro was yielding 10%.
There was no outcry in Italy and nobody said that other countries should take them over.
It was taken for granted that high interest rates were paid.

And now, suddenly, because we have had low interest rates for ten years and have become massively indebted, because money has become cheap, for investments as well as for the public purse, we want to prolong this via Eurobonds.
This is a trap from which we cannot escape, and I stand by that.
Mr President, Commissioner, your answer really disappointed me to some extent.

They obviously want to stick with the old system, which means that complicated negotiations have to be conducted with each country, and that in turn means that no further access to literature will be granted, so that no more opportunities will be created for the blind and visually impaired.
This is all the more regrettable because the European Union is now blocking progress in WIPO, whereas many other contracting parties are ready to move forward.
This is a huge missed opportunity.

You are not fighting for more rights for copyright holders, but you are fighting against people whom the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities is supposed to guarantee equal access to cultural goods and to enable to participate fully in these cultural goods.
I appeal to you: please dismantle barriers and agree to a binding treaty.

Ladies and gentlemen, the debate here in this House shows that we actually represent the whole spectrum. On the one hand, there are those who do not want any restrictions, and on the other hand, there are those who equate any form of immigration with the danger of mass immigration.
These are the two extremes within this House.
I think we need to look at family reunification with a great deal of common sense and rationality.
On the one hand, there are indeed problems in Member States where overly restrictive measures are being taken.
Family reunification cannot be burdened with so many obstacles that in the end no one can be reunited.
On the other hand, it is entirely legitimate for Member States to require language skills, to require integration and to require a willingness to integrate within a state.
Secondly, the Member States have the right, and we should not brush these problems aside, to formulate the problems that arise in connection with family reunification.

If countries such as Greece or Malta or also Romania or Italy are no longer allowed to state the problems they have, some of which are related to this, then I do not know whether we are democratic if we simply ignore problems.
I do not think that we should have a new directive right now.
I think we should carefully discuss the problems and then think about how we can solve them.
Constantly producing new directives does not lead to better directives or better implementation of them.
We currently have some implementation problems.

However, I fully understand that we must first deal with the fact that we should not be rushing into another new directive and adding something to it before we have even properly discussed the problems of the old directive.
I am in favour of listening to the Member States and at the same time respecting the fundamental right to family reunification.
If we do this with common sense and reason, then I believe we will get on the right track.
Mr President, 'Pomyśl zanim pomyślisz' - 'Think before you think' - urges the Polish aphorist Stanisław Jerzy Lec, and this advice would do us good in this Parliament from time to time.
A few weeks after the resolution on the Central Asia strategy adopted in this House, in which the Central Asian states were given warnings, we are now seeing strikes in Kazakhstan.
in Kazakhstan, without waiting for the results of the investigations still under way, which are being carried out with international participation.
While the dead are to be pitied, and the injured and arrested as a result of these events have the right to have the events investigated, the background remains unclear.

Even Vladimir Kozlov, Chairman of the unregistered opposition party Alga, said, and I quote, 'That was a group of other people. Unrest is not in the interest of the strikers, because it only gives grounds for using force against them'.
A Central Asia expert, Arkady Dubnov, said, and I quote: 'It cannot be ruled out that some of the Kazakh opposition living abroad will also get involved'.
On the other hand, however, it might suit someone in the Kazakh capital, Astana, very well to keep tensions going.
After all, Kazakhstan ’ s power elites are already fighting over the succession to President Nursultan Nazarbayev.
We had the opportunity to speak with the Kazakh opposition in the presence of the Kazakh ambassador here in Parliament.

The UN Special Representative, Miroslav Jenca, said today that he was impressed by the Kazakh Government ’ s attention to the events in Zhanaozen and that it was necessary to uncover the true background to the unrest.
We should take the Kazakh authorities at their word and judge them by their actions, but we should not jump to reflexive pre-judgements.
If I understood you correctly, the statement you made today is, yes, the Commission is fighting for the continuation of the food programme for the most deprived people in the European Union.
It will do everything to put forward specific proposals in the foreseeable future on (a) where the programme will be based and (b) how it will be financed.
I think this is urgently necessary, and I think we have a joint responsibility to ensure that this food programme actually becomes a reality.
not because I think it is the final solution, but because I think it sends a signal to people that we still have a basic sense of the solidarity that is needed with those

the living conditions of people in the European Union who, unfortunately, were not able to take part in development and who have become the first and greatest victims of the way in which the European Union has fought the crisis.
I am thinking of Greece, I am thinking of Spain, I am thinking of other countries where exactly what we are doing as the European Union is to the detriment of the poorest of the poor.
We are sending out a signal, but unfortunately that is all.
I therefore think that it is urgently necessary that we not only look at the 18 million people in the European Union who are currently dependent on food aid,
but that we must make the fight against poverty our top priority.
We have not sent that signal so far.

But in my view, that is the crucial point if we want to make further progress here and if we want to win the confidence of the citizens of our European Union that we are really creating a social and solidarity-based Union.
In that sense, you have our support.
Ladies and gentlemen, we ourselves are also called upon to do something to ensure that the governments that are currently blocking have to give up this position.
I think that is also a form of self-commitment for us.
Honourable Mr Daul, I have listened to you carefully.
What you are proposing, alongside the subsidiarity test, is a growth test for all expenditure, which means narrowing the objectives to growth.
I would like to know how you reconcile this with the claim of a Christian Democratic group, which surely has goals beyond growth.
It should be common knowledge that the economy does not give birth to children, does not raise them, and cannot provide them with a life in an intact environment.
This therefore speaks in favour of the priorities being changed by the Commission and also by the Council, of them being reconsidered.
Our group will examine very closely whether what you have presented to us this morning really does represent a change of priorities.
Until now, the motto has been: first the economy, which needs functioning financial markets.
The initial priority was to stabilise public finances, i.e. to reduce debt and calm the markets.
Mr Daul, who is unfortunately not here, I just wanted to say to him that I would like to keep it exactly as Georges Marchais did.
I did not understand his answer, but I disagree with him.
I think that the measures that have been introduced were wrong.
They were not right. It was not right to focus exclusively on economic recovery and to ignore what was happening to people.
It was wrong to put the stability of the euro above everything else and to say that we do not care at all what happens to the people.
It must be the other way round: first we must look after people, and then we can go on to talk about Euro stabilisation.

The speculative attacks on Spain have driven up the interest rates on Spanish government bonds to over 6% and more, and thus close to the interest rate level at which other countries such as Greece, Portugal or Ireland have taken up the support of the rescue fund.
The austerity policy imposed on the Member States by the EU, the ECB and the IMF, with drastic cuts in public expenditure of all kinds, from
cutting wages and pensions, raising value added taxes and so on, have simply imposed burdens on the citizens and have not prevented the spiral of austerity.
Rigorous cuts in state expenditure worsen the downturn and drive up the debt ratio of states, and that must be our common conclusion.
When Mr Draghi recently said that the European social model is at an end, I was pleased, Mr Barroso, when you said, ""No, we in the Commission do not think so."
But I also expect you to go to Greece, to send your people to Greece, to Portugal and to Ireland, and to keep a close watch on what is happening there.
What is going to happen to the people who are currently struggling to survive?
What happens or what do we do together to finally break this spiral of self-destruction that has started, particularly in the debt-ridden countries most affected?
We cannot just stand by and do nothing.
We will not benefit from any social programmes that will somehow work if other measures have been taken.
We need to act now. Where are your objectives from the 2020 strategy on combating poverty?
What has happened to the European Platform for Poverty?
It has been put aside.
That was before the crisis.
However, at the heart of the crisis, people need help, they need support and they need the European Union to act.
They do not need empty promises.
We need specific ideas about what should be done now.
If you do that, you will get the support of my group.
Support all those within the Commission who have clearly recognised the signs of the times and want to change things.
But please do not just carry on as before and say, ""It will be all right."
It will be no use at all if things continue like this. I call on you to come up with a specific project, a specific programme, so that we can now really help the people in the countries affected.
Do something about it.
We will have to do this in our countries so that the money for the ESF for 2013 or 2014 to 2020, or for the other programmes, is not blocked or cut.
the Member States must meet their responsibilities.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the current state of public finances and the economy in Europe does not allow for a tax policy fairy tale.
The situation in the Council is quite simply that little progress is being made on tax policy proposals.
Why?
because some countries are against further tax cooperation.
They benefit from aggressive tax planning; they are tax havens themselves.
The Treaties clearly state that unanimity is required on tax issues.
That is why the European Commission has repeatedly failed in the past with its proposals.

They are being watered down, they are not moving forward, as with the interest rate directive, and in the case of more recent proposals, nothing ambitious at all has been proposed in advance, out of a sense of obedience.

I therefore ask you, Mrs Schmitt, and I know that your job is not an easy one, to change your strategy in this respect, to put forward tough measures and then to rely on enhanced cooperation in order to end the deadlock in the Council.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the proposal before us will certainly not prevent state bankruptcy where it should occur.
However, doing something is still better than doing nothing.
Extraordinary situations sometimes require extraordinary measures.

The budgetary and debt crisis in some countries has led to such an exceptional situation that important projects and measures under cohesion and structural policy can no longer be carried out, because the banking sector is no longer available as a financier and private investors are no longer available.
I see an exceptional measure in the proposal before us to create risk-sharing instruments to provide further funding for projects in structural policy.
Funds from cohesion and structural policy, which are available to countries wishing to use these instruments of division, can be used to provide financial security for projects already under way.

I am very grateful to the rapporteur, Ms Hübner, for having contributed greatly to clarifying the Commission proposal and for having once again clearly stated when and how these instruments should be used.
However, I also believe that these risk-sharing instruments should only be due to the current situation and cannot be a precedent for the future funding period.
Mr President, when the amendment to the 2009 biometric passport regulation called for a review after three years and for four studies to be submitted, our group supported that.
in particular because it has repeatedly expressed serious reservations on data protection grounds and also because of the overestimation of the usefulness of biometric data in travel documents.
The belief in perfect identification, the overestimation of this tool and, as a result, the underestimation of other links in the security chain are really problematic.
The handling of biometric data is still subject to this major misconception, namely the assumption that they are immutable and most trustworthy.
That is not the case at all.
These data can also change over the course of a lifetime.
In children, they look different to how they do in old age.
That is why reliable and serious studies and conclusions from them are necessary.
Counterfeiting is still being carried out diligently.
We have now been able to establish this.
As events in the United Kingdom and in other countries have repeatedly shown, these chips are not safe from abuse at all.
They are happily snapped and can cause lasting damage to those affected.
The standards within the EU are also extremely varied.
The error rate is very high, as in France and the Netherlands.
We have to keep asking ourselves what biometric data are good for if documents are being forged and are in circulation.
So if one link in the chain is falsified, if the source documents are inconsistent, biometric data will not help us further.
The Chaos Computer Club in Germany took only a few minutes to demonstrate a forgery.

The Chaos Computer Club friends needed the lid of a plastic bottle, superglue, a digital camera and some wood glue. In a few minutes, they had cracked the chip. That is enough about the supposed security.
We expect the Commission to submit the studies and, of course, the results and consequences that follow from them.
Mr President, the Council and the Commission are not represented here today to the extent that I would have liked, out of respect for Parliament.

As a Member from a region that is heavily affected by the planned changes, namely the Eifel, I have to ask myself, after following the debate with attention, whether the unfortunately rather inadequate attempt
a harmonisation of the criteria for disadvantaged areas really meets the needs of these regions, especially as the necessary information is not available for a meaningful and responsible decision.
Above all, I wonder whether an adequate impact assessment has really been carried out.
In order to do justice to farmers, we need not only biophysical criteria but also socio-economic criteria.

I therefore call on the Commission to reconsider this important aspect, with the necessary time, independently of the overall package of CAP reform and, above all, after the necessary documents have been submitted and the effects have been carefully analysed, with regard to the criteria.

I believe that we need to work with the Asylum Support Office and the Greek authorities to identify more quickly which of the people who have come to Greece could actually be eligible for international protection.

also be transferred to other countries in the European Union, because firstly they definitely can never return to their home country and secondly, they may have potential and qualifications which could be useful for all of us.
However, essentially, we also need to help the Greeks to register and identify people more quickly, so that aid processes can move forward more quickly, but also
those who have neither the qualifications nor the right to stay in Greece or the European Union, because they would actually have a chance to stay in their own home countries.
Mr President, Minister, Commissioner, I would like to thank the rapporteur for her always very committed and exemplary work.
I share many of the criticisms expressed by Sophie in 't Veld, and I support them, or have supported them in part.
However, there always comes a point at which one has to take responsibility.
For the EPP Group, or for myself, I therefore claim that we must now deal with realities and cannot retreat into ideological or theoretical positions.
The fact is that the United States has been collecting these PNR data for ten years and there has not been a single complaint.
The fact is that we do not want to stop data traffic, even if we are opposed to it.
You have just explained that.
It is also a fact that we do not want air traffic between Europe and the United States to stop.
It is also a fact that the United States has a right to know who is coming into their country.
It is also a fact, in my opinion, that the legal services have all told us in oral statements that this agreement is better than the other agreement, better than the alternatives.
Therefore, we must also take responsibility here.
That is why we were elected.
Responsibility for citizens who would otherwise be left to fend for themselves with less protection and fewer rights.
responsibility for the airlines, which otherwise – and the Commissioner also mentioned this – would be left in a state of absolute legal uncertainty.
responsibility for the EU as a contracting party in matters of data protection and security, and also responsibility for a balanced relationship between security and data protection.
Therefore, all in all, and taking into account the fact that this agreement is not perfect, we are in favour of giving our approval to this agreement.
If you want to better protect citizens and if you want to give citizens better data protection, then you take on this responsibility here and vote in favour of this agreement. Thank you.
Mr President, Commissioner, thank you for your very informative remarks.
Thank you very much.

Numbers sometimes say more than big words, and to date, the European Patent Office has granted almost 2 000 patents on plants and almost 1 200 patents on animals, which have been created with or without the use of genetic engineering.
I should like to mention two outstanding examples: the granting of patents on genetically modified and selectively bred barley, which applies to the entire chain from seed to plant.
Our farmers are affected, as are our brewers, our producers and also we consumers.
In my opinion, this example shows the devastating extent of patenting.
Second example: the so-called long-life tomato, not to be confused with the long-life oil that you have in the engines of your diesel cars.
This patent was obtained by circumventing valid EU rules by using this trick of the US company Arcadia applying for it.
It is not the tomato that has been patented, but its genetic material, specifically selected enzymes.

This shows what tricks are being used, even though, as you said, Commissioner, in the 1998 Biopatent Directive, Article 4 clearly states that plant varieties and animal breeds, and essentially biological methods for breeding plants or animals, are not patentable.
We believe that this definition must be clarified and interpreted more precisely.
Technical processes used to support conventional breeding must not lead to patentability as a whole.
We say specifically: no patent on life.
We need a clear signal here for more ethics and more honesty in our dealings with creation, and once again, against the patenting of life throughout Europe.
Therefore, Commissioner, we call on the Commission to state clearly that patents on breeding material and genetic resources are and will remain prohibited in Europe.
Secondly, we are calling for a clear position on Article 4 of the Biopatent Directive: no patents on life.
Thirdly, Commissioner, on behalf of many farmers and breeders in Europe, we are calling for the breeders' and farmers' privilege to be clearly enshrined in all legislation. Thank you.
Thank you, Madam President. I am taking the floor to speak on the discharge of Europol and Eurojust.
As for Europol, there is little, if anything, to complain about.
The annual financial statement reflects the financial situation in 2010 in all essential aspects, and all underlying transactions are in accordance with the rules and therefore also lawful.
Only the high transfers in relation to the actually low total costs incurred are noticeable.
Europol is putting this off until it moves into a new headquarters. That is, of course, understandable.
But Europol agrees to take further measures in the future to avoid such high transfers.
Furthermore, Europol will commit itself to monitoring the implementation of the budget on a monthly basis from 2012.
I recommend that we follow up on this to see whether it actually happens.
Against this background, I recommend granting discharge to Europol.
to Eurojust. Here, the annual financial statement reflects the financial situation in 2010 in all essential aspects.
All transactions are also legitimate and in accordance with regulations here.
But here, too, high operating costs were transferred to 2011.
After the creation of Eurojust, this was mainly due to an organisational structure project, a new computer infrastructure and costs related to data processing.
It is announced that everything will be improved in the future, and that procurement requirements will be better observed.
I recommend that we also review this in the future and, above all, check whether all vacant positions will actually be filled in the future.
That has been improved.
But if these positions are not filled soon, we would have to consider cutting the budget, and that would be negative for Auris.
I still recommend approval. Thank you.
Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, what must worry us and fill us with panic is not the sheer number of young unemployed people in Europe alone, but the development
The International Labour Organisation has found that the number of young unemployed people increased by 26.5% between 2008 and 2011.
It is this development that urgently requires action. We are not only cutting off an entire generation from the labour market, we are cutting off an entire generation from a future worth living.
If we give young people the feeling that they are absolutely useless, we should not be surprised if they turn away from this EU, because they have no reasonable job or life prospects.

Commissioner, I urgently ask you to ensure that decisive counter-measures are introduced. I can already see some of them, but please ensure that the long-standing intellectual calm does not now turn into operational panic.

When I read that the Commission is launching a programme whereby young people who apply for jobs abroad are to be financially supported, I feel that we are putting on a travelling circus that does not solve the problem.
On the contrary, it will be counterproductive, because then technicians, doctors, engineers and nursing staff will be poached by the richer countries that are not in this crisis.
What is needed now, in this crisis, which is the cause of this development, are offensive measures, namely the dismantling of this obsession with saving, which is holding back the economy in Europe.
We need a targeted growth policy, we need to strengthen domestic purchasing power and public investment in education, research and alternative energies.
That would be the way.
With the combination of European funding, it would then be possible to develop an integrated concept that actually provides young people with a future.
One last comment: this youth unemployment also does not go hand in hand with the nonsense of wanting to raise the retirement age to 70.
We need a different policy there, one that gives young people access to the labour market.
That is what I am urgently asking for.
Madam, you have just said that anything that would speak in favour of the financial transaction tax would be speculation, and you yourself have then come up with very speculative examples.

I would like to know what you think of the Commission study, in the light of what you have said, which very clearly states that we have previously, inadvertently, included too little of the real economy in our dependence on

the financial markets, that is, 95% of companies are by no means dependent on it for raising money, and that one can very well turn the negative growth effect that we have been talking about so far into a positive one by making appropriate growth-promoting public investments.
Is that just speculation for you or would you say that such a well-founded, wide-ranging study by the Commission has its justification and is a strong argument?

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the financial transaction tax is certainly not identical with the Tobin tax, which has been talked about and discussed for many years, but I think it incorporates essential elements of the Tobin tax.
It at least takes up the idea of involving the financial markets in taxation, of taxing them and, above all, involving them in the costs of the crisis.
I think this sends out a very important signal to the financial markets.
But it is also a very important signal to our citizens, to our voters.

There have been a whole series of actions, petitions and written initiatives addressed to politicians in a whole series of Member States calling for a financial transaction tax finally to be introduced at European level.
I think that Parliament has achieved a great success with the majority that is emerging here in favour of this report and this tax.
Certainly, it is not sufficient to stabilise and limit the financial markets as might be necessary, but I think it is a really important step that has been taken here.
I would like to appeal to the Council to take the voices of the citizens and voters seriously and to work towards
that it is actually introduced, and that it is not blocked in the Council due to the British position or any other position.
I think it is clear that the citizens of the European Union want this tax, and I think it is a great responsibility

It is now up to the Council to become active, and I would like to use my speaking time to appeal to the Council to do everything possible to ensure that this tax is actually introduced and becomes a reality.
Ladies and gentlemen, I think that, despite all the detailed problems that we can discuss and that we will have to discuss further in the legislative process, we should agree on one thing:
Financial markets cannot function on the basis of the model where, when everything goes well, the banks reap the profits and, when something goes wrong, the taxpayers have to take the blame.
It therefore makes sense to subject financial transactions to taxation as well.
It makes sense to do this across borders.

In Europe, or at least in the euro area, we will not have distortions, but will make a lasting contribution to creating a stable financial centre that generates what financial centres are actually intended for, namely that companies

access to capital, that savers have the opportunity to save something or to set something aside for their old age, and that financial markets thereby fulfil once again the function for which they were created, namely to provide added value for the real economy.
All the things that drove us into the financial crisis were products that did not create any value for the real economy.
That is why it makes sense to use a tax to ensure that this is heavily penalised, that this is sanctioned, that this is regulated, so that capital fulfils its role again.
That is why I am calling for this proposal to be adopted today by a large majority.
The reform of the generalised system of preferences was clearly necessary.
It is now good that there are longer run times and that there is more security for exporters.
However, the criteria are too vague, as the Committee on Development has already said.
Unfortunately, the Commission refers only to the World Bank classification.
In the past, Parliament has repeatedly expressed very critical views on gross domestic product being the only criterion.
The definition of countries with higher middle incomes can refer to countries with a gross domestic product of USD 4 000 or up to USD 12 000.
This is therefore a huge and far too wide range, and it does not in any way reflect the realities on the ground.
We certainly do not find it problematic that countries such as Saudi Arabia and Kuwait are finding out.
The reform in this respect was therefore a good one.
But these countries are high-income countries anyway.
Russia and China are countries with higher middle incomes, and we certainly do not need to worry about their withdrawal from the GSP.
But this category of countries also includes much poorer countries such as Namibia and Ecuador.
Until now, the Commission has at least also taken into account the vulnerability criterion, that is, countries that export little or only a very small number of goods.
This will at least prevent countries that are less dependent on exporting products and are therefore very vulnerable from being classified as too high.
Why did the Commission then remove this criterion?
Countries such as the Dominican Republic, the Fiji Islands, Gabon and Botswana will therefore be excluded, even though their economies fall within the vulnerability criterion.
Why has the Commission not continued with the old criteria, which were already somewhat inadequate?
There is a suspicion that the Commission wants to force poorer countries into free trade agreements that are unfavourable for them.

However, the system of preferences is an instrument for promoting development and cannot be replaced by pure trade instruments, in which the development path of a country or a region plays no role at all.
However, perhaps the Members in the plenary will still have the opportunity to vote in favour of our amendment, which incorporates vulnerability as a criterion.
However, the new regulation also means that trading regions are being split up.
Neighbouring countries in customs unions will soon have to comply with different rules when exporting to the EU.
This has nothing to do with our goal of regional integration.
Instead of individual countries, the Commission should pay more attention to developing regions.
We Greens have tabled an amendment on this too, which I hope will find a great deal of support.
It is also problematic if countries that benefited from the GSP yesterday are to be dropped from the programme overnight.
There must be transitional periods, and we see in our own countries that a few months is not enough time for economic development.
Ultimately, we Members must also consider whether we want to focus on speed in all legislative proposals and then work on lopsided compromises, or whether we would rather involve the entire plenary.

I think that for legislation, the whole Parliament is important, and democracy as well. It is not the time or the speed that is important, but rather we need time to implement democracy and properly adopt laws. Thank you.

Mr President, Baroness Ashton, I was also a member of the European Parliament's election observation mission in the Democratic Republic of Congo, and I was able to see how many people stood in the polling stations with so much hope and expectation of a better future, waiting to cast their vote.
We now know that these expectations were not fulfilled.
Although the country calls itself the Democratic Republic of Congo, these elections were neither fair nor democratic.
We need to address this issue aggressively, as Mrs Nedelcheva has done in her report.
The previous President, Joseph Kabila, has declared himself the winner of the election and the opposition is being repressed.
In the east of the country, the violent clashes between government troops and so-called rebels have flared up again, and it is unclear whether third countries such as Rwanda are involved.
The civilian population is certainly suffering, and has not been able to live in peace and tranquillity for decades.
The country ’ s riches are being squandered in a swamp of corruption and the Congo remains at the bottom of all international rankings.

The international community of states, and here particularly the European Union with its possibilities, must increase the pressure on the President of the Republic of Congo to allow real democracy in his country. At the next regional elections, he will have the opportunity
and that the Congolese army does not also harass the civilian population, but finally arrests internationally wanted war criminals such as Bosco and Ntaganda and brings them before a court.
All states should finally commit themselves internationally to stop delivering any further weapons to the Congo.
Without pressure from outside, human suffering and poverty in this country will not end.
Colleagues, with the two-pack, further intervention rights against crisis countries in the eurozone are to be created.
The European Semester, which was created as a framework for the necessary and improved coordination of financial and economic policy, is to be given teeth.
According to the Commission, Member States should be able to be forced to accept financial aid.
The Six Pack has already created extensive sanctions that can be imposed on eurozone countries with financial problems.
The European Semester is already being perceived by citizens more as a dismantling of democracy than as a meaningful economic policy coordination instrument.
At the same time, the German Government is openly threatening Greek citizens with expulsion from the eurozone if they make the wrong decision in Sunday ’ s election.
Why do we not stop with these humiliating and undemocratic threats?
In a situation where the euro crisis has once again become dramatically worse – I am thinking of Spain, Cyprus – it will not help us to put Member States that are in crisis in the dock.
We should finally develop solutions. The TUPEX rapporteurs have developed some very sensible approaches to this.
Elisa Ferreira certainly drew attention to the repayment fund. That would be a worthwhile and sensible measure to combat debt.
However, I also support the proposal by Mr Gauzès on an orderly sovereign default.
But it is central that Europe makes a joint and solidarity-based effort to get the euro area back on a growth path.
The strong countries in Europe must stop pointing their fingers at their partner countries with smugness.
They must start doing their homework, investing in growth and taking responsibility.
The unilateral austerity policy à la Merkel has simply failed as a crisis solution.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, in June 2010, the EU heads of state or government finally acknowledged that the European Union needs better-coordinated financial and economic policies.
At the summit, you invented the European Semester, so to speak.
It is quite right that members of an economic and monetary union should not be allowed to look solely at how their policies affect enterprises, employees and consumers in their own country.
A European perspective is necessary.

A single market without coordination in economic, wage and social policy presents Member States with a great temptation, namely to create short-term growth and jobs at the expense of their neighbours.
Such imbalances could bring the monetary union down economically and politically.
The EU is currently facing precisely such a situation.

For years, the German economy has been draining its neighbours dry, providing no impetus through investment and wage growth. Instead, Berlin has driven the eurozone almost into ruin with a decade of aggressive export policy.

It is therefore pleasing that the Commission is now slowly getting to grips with this core problem of the euro crisis in its country-specific recommendations and is making appropriate recommendations.

The Federal Republic is quite rightly being called upon to invest more in education, research and childcare, and the Commission is quite rightly calling for the abuse of mini-jobs to be stopped and for wage growth to be adjusted to productivity growth.
This is a belated but important realisation.
I hope, Commissioner Rehn, that you will vigorously put this request to the German Government, even though there is no formal procedure for surplus countries.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, first of all, I would like to express my thanks to the Commission, and especially to Commissioner Barnier, for the very simple reason that the experience from the last legislative term, before 2009,

was that, with the exception of a very brief moment of awakening when the proposal for the European private company came, the predecessor of Commissioner Barnier, Commissioner McCreevy, has actually let company law sleep a deep sleep. And how, in these five years, this important issue, which is also important for the internal market, has not been addressed.
have been achieved.
Thankfully, that has changed fundamentally.

In the meantime, the Commission has produced a number of consultation documents, an expert group has been established to look at the further development of company law, and we now have a consultation, which is very timely, and which we in Parliament will of course also be dealing with.
The Legal Affairs Committee has decided that we do not want to do this in the form of a report, simply because a report requires additional time and is naturally somewhat more detailed.

We have chosen the means of an oral question to the Commission in order to be able to discuss this issue with the Commission, with Commissioner Barnier, and at the same time, in a subsequent resolution, to make our position as Parliament clear as to what should happen now.
I believe that it is necessary, as we did once before, about ten years ago, to develop a new action plan for European company law, and for many reasons.
There are gaps in European company law, real gaps, which basically mean that the internal market has not been established.
I would like to give the example of the lack of the fourteenth directive on the change of seat, which, in my opinion, is a typical example of such a gap.
Parliament has twice called for this in legislative initiative reports, calling for the Commission to come up with specific proposals.
There is also the expert group that the European Commission itself appointed, which has expressed the same opinion.
It is simply one of the fundamental freedoms of the internal market that companies must be able to move within the internal market with regard to their registered office.
This possibility does not exist today, or only under extremely difficult conditions.

Against this background, however, I must also criticise the fact that the European Commission's previous opinion on the fourteenth directive actually falls significantly short of Parliament's expectations and, in our view, does not meet the standard of a qualified response to a legislative initiative.
But we are hopeful that in the course of further work, if an action plan is produced, this proposal will then hopefully be included in the action plan.
Another important point in company law is the question of further developing company law with the aim of removing burdens for companies.
We already have very concrete proposals from the Commission on the table, for example on the subject of
accounting, but I think there is still more to do and further aspects which will now be clarified in the consultation.
We also expect further proposals from the Commission.
I would also like to ask the Commission to breathe new life into the subject of the European private company, which is currently stuck in the Council.

If there are individual Member States, and I can say this as my own opinion, if there are individual Member States in the EU which, due to their own lack of determination, are not in a position to resolve this deadlock on their own,

Europe forward, then one has to think about using the instrument of enhanced cooperation here, as one has done with other projects, such as patents or family law, so that at least all other Europeans have the opportunity to benefit from this possibility.
I would ask the Commission to give this matter more thought.
Another open issue is the question of applicable law, i.e. some kind of Rome Regulation for cross-border problems in company law.
I also believe that this issue requires further investigation and discussion, and I would ask the Commission to do so.
This is also closely related to the issue of the future of the CAP.
insolvency law, but the Commission has already announced that it will bring forward a specific proposal in the near future.
As you can see, there is a huge amount to do in company law.

I am grateful that Commissioner Barnier has taken the initiative again here and that it is clear that in the second half of this parliamentary term we will receive significant proposals that can represent progress in shaping the internal market.
Parliament supports this in principle and in approach.
We will of course still have to discuss the details, and we will certainly have to do that.
But that is why I would like to thank the Commission.
We would of course be pleased if the Commission were to take up our initiatives and turn them into concrete proposals.
Mr President, Commissioner, I very much appreciate your proposal on productivity-related wage policy.
But when it comes to the minimum wage, you have been weak compared to Mrs Merkel, and I do not believe you on the issue of job creation in the healthcare sector.
How on earth can new jobs be created when the Commission has already forced the southern and eastern Member States to reduce their hospital capacity by up to half?

How can jobs be created in the healthcare sector if, in the new country-specific recommendations for the European Semester, the Commission is urging some Member States to cut their healthcare spending even further?
I am afraid that this point in this plan is pure electioneering, not a real initiative for new jobs.
On the contrary, austerity measures are already causing a shortage of medicines and drugs, not only in Greece.
I think that anyone who is constantly putting in place new bank rescue packages must now work towards a rescue package for people, particularly to ensure medical care in the crisis-hit countries.
Mr President, the mood among the countless pro-Europeans like me is one of frightened horror at what is currently happening in Europe.
It is nevertheless pleasing that we are now being told much more clearly what we are dealing with, and that we are not turning a blind eye to Italy.
You know yourselves that there are a number of pitfalls, not only the debt and growth trap, but also the trust trap and the power trap, esteemed Commission, and also addressed to the Council.
Unfortunately, you have so far failed to address the central issue of the separation of the financial economy from the real economy.
Everything that we have decided here is repeatedly assessed by experts as meaning that the shadow banking system has still not been seriously curbed.
We are still dealing with systemic risks. The question of 'too big to fail' has still not been properly addressed and hardly needs to be discussed with regard to the rating agencies.

I can only urge you, as you now prepare for the Council summit, and advise you, to restore credibility in Europe by seriously addressing this power issue between the real economy and the financial economy.
to the point where they can finally work in a credible way to rein in the banks in front of the European citizens, and not always have the citizens carry the burden.

Mr Fjellheim, you come from a country that rejected the euro, presumably with the central justification that it wanted to retain the flexibility of devaluation, precisely for socio-political instrument reasons.

In the light of the experience you have now gained in Sweden, would you recommend to the Greeks to leave the eurozone as well, so that they can actively use this social policy instrument of devaluations?
Mr President, I would like to thank the shadow rapporteurs and the rapporteur.
We adopted this report in the Committee on the Environment, Public Health and Food Safety by a very large majority, with 62 votes in favour, no votes against and no abstentions.
This shows that we are actually very, very close together, including when it comes to compromises.
The highest priority in this legislative procedure must be a high level of protection, high consumer protection, especially for the small people in our society, for vulnerable children.
And as far as gluten intolerance is concerned, this is a problem, and especially in kindergartens.
If you look around you, and I have two children of that age, you will see what allergies and intolerances are rife there. I think we have got it right.
We should then discuss this together at second reading with the Council and the Commission.
I think that we will then achieve a good result.

Mr President, a report on a road accident at the weekend involving a lorry driver who fell asleep at the wheel due to exhaustion and drove into a ditch has once again confirmed to me how necessary it is
a clear and consistent regulation of driving and rest times and their monitoring using a digital tachograph is essential for us, for road safety and also for the people concerned.
The digital tachograph represents a certain innovation. It has new problems, but also still has old problems.
New problems are arising from new possibilities for manipulation, which we must also get under technical control.
The old problems are those that we can already see in connection with controls or lack of controls in various Member States.

What we are now regulating anew, in my view, has to take into account essentially the following: firstly, that not every time when no journey is recorded does that automatically mean that a rest period is in progress.
Many drivers have to load and unload, and this, of course, must be counted as working time.
The second problem is that the speed recordings have been removed from the file.
I think that is wrong, because there are major accident risks here too.
We would also like to have weight sensors and we would also like to have a hotline so that people can report fraud anonymously and so that it can be investigated.
I will also be tabling an amendment requesting that animal transport controls be included in this regulation.
I think this is important because it is also a very central element of our legislation.

Madam President, many thousands of people could still be alive today or would not have permanent disabilities if only they had received professional medical assistance quickly enough after their accident, that is, within the first so-called golden hour.
We are a whole step closer to the vision zero.
The eCall system that we are discussing today cannot prevent accidents, and it does not relieve us of our duty to provide first aid.
It is only intended to automatically inform an emergency call centre in the event of a serious accident.
This must also establish verbal contact with the vehicle involved in the accident in order to avoid abuse and overloading by minor accidents.
Only when the emergency call has been confirmed or no one answers anymore are emergency doctors dispatched to the scene of the accident, equipped with a few but vital pieces of information.
The Member States are ultimately responsible for the functioning of this rescue chain.
As long as the sensors built into the vehicle do not detect an accident, the eCall system is asleep, sends no signals or data and cannot be located.
From the point of view of data protection, it is therefore far superior to a mobile phone.
Only with the automatic wake-up controlled by sensors or the conscious manual triggering does the system become active.
It then sends a pre-agreed minimal set of information to an emergency control centre.
Europe-wide uniform standards and specifications will ensure that this data is recognised, assessed and dealt with as an emergency call when it arrives.
Therefore, the Member States must also upgrade and expand their emergency services chains accordingly.

We want this eCall emergency call system to be based on the EU-wide emergency number 112, to be free of charge for users, to work everywhere in the EU and to be compatible with the emergency call systems of neighbouring third countries.
It should be based on an interoperable, technology-neutral, open system architecture.
Other intelligent public transport services, such as real-time traffic information or the availability of parking spaces, can later be offered via these.
In recognition of the great achievements of the car industry in providing private emergency call systems, these should be allowed to continue to exist alongside the public eCall system based on 112.
Users can also decide in favour of such a system on the basis of detailed information.
This must be accompanied by the possible costs.
use of the data by workshops, insurers, breakdown services or other service providers.
A small village in Franconia is known for its good beer. This rhyme points out a small problem that we have created for ourselves today.

Because if this brewery or this drinks trader is 107 km from where I live, I have no chance that he will continue to offer me his goods in future without having to buy a new tachograph, without having to face new bureaucracy.

And therefore, dear colleagues, I voted against this motion for a resolution today, because I believe that we should not impose too much on our small and medium-sized enterprises. There were many points that we managed to include, which were positive, thank goodness.
Fifty kilometres were planned, just within the catchment area of a regional provider, to be taken on.
That was completely absurd on the part of the European Commission.
Therefore, 100 km is just about possible.
But what if it goes beyond that?
I think that is going too far.
And one last point, as a representative of a workers' organisation in the CSU, I would also say: this regulation creates complete control of workers.
Why?
For example, the person who supplies the breweries with their goods is monitored throughout the entire working day using GPS.
So, from a data protection point of view, I also consider what we have decided today to be highly questionable, and I would like to stress once again why I voted against it.
Thank you for that question.
Why am I not waiting for this decision?

I waited in the Legal Affairs Committee for the consent of the Group of the European People ’ s Party (Christian Democrats), when we were still in the process of asking the necessary questions on legal certainty in relation to ACTA.
There was silence in the forest and refusal.
That is not what they wanted. Now it is obvious that the EGH is being used to cause delays. That is clear.
Why did I mention the wide scope for interpretation?
You asked two questions, so I have to answer both of them, unfortunately.
because these broad formulations allow one to read out and interpret everything, including the monitoring of the Internet and the auxiliary police role of the ISPs.
This means legal uncertainty, and legal uncertainty does not help our economy either, ladies and gentlemen.
Mr Leichtfried, I would like to ask you: are you in favour of us discussing the modernisation of copyright at European level?
Or are you completely against the rejection of all intellectual property?
How do you envisage the e-commerce directive being adapted to future modern circumstances?
From your opinion, I understand that you reject any debate on these issues and that you are not in favour of intellectual property.
Is that true or how do you see it?
Madam President, it is now becoming clear that this is not about individual facts that we have to discuss, but about the fundamental question of how we stand on democracy and on civil rights.
If we look at ACTA, we see that it is in a way symbolic of a lack of transparency, a lack of democracy and a violation of fundamental rights, because it was negotiated in secret.

and undemocratic in nature, because the free use of the internet would simply no longer be possible afterwards, nor would the freedom to use generic medicines to the extent that we have been able to do so until now.
These are the reasons why millions of people have taken to the streets and have already voted on ACTA.
Yes, ACTA has been voted on in the streets, on the Internet, in relation to the Petitions Committee, in many countries, not only within the EU, but also
also outside the EU. And I believe we must not ignore this vote. It is a vote of direct democracy by many citizens who elected us.
All I can say to you is: put ACTA in the archives. That would be decent.

Mr President, as rapporteur for the Budget Committee and the Budgetary Control Committee, I have concluded two procedures under your presidency: the budgetary rules, so that we have a set of rules for the next MFF period, and the Olaf procedure.
This regulation has been languishing in the Council since November 2008, and you have brought this procedure to a conclusion on the basis of the results of the Polish Presidency.
I would therefore like to thank two really very committed financial officials who have worked on these files, and also Ambassador Trakanshammer.
They will be missed, and his efficiency as a negotiator will be missed.
You have really earned your spurs in terms of the financial interests of the European Union.
Thank you very much.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, I am speaking here as a representative of my group and I would like to support the motion by Mr Fjellner.
Ladies and gentlemen, we do not have many raw materials in Europe.
The raw material we have is our brains and our creativity.
This requires us to handle the issue of intellectual property rights with care.
The European Commission has referred this proposal, which it has submitted to us, to the European Court of Justice for an opinion in order to clarify outstanding issues.
I think that, out of respect for the highest court in the European Union, we should wait and see what this decision is.
There is no substantive reason at all to take this decision today.
Nobody will be prevented from voting against it when the Court of Justice has presented its decision and its opinion.
However, the decision taken then will be made in a different state of awareness and with different knowledge from that of today.
I do not think that the speed demons on the internet motorways should determine our policy, and so I would ask you to support this motion for a resolution.
Mr President, as always, when Mr Turmes speaks, it is very wise and one would like to see that what he says has much more influence on European policy.
I wanted to follow up on that.
Of course, these project bonds are a good idea, but only if they are oriented towards sustainability and if the political foundations are laid for that.
We run the risk of using guarantees to cover risks that we do not have under political control.

Specifically, the energy transition in Germany: 8 300 3 800 km of transmission network would be necessary. Only 100 km of these corresponding transmission networks have been built because it fails due to resistance from the citizens. By the way, citizens who are very environmentally oriented.
We need to come up with something in politics.

One idea would be to create joint technology corridors, so not only combining high-speed rail lines with broadband networks, but perhaps also building the power transmission networks alongside the motorways.
This, however, requires a much more comprehensive plan than the one currently envisaged, but I would at least like to suggest it at this point.

Ladies and gentlemen, I regard Schengen as one of the greatest achievements in the European Union. But I am also aware that borders are always a very sensitive issue for our Member States and always will be.
I am aware that the problems that exist between the Member States in relation to Schengen cannot simply be ignored.
There are various problems.
Ignoring them does not make the problems go away, nor does it help to solve them.
I live on the border with the Czech Republic and nobody in our area would think of wanting to close the border again just because we have problems.
However, I also ask the Commission for support in this area, not for restrictions and reprimands.
The Schengen Information System is anchored in the treaty because we want to guarantee security while at the same time guaranteeing freedom of movement.
If we successfully carry out the surveillance operations, particularly at the borders with the Czech Republic, where one or two drug dealers, not just in a few cases but in many cases,
trying to cross over, then I think it would be a good idea if the German Federal Minister and the Czech counterpart sat down together to solve the problems on the border together.
The Commission should not act in a regulatory way in this area, but should act positively and supportively.

We want to keep populists in check, but we want to solve the real problems together. So I also call on the Council to work with Parliament to find a solution so that we can successfully adopt the Schengen files, so that we have freedom of movement and at the same time guarantee the security of our citizens.
Mr President, the cases of disproportionate border checks listed in Parliament ’ s question also show why the Council wants to exclude Parliament from Schengen.
The Council wants to continue undermining freedom of movement without involving Parliament or any form of parliamentary control or any form of publicity.
No wonder, then, that you prefer to pat yourselves on the back in back rooms, away from any awkward questions.
It is no wonder that the German Government wants to exclude an evaluation of internal borders completely and therefore does not want to see how much freedom of movement is being restricted.
Schengen does not belong to the governments, but to the people of Europe and therefore also belongs in the European Parliament. Thank you.
Mr President, President of the Council, it is a special moment for me and for my group to welcome you here in your capacity as President of the Council of the European Union.
We are also pleased that, for the first time in the history of the European Union, a person whom we count as part of our political family is at the head of the EU.
You are taking over the Presidency at a time when the European Union is facing huge challenges, and it is a question of setting the course for the future of Europe.
Union will actually have a future or whether it will prove, in my view a historic defeat, to be a footnote in history.

I know from the discussions we have had with you, with the representatives of your government, with the chairpersons of the political parties and groups in your country, that you are committed to this
the Presidency has prepared excellently and that it is also aware of the scope of its task.
However – and you made this clear to us again today – you are deliberately trying to draw the attention of those in power to people ’ s living conditions.

I can tell you that, after all that I have heard in recent years, this is the first time that a President of the European Union has actually done exactly that and has announced and said that this tone

I have long missed the fact that in official speeches, people have said: this is about people, it is about those who are not responsible for our being in this crisis, but who have to bear the greatest burden.
It will be difficult to implement this approach.
However, I think that a great deal would be gained, and I am sure that you will do this, if you encourage people, above all, with your Presidency and the way in which you conduct it,
exercise, that they stand up for their rights and that they contribute to the Council and the European institutions being fully aware of this task.
We keep talking about the euro and its stability, about the need, which I also see, for it to be important for the functioning of the European Union and the euro area.
But this is an instrument.
There were political conditions for this, and they have been defined.

The European Union can never have the goal of accepting that people in the European Union have to go hungry, that health systems collapse and that local authorities have to stop their work in order for the euro to function.
Something has gone wrong here.
This is not what we can call the objective of the European Union.
I strongly support your call that we must focus on social cohesion and solidarity between the individual Member States, but also between the various population groups.
The European Union must not allow itself to be used again and again as an instrument or spokesperson for just one small section of the population without also representing itself.

One last point that I would like to raise, to underline that all the things that you have mentioned as your priorities are important to us and that we will support those points that go in the direction that I have just defined.
Please do something so that people no longer have to be afraid.
Fear is the worst advisor for democracies.
I think that some of the decisions made in recent weeks and months, especially the elections in Greece and the referenda in Ireland, were also characterised by fear.
If the leaders of the European Union contribute to people being afraid to make their own free decisions, then that is not a good sign for the future of the European Union.
Good luck with your work.

eyes are currently on us and on the Council to take this step, to overcome the crisis of the banks in Europe by taking another step towards European integration, namely through a European banking union.

Madam President, with the proposal to exclude developing countries from preferential market access, the Commission is trying to force these countries into free trade agreements.
These are not free trade agreements, as the term "economic partnerships" suggests, but are concerned only with the interests of the EU and not with the development interests of the partner countries.
This runs completely counter to the EU ’ s objective of reducing poverty and aligning all external policy instruments accordingly.
Countries such as Cameroon, Côte d ’ Ivoire and Namibia would be seriously affected by this restriction.
Regional integration projects, such as those in south-east Africa, would collapse if Kenya were to enter into such an agreement.
South-South cooperation would be at an end.
We cannot advocate free trade in the world and mean only that other countries should please buy our goods.
If the Commission really believes that trade can promote development, then it should not block it.
The Development Committee of this House has recommended rejecting the Commission proposal.
We should support that in the plenary.

is not fit to strengthen a European community, a community based on solidarity.

Madam President, the new directive creates a basis for dealing with books, newspapers, works of art, films, musical compositions and TV productions which are protected by copyright but for which the rights holder cannot be identified or located.
Accordingly, books, films, photographs or other intellectual creations for which no rights holder can be found should also be made available to the public.
And so a cultural treasure is unearthed.

In the Committee on Culture and Education, we felt it was particularly important in this legislative initiative to highlight the special importance of the diversity of the cultural heritage of the European Union.

In the archives of our museums, libraries, publishing houses and broadcasting organisations there are countless unused works, and until now they have not been made available simply because the rights holder is unknown.
That will change with the directive.
It will make it possible to use this cultural asset in the future, provided that a careful search is carried out before publication.
This is a very important point, so that we do not simply say that we cannot find the rights holders.
A balance had to be found between the real rights of creators of works and the public's need to be able to use such works where the rights situation cannot be clarified.
We also had to ensure that both the rights holders who are known and those who are not known continue to be fully protected.
Where the rights holders cannot be found, the draft will make it possible to use digitised orphan works.
Projects such as Europeana and other major European digitalisation projects, but also our museums, archives and educational establishments and public broadcasting, will be affected.
In future, however, we must also ensure that orphan works in the archives of private publishers are made available to the public.
This draft directive and the compromise that has now been found are, in my eyes, truly a compromise and a first step in the right direction.
I believe that the cultural heritage of the Sahel region has enormous potential.
We should continue to work on this when we revise the directive in two years' time.

signals from the Council on how we can boost Europe ’ s growth and competitiveness potential and, above all, how we can give young people jobs again.

or the Erasmus for Young Entrepreneurs programme or the media pluralism programme, there are only cuts here.

The Nordsee Werken in Emden had to file for insolvency last Wednesday because a guarantee from the Lower Saxony state government was surprisingly not extended.

And so I ask the Commission to examine this procedure with goodwill, but also in good time, so that these 700 jobs are not put at risk, because these 700 jobs are needed to organise the energy transition and to organise future-proof energy on the seas of Europe.
Please do not let your colleagues there suffer as a result of the state government ’ s poor crisis management.
Dual-use goods often look harmless, but in the hands of authoritarian regimes they can be as dangerous as machine guns or tanks.
We undermine our foreign policy objectives when we send surveillance technologies to authoritarian regimes that can use them to control and repress democratic movements.
It was European mobile phone software from Nokia Siemens Networks that handed the Iranian security forces the tools to suppress the Green protest movement.
Protesters were abducted, arrested, tortured and sentenced to death in large numbers.
That is just one example among many.
In addition, the Commission, with its catalogue of measures for an innovative and competitive security industry, as it is called, promotes these dubious dual-use goods.
The aim is to make better use of the synergies between security and defence technologies.
We run the risk of literally selling out human rights and also our own security interests for the sake of a dubious industrial policy.
