Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I think that today's debate on MiFID is entering the core of financial market regulation.
in this parliamentary term, the issue is to regulate the financial markets and not just to look at individual products, as we have done in the past.
I will just mention a few key points.
We did not make things very easy for each other in our work last year.

We held a hearing in committee, we will carry out a survey of all stakeholders, all interested groups, and on the basis of that, we will work out a result using various processes, working documents and the like, which we will present to the plenary for a vote today and tomorrow.
I would like to pick out a few points.
Number one: market structure.

On the one hand, we must of course acknowledge that a lot has been done in the financial markets in recent years. But our interest as Parliament is that every trade in financial instruments takes place within the MiFID rules. That is why we have, under many conditions, a new category

the so-called organised trading facilities, where only non-equities are to be traded, where proprietary trading is to be prohibited, and where, according to the rules on execution and transactions and interaction with customers, execution at the firm's own discretion is possible.
I think that we are making an important contribution to ensuring that the whole area of over-the-counter instruments that are traded within the MiFID world is traded.
Another major issue that was not covered in the last MiFID was algorithmic trading or high-frequency trading.
Here, too, we wanted to lay down clear rules of the game so that this area, which can be very dangerous and can also be manipulated, is subject to strict rules, with minimum holding periods,

trading interruption possibilities, with the possibility of testing algorithms, with the possibility of supervisory structures looking at algorithms, with the introduction of a so-called tick size regime, a minimum price size, and through appropriate fee systems.
Another subject I would like to address is the area of commodity futures markets.

I believe that we have a special responsibility here when it comes to restricting the speculative element in commodity futures markets so that we do not contribute through false regulations to the situation where people in the third and fourth worlds can no longer afford to buy food.
We have therefore adopted a two-pronged approach with the aim of curbing speculation without harming the real economy.
We want to achieve this with position limits, with strict upper limits on the number of contracts or positions.
But we also want to ensure that those who can prove that they really need a product will not suddenly start speculating in this market, by means of a so-called position check system.
Another subject that concerns us greatly is investor protection.
I would just like to point out that we have adopted a very broad approach when it comes to guaranteeing consumer and investor protection.
The point is to identify the needs of certain customer groups.
Not every small investor needs access to all highly complex documents.

It is about defining appropriate products for target groups, carrying out product checks, telephone recording, appropriate advice, disclosure obligations, but, and I want to briefly mention this, in the area of commissions, to make subsidiarity.
The Member States should have the opportunity to make appropriate adjustments in accordance with their structures.
I think we are on the right track.
After my time is up, I would like to thank everyone who has contributed here.
I think it is a pity that we only get four minutes to speak on such a difficult dossier.
I would like to thank all the colleagues who have been involved here, the Commission, for their very constructive role in recent weeks and months.
I hope that with a strong vote in the plenary tomorrow, we will be able to enter into negotiations with the Council in order to be able to conclude this important file as soon as possible.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, thank you very much for the generally good cooperation on this report.
We have been able to further improve a Commission proposal that was already a big step in the right direction.
There will be greater transparency in market structures, and there will be more comprehensive transparency, particularly in the area of derivatives trading.
under the MiFID framework, the decisions on high-frequency trading in our compromise go much further than what the Commission originally proposed and deserve the support of Parliament as a whole.
The report also points in the right direction on the issues that have provoked such strong public criticism.
In the area of commodity and food speculation, after long and difficult debates, we now have much stronger rules than the Commission's proposals, because position limits are now being made binding.
However, we still need further changes in this area in order to close gaps.
I hope that this will be supplemented by the plenary vote and, finally, by the trialogue.
It is pleasing that Mr Barnier has already signalled his support for Parliament ’ s more far-reaching proposal.
However, the report is weak in the area of consumer protection.
We now have the possibility of banning products, which is progress, but we still have no change in the area of commission-driven advice.
The sale of financial products is still not primarily focused on the interests of customers, but is strongly dominated by the question of which product offers the highest commissions.
Consumers have lost a lot of money in Germany and throughout the EU.
Think of the open-ended real estate funds.
For citizens in France, Italy, Spain and Germany, for example, not much will change even after our compromise.
The basic principle will continue to be commission orientation.
It is a bit simplistic to refer to subsidiarity here.
I would have liked to see stronger rules here.
Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I can only say that good things come to those who wait.
The report on the type-approval of vehicles in the L category has ultimately turned out to be a good report.
The work took longer than any of us had thought, and especially longer than we had hoped. And there were many more pitfalls than we would have liked.
But at this point, I would like to thank the rapporteur, Mr Van de Camp, once again very warmly for the really cooperative and good collaboration.
Nevertheless, together we have achieved a great deal in the negotiations with the Member States.
As rapporteur for my group, I recommend that you adopt this report. That said, it could have been much better.
And by 'better', ladies and gentlemen, I mean 'safer'.
For us social democrats, vehicle safety and thus the safety of drivers have been at the forefront from the very beginning. In particular, the safety of scooter and moped riders.
The number of motorcyclists involved in accidents has remained constant for years.
In general, the number of people killed in road accidents in Europe is falling, but not in the case of motorcycle accidents.
Reducing the number of motorcyclists and moped riders killed is indeed a matter close to my heart.
Fewer deaths on Europe's roads, that was and is my goal.
Every death is one too many.
This issue must be dealt with intensively, particularly in a regulation that is so beneficial for the internal market.
I am still convinced that this can be done using effective anti-lock braking systems, or ABS.
And I am just as convinced of the cost-effectiveness.
It is not true that ABS is too expensive.

However, we have achieved the fact that from 2016, all new, and from 2017, all existing motorcycle types with an engine capacity of over 125 cc must be fitted with ABS as standard by the manufacturer.
On mopeds, manufacturers can optionally equip ABS or the combined braking systems.
Unfortunately, we have not achieved any improvement on the Commission proposal in this respect.
I would have liked much more here, but the Commission and the Member States have, to put it mildly, been very slow to respond.
However, we did achieve the result that the Commission would present a report in 2019.
The report must be based on road accident data from the Member States and should examine the mandatory fitting of ABS to motor scooters.
It will also be sent to Parliament and to the Council.
On the basis of this report, the Commission will then consider making the installation of ABS mandatory on scooters.
As you can see, this issue remains very much alive.
Therefore, we will try to close the binding gap in the legislation from 2019.
I am sure that the data collected will prove me right.
I am convinced that this is indeed a good day for the drivers of two-wheeled vehicles in Europe and that the number of accidents will decrease in the future.
Mr President, Mr Groote, there is no technology without risks. There is no perfect energy generation technology.
And I believe that applies to what we are discussing here today as well.
I am very much in favour of us discussing this rationally.
What we are currently experiencing is a new access to energy markets through unconventional resources, and this applies both to gas, mainly to gas, but also to oil.

I don't think it's appropriate to fall into great euphoria about the great gas future. It's just as inappropriate, as some of my colleagues from the Greens and the Left have done here, to paint great horror and nightmare scenarios on the wall that have nothing to do with reality.

I think we need to bear in mind that these unconventional resources have what it takes to change all the energy supply relationships that we know between suppliers and demanders, and that the economic calculations for previous energy sources are also being called into question.
We should face the development.

I think we should take note that this can also be an opportunity for Europe and not just a risk. We should prepare ourselves to positively embrace and utilize new technologies, to fairly weigh opportunities and risks, and not to fall into ideological debates. Thank you.
Mr President, Commissioner, thank you for your answer. It does not entirely satisfy me.
I think that is a very, very long time: one year between being notified that the complaint has been received and possibly getting a reply so late.
I believe that a complainant who takes the trouble - and usually he has already gone through the entire legal process in his Member State - deserves more.
We rely on our citizens to become involved in the European Union.
If they do so in this form, we should treat them accordingly.

I believe that in the long term, it is necessary and indispensable that we have a procedure here that is clearly formalised and clearly regulates rights. This is now a declaration on how the Commission intends to handle this.
On another point, I have no objection to trying to reach some kind of unanimous statement with the Member States if it helps the cause.
However, this will not always be sufficient.
We see that, for example, in economic matters, things move relatively quickly when one company sues another for infringing EU law.
But when it comes to environmental issues and similar issues, where many, many citizens are calling for the European Union to act, or also social rights, then it proceeds a bit more laboriously.
I believe that these facts must also be clearly taken into account.

And I would like for the next report that we have a clear formalisation of the rights of the complainant vis-à-vis the Commission, which are also really clear and which are also really well communicated.
Once again, if we really want European law to be applied, after we have spent so much time on procedures here in the European Parliament, in the Council,
jointly decided. Then we must inform those who are responsible for this, who are really engaging in a European way, as well and as precisely as possible.
This is a very, very important issue for me.
I will also be watching closely next time to see what happens here.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, five years ago, the EU launched its Central Asia strategy.
If you ask today for an assessment of this strategy, the verdict is mixed.

We see how the Member States individually strive to establish good economic relations, particularly with the resource-rich countries in this region, while efforts to achieve progress in other areas are lagging.

accompany these states in their transformation process towards modern democratic societies, or, as the most recent events in Kazakhstan show, whether the development is even characterised by setbacks.

Nevertheless, or perhaps precisely because of this, the European Union and its Member States must express their influence in this region more strongly together, including and especially towards Kazakhstan, which has now become a major supplier for several EU countries.

Isolating Kazakhstan would only lead to the competition for influence in this geopolitically important country, where the pressure to respect civil and human rights is not so important, being even more strongly asserted by those who are not so concerned about it.
Linking economic interests with the promotion of human rights is the core of the Central Asia strategy.

The report before us provides a good guideline for this. Thank you very much.

Ladies and gentlemen, to the Council members who are not here, perhaps you are following us via the Parliament's video stream, as you obviously have neither the capacity nor the desire to talk to us live.
I regard the Council ’ s absence here today as a gross affront to Parliament.
Instead of avoiding this debate, you should rather reveal your secret as to why you are refusing to improve the protection of personal data in the area of police and justice through a directive.

For years, the police and the judiciary have been, as we know, a paradise for infringements in the area of data protection, not only in the case of SWIFT, but for example on a daily basis, in that data on citizens who, for example, demonstrate against Nazis are collected and stored illegally.
We need the same protection standards for the public and private sectors and for the police and the judiciary. That is what this is all about: guaranteeing civil rights.
If you, the Council, are asking Parliament to play a constructive role in data protection-related regulations such as EU PNR or Europol, then the same should apply to the Council in reverse.
Therefore, I call on you to put an end to the blocking policy in the area of data protection.
